title: United Nations Verification Mission in Guatemala :#programme budget implications of draft resolution A/54/L.27 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/54/L.27 (MINUGUA), requirements of $27,694,300 will be charged against the provision of $90,387,200 for special political missions requested in Section 3 of the proposed programme budget for the biennium 2000-2001. No posts specified. ACABQ comments were presented orally; no separate quantified recommendation stated in this document.

title: United Nations Verification Mission in Guatemala :#programme budget implications of draft resolution A/53/L.20 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: ACABQ reviewed SG’s statement A/C.5/53/32 and performance report A/C.5/53/25. SG estimated 1999 costs at $29,785,100 gross ($27,648,800 net). ACABQ notes a 1998 unencumbered balance of $5,008,000 net ($4,678,600 gross), and recommends an additional 1999 appropriation of $22,640,800 under Section 3 after offsetting the unencumbered balance. Staff assessment of $2,465,700 is to be fully offset by income and not new money. Staffing structure remains unchanged; only a minor adjustment converting three international General Service posts to local level; no new posts created. ACABQ does not recommend any reduction in SG estimates and emphasizes efficient management and monitoring.
stages.budget_section: Section 3, Peacekeeping operations and special missions

title: United Nations Verification Mission in Guatemala :#programme budget implications of draft resolution A/54/L.33/Rev.1 : report of the 5th Committee : General Assembly, 55th session

Of this, $7,243,200 would be charged against the existing provision for special political missions (absorbed), and an additional appropriation of $8,992,200 would be required under section 3, Political affairs, of the 2000-2001 programme budget. This is a Fifth Committee recommendation to the GA; final GA approval is not recorded in this document.

title: Programme budget for the biennium 1994-1995. The situation of democracy and human rights in Haiti. Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/48/L.57 : 15th report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions.

stages.notes: SG estimated additional requirements net of staff assessment of $18,707,700 to extend MICIVIH to 31 March 1995, comprised of $16,596,300 for personnel and $2,111,400 for operational costs. Due to low operations from 1 Jan–31 Mar 1994, $2,558,800 from the previously approved $4.0 million (for that quarter) would offset part of the new requirements. ACABQ notes security uncertainty and recommends informing GA that up to $15,692,000 additional expenditure may be required under section 4, and authorizing the SG to enter commitments up to $15.7 million through 31 March 1995. Activities are of an extraordinary nature and should be handled outside contingency fund procedures (per GA res. 41/213). Background: GA had approved $13,183,500 for MICIVIH in 1992-1993 biennium and $4.0 million for 1 Jan–31 Mar 1994 under section 4. Staffing composition referenced (not new posts): 1 USG Special Envoy, 1 D-2 Director of Human Rights, 186 other international staff (incl. 135 observers), 254 local; 116 staff already on board; deployment schedule in annex III.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/48/L.57, additional requirements not exceeding $15,692,000 would arise under section 4. The Secretary-General would be authorized to enter into commitments not to exceed $15,692,000 for the period ending 31 March 1995. Any additional appropriation as necessary would be considered during the forty-ninth session of the General Assembly.
stages.budget_section: Section 4, Peace-keeping operations and special missions

title: United Nations Verification Mission in Guatemala : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/56/L.42 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: ACABQ considered SG statement A/C.5/56/26 on draft resolution A/56/L.42 (MINUGUA). If the GA adopts the draft, additional requirements of $14,755,100 for 2002 would be charged against the existing provision of $93,691,600 (before recosting) for special political missions in Section 3 of the proposed 2002-2003 programme budget. Proposed staffing reflects vacancy factors of 5% (international) and 3% (local). Estimated operational costs for 2002 are $2,432,200, including $246,800 in non-recurrent costs. Prior biennium (2000-2001) appropriation was $43,233,400 with estimated expenditures of $39,871,600, leaving an unencumbered balance of $3,361,800. Extrabudgetary projects estimated at $2,342,100 in 2002 are anticipated. Mission mandate extension period covered: 1 January–31 December 2002.
stages.posts_levels: 48 International Professional, 17 International General Service, 4 Field Service, 139 Local-level staff, 63 United Nations Volunteers

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/56/L.42, $14,755,100 would be charged against the provision for special political missions under section 3 for the 2002-2003 biennium. Amount is to be charged against an existing provision (no new appropriation specified).

title: United Nations Verification Mission in Guatemala : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/57/L.27 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General’s statement under rule 153 for draft resolution A/57/L.27 (MINUGUA extension 1 Jan–31 Dec 2003). Additional requirements: $11,631,400 net ($12,990,400 gross). Section 3 appropriation for special political missions in 2002-2003 was fully allocated; therefore additional appropriations to be treated under paragraph 11 of annex I to resolution 41/213. Staff assessment ($1,359,000) is fully offset by income and not counted as new money. Estimated 2003 costs shown also as $11,602,900 net ($12,961,900 gross); after recognizing a 2002 deficit of $28,500, the requested additional amount for 2003 is $11,631,400 net. No non-recurrent requirements or liquidation costs. Net reduction of 38 positions proposed: abolition of 7 Professional, 9 Field Service/Other international General Service, 16 local staff, and 7 UN Volunteers; creation requested of 1 National Officer plus 2 local level posts for public information and 1 General Service (Other level) + 1 local level staff to support the new Mission History and Lessons Learned Unit (P-5 head redeployed, not new). Extrabudgetary resources anticipated in 2003: $682,700 (donors: Sweden, Canada, Germany, Norway, Belgium) for legislative reform, round-table meetings, and public information; not part of regular budget appropriations.
stages.posts_levels: National Officer, General Service (Other level), Local level

stages.notes: Mission: MINUGUA. ACABQ reviewed SG statement A/C.5/57/30 on draft resolution A/57/L.27/Rev.1 (extension of MINUGUA to 31 Dec 2003). SG estimated requirements for 1 Jan–31 Dec 2003: $11,602,900 net ($12,961,900 gross), a reduction of $3,152,200 net (~21%) vs 2002 appropriations. ACABQ recommends informing GA that, upon adoption of A/57/L.27/Rev.1, additional appropriations required: $11,631,400 under section 3 (Political affairs) and $1,359,000 under section 32 (Staff assessment), the latter to be fully offset under income section 1 (Income from staff assessment). 2002 expenditure estimated at $14,783,600 vs $14,755,100 appropriated, resulting in an estimated deficit of $28,500. Civilian personnel costs for 2003: $9,606,300 (vs $11,868,600 in 2002) with vacancy factors of 17.5% (international) and 3% (local). Operational costs for 2003: $1,920,300 (vs $2,432,200 in 2002). Other programmes for 2003: $257,000 (vs $314,800 in 2002). Vehicle fleet to be reduced to 98 (from 128). Staffing reduction vs 2002: 38 posts (−16 international: −7 Professional, −7 GS, −2 Field Service; −16 local-level; −7 UNV), offset by +1 National Officer. Military observers discontinued; police observers reduced from 10 to 6 (one per regional office). Extrabudgetary Trust Fund for the Guatemala Peace Process: $1.2 million allocated to various activities; projected $682,700 available for 2003 based on donor contributions. Mission planning assumes termination end-2003, with liquidation planning to begin; Government requested possible extension into 2004.
stages.posts_levels: 41 Professional (international), 10 General Service (international), 2 Field Service, 6 National Officers, 118 other local-level staff, 56 United Nations Volunteers

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, should it adopt draft resolution A/57/L.27/Rev.1, additional appropriations would be required for the programme budget for the biennium 2002-2003: $11,631,400 under Section 3, Political affairs. Staff assessment of $1,359,000 under Section 32 is offset by an equivalent amount under income section 1 (not new money). This document does not record the final GA approval.

title: Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against Women : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/62/L.20/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: SG statement (rule 153) for draft resolution A/C.3/62/L.20/Rev.1 estimates total additional requirements of $10,807,950 for the biennium 2008-2009, charged to the contingency fund. First-year (2008) additional requirements total $8,934,250; second-year (2009) $1,873,700. Breakdown: Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) $10,357,400 total ($8,501,300 in 2008; $1,856,100 in 2009). Section 23 (Human rights) $415,450 in 2008 (travel, per diem and terminal expenses for Committee members; plus general temporary assistance equivalent to six work-months at P-3 valued at $75,000). Section 28D (Office of Central Support Services) $35,100 total ($17,500 in 2008; $17,600 in 2009) for contractual services (sound technicians). No posts are established; GTA only. Activities include one additional three-week session in 2008 (with up to seven days in parallel chambers), parallel chambers in one regular session in 2008 and one in 2009, and an additional one-week pre-session working group in 2008. Modifications to outputs in Section 23 are required: revise meeting counts and lists of issues. Two of the five sessions in 2008-2009 to be held in New York, noted as an exception to GA resolution 40/243 on meetings at established headquarters. Operative paragraph 14 (activities from 2010 onward) to be addressed in the 2010-2011 proposed programme budget and not costed here. Present statement supersedes E/2007/L.42; prior estimate ($14,156,400) is replaced by the current $10,807,950 (difference $3,348,450).
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: ACABQ recommends an additional appropriation of $10,772,850 for the 2008-2009 biennium, to be a charge against the contingency fund, allocated as $10,357,400 under section 2 and $415,450 under section 23. Following issuance of the SG’s statement (A/C.5/62/15), $35,100 was identified for absorption under section 28D (Office of Central Support Services), reducing the contingency fund charge from the SG’s $10,807,950 estimate to $10,772,850. Earlier higher estimates ($14,156,400 total; $14,121,300 charge) in A/62/515, sect. II.H were superseded by A/C.5/62/15.
stages.budget_section: Sections 2 (General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management) and 23 (Human rights)

These additional appropriations would be considered by the Assembly under contingency fund procedures. This is a Fifth Committee decision; final GA approval is not captured in this document.

title: International Civilian Support Mission in Haiti :#programme budget implications of draft resolution A/54/L.36 : report of the 5th Committee : General Assembly, 54th session

Breakdown: $9,173,100 for the International Civilian Support Mission in Haiti (ICSMH) and $1,180,100 for the International Civilian Mission in Haiti (ICMH). title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/49/L.64 : report of the 5th Committee : General Assembly, 49th session.

stages.notes: SG estimates total additional requirements of $12,054,300 (net of staff assessment) for MINUGUA for 1 Apr–30 Sep 1995; $1,915,500 from previously approved resources (Oct 1994–Mar 1995) can offset part of this, leaving an additional appropriation required of $10,138,800 under section 4. An additional $769,000 for staff assessment (section 28) is fully offset by income (section 1). Costs reflect continuation of previously authorized staffing levels: 113 international staff (1 D-2, 2 D-1, 8 P-5, 13 P-4, 22 P-3, 5 P-2, 35 GS, 27 FS), plus 10 military liaison officers, 60 civilian police, 72 UN volunteers, and 135 local staff; includes helicopter rental to reach remote areas and $100,000 for human rights education/public information. SG notes MINUGUA is extraordinary and outside contingency fund procedures. If the mandate is extended beyond 30 Sep 1995, SG requests authority to enter into commitments through Dec 1995 up to $5,600,000 (approx. half of recurrent monthly cost).

Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, upon adoption of A/49/L.64, an additional appropriation of $9,423,100 under section 4 would be required for MINUGUA activities from 1 April to 30 September 1995. MINUGUA activities are of an extraordinary nature and should continue to be dealt with outside contingency fund procedures per para. 11 of annex I to GA resolution 41/213. A staff assessment appropriation of $769,000 under section 28 would be offset by the same amount under income section 1. If the GA at its fiftieth session extends MINUGUA beyond 30 September 1995, the SG would be authorized to enter into commitments up to $4,711,500 under section 4 for activities from 1 October to 31 December 1995.
stages.budget_section: Section 4, Peace-keeping operations and special missions

title: Programme budget for the biennium 1994-1995 :#the situation in Central America : procedures for the establishment of a firm and lasting peace and progress in fashioning a region of peace, freedom, democracy and development : establishment of a human rights verification mission in Guatemala : programme budget implication of draft resolution A/48/L.63./Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly : 18th report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions.

stages.notes: SG estimate for MINUGUA (initial 6 months) under section 4 totals $16,291,800 net: $8,393,200 personnel (salaries, fees, travel, allowances) and $7,898,600 operational (premises, vehicles, furniture, communications). Includes $2,560,600 for transfer of 132 vehicles/surplus equipment from other missions. ACABQ recommends authorizing commitments not exceeding $12.9 million 'at this stage'; the actual appropriation to be set on the basis of the 1994-1995 performance report later in 1994. ACABQ urges refining estimates after GA adoption, exploring host Government support (premises, landing fees) via a status-of-mission agreement, and avoiding double charging for redeployed assets per GA decision 48/496. Proposed staffing table: 56 Professional (1 D-2; 2 D-1; 14 P-5; 12 P-4; 22 P-3; 5 P-2), 39 GS, 30 Field Service; plus 72 UN Volunteers, 10 military observers, 60 civilian police. ACABQ considers 5 P-5 plus a D-1 for admin/personnel/finance excessive pending an operational plan; suggests expanding UNV component by 10–15 to reduce international civilian staff; does not recommend establishing resident field audit (to be provided from HQ) nor a resident management review officer at this stage. Equipment reductions recommended: vehicles from 132 to 110; computers from 203 to 150; printers from 152 to 70. SG indicated intent, after a final peace agreement, to seek Security Council authorization for a peacekeeping operation with a special account.
stages.posts_levels: D-2, D-1, P-5, P-4, P-3, P-2, General Service, Field Service

stages.notes: Secretary-General requests an additional appropriation of $16,291,800 net of staff assessment for MINUGUA for the six-month period Oct 1994–Mar 1995 under section 4. Staff assessment of $956,600 under section 28 is fully offset by Income section 1 and should not be counted as new money. The request cannot be absorbed within existing resources (section 4). The SG considers these extraordinary expenses to be outside the contingency fund procedures (per GA res. 41/213, annex I, para. 11). Mission staffing also includes 10 military observers, 60 civilian police, 72 UN Volunteers, and 135 local staff. Estimates include transfer from other missions of 132 vehicles and surplus equipment, with corresponding payment estimated at $2,560,600, already reflected in line items.
stages.posts_levels: D-2, D-1, P-5, P-4, P-3, P-2, General Service, Field Service, Local

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, upon adoption of draft resolution A/48/L.63/Rev.2, the SG would be authorized to enter into commitments in an initial amount of $7.5 million under section 4 for the biennium 1994-1995, for a six-month mission in Guatemala to verify human rights and compliance with the comprehensive agreement. Final level of authorization to be determined after review of revised budget proposals to be submitted no later than 30 November 1994. The SG is requested to seek cost reductions (including staffing and related costs) without hindering the mandate, ensure full internal audit and management review, and enhance UN system coordination. Further revisions to the draft resolution (Rev.2) did not entail additional PBIs.

title: Emergency international assistance for peace, normalcy and reconstruction of war-stricken Afghanistan and the situation in Afghanistan and its implications for international peace and security : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/56/L.62 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Request covers extension of UNSMA for 1 January–31 December 2002. Total requirements of $7,355,400 include non-recurrent costs of $855,100 (notably 4 armoured vehicles, communications equipment, and minor infrastructure repairs). To be charged against the $93,691,600 provision for special political missions under section 3 of the 2002-2003 proposed programme budget, subject to GA adoption of A/56/L.62. Staffing changes proposed: 1 new D-1 (head of Political Section), upgrade of Civil Affairs Coordinator from P-5 to D-1 (upgrade, not a new post), 2 new P-4 Civil Affairs Officers, and 2 new Field Service posts (Security Coordinator and Air Safety Officer). International staff increase from 32 to 37; local staff remain 49. Military advisers reduced from 4 to 3. Prior biennium (2000-2001) appropriation for UNSMA was $9,375,200; estimated expenditures $8,021,400 (for reference).

Amount to be charged against the $93.7 million provision for special political missions in the proposed programme budget for 2002-2003. Secretary-General also proposed upgrading one P-5 to D-1 (Civil Affairs Coordinator) and reducing military observers from 4 to 3. Costing assumes a 12% vacancy rate for international and local staff; performance report for 2000-2001 reflected $1.2 million in savings due to a 16% vacancy rate. Mission: United Nations Special Mission to Afghanistan (UNSMA).
stages.posts_levels: 1 D-1 (Head of Political Section), 2 P-4 (Civil Affairs Officers), 2 Field Service (Security Coordinator, Air Safety Officer)

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, should it adopt draft resolution A/56/L.62, $7,355,400 would be charged against the provision of $93.7 million (before recosting) for special political missions under Section 3 in the proposed programme budget for the biennium 2002-2003. Amount is charged against an existing provision; conditional on GA adoption of the draft resolution.

title: Global efforts for the total elimination of racism, racial discrimination, xenophobia and related intolerance and the comprehensive implementation of and follow-up to the Durban Declaration and Programme of Action : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/63/L.51/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General’s PBI for Durban Review Conference and preparatory process under the 2008-2009 biennium. Overall estimated additional requirements total $3,754,800, of which $3,184,400 to be absorbed within existing appropriations (Section 2: $2,409,000; Section 23: $740,600; Section 28E: $34,800). New appropriation sought: $570,400 under Section 2 for: third substantive session of the Preparatory Committee ($382,900) and the intersessional open-ended intergovernmental working group ($187,500). Conference-servicing costs for the Review Conference itself ($675,200) to be covered from programmed resources in Section 2. Under Section 23, requirements totaling $444,700 (experts’ participation $119,500; Chair travel $4,800; staff travel $101,200; consultants $81,000; information campaign $78,000; printing $40,000) will be accommodated within existing appropriations; no new appropriation requested. Preliminary estimate of $6,794,700 reduced due to fewer regional meetings (only two held) and reduced documentation. Initial estimate for GTA (2 P-4, 2 GS(OL), 18 months, $837,000) no longer sought; covered via existing/extra-budgetary resources. Extrabudgetary needs estimated at about $1,071,100 for NGO/NHRI participation; funding not yet received at expected levels. Actual expenditures as of Oct 2008: $2,052,200. The $570,400 would be a charge against the contingency fund unless offset elsewhere in Section 2. No posts are established by this proposal; GTA only, financed within existing/extra-budgetary resources.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: Biennium 2008-2009. The Secretary-General estimates total additional requirements of $3,754,800 for the Durban Review Conference and its preparatory process, of which $3,184,400 are expected to be absorbed under sections 2, 23 and 28E. Net appropriation sought: $570,400 under section 2 for the third substantive session of the Preparatory Committee ($382,900) and the intersessional open-ended intergovernmental working group ($187,500). Conference-servicing costs for the Durban Review Conference ($675,200) are to be met within programmed resources. Under section 23 (Human rights), estimated needs of $444,700 (experts participation $119,500; Chair travel $4,800; staff travel $101,200; consultants $81,000; information campaign $78,000; printing $40,000) are to be accommodated within the approved appropriation (no new appropriation). Under section 28E (Administration, Geneva), support services of $34,800 are also to be absorbed. Actual expenditure as of 20 October 2008 for the preparatory process: $2,052,200. Preliminary estimates reported earlier totaled $6,794,700 but were reduced due to fewer regional meetings held and lower documentation requirements. No new posts are requested; earlier preliminary GTA assumptions (2 P-4 and 2 GS (Other level) for 18 months each) are covered through existing/extrabudgetary resources.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: ACABQ reviewed the Secretary-General’s statement A/C.5/63/17 for draft resolution A/C.3/63/L.51/Rev.1 on the Durban Review Conference process. Preliminary additional requirements had been indicated at $6,794,700 for the 2008-2009 biennium (sections 2 and 23); revised overall estimated additional requirements are $3,754,800 across sections 2 (GA/ECOSOC affairs and conference management), 23 (Human rights), and 28E (Administration, Geneva). As of 20 October 2008, actual expenditure for the preparatory process was $2,052,200, with additional requirements from the Preparatory Committee’s second session estimated at $1,702,600. Of the $3,754,800 total, $3,184,400 is to be met through absorption under the various sections (soft rejection). Net additional resources sought — and recommended by ACABQ — amount to $570,400 under section 2, to fund the third substantive session of the Preparatory Committee ($382,900) and the intersessional open-ended intergovernmental working group ($187,500). The need for additional appropriation for 2008-2009 is to be determined in the consolidated PBI/revised estimates and contingency fund utilization.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/53/L.57 : statement / by the Secretary-General

stages.notes: Total 1999 requirements for MICIVIH under section 3 are $7,667,400 net of staff assessment ($8,468,800 gross), including $776,700 in non-recurrent costs. Staff assessment requirements total $849,800 (comprised of $801,400 for 1999 plus $48,400 for 1998 overexpenditures) and are fully offset by income section 1. Activities are extraordinary and handled outside the contingency fund per GA resolution 41/213, annex I, para. 11. Certain costs (e.g., rental, public information) are shared 50/50 with OAS; no extrabudgetary contributions are anticipated for 1999. Mission staffing remains unchanged; consultants (14 international, 4 local) supplement activities, but no new posts are created.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/53/L.66 : report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

stages.notes: Scope: Extension of UNSMA from 1 Jan to 31 Dec 1999 and creation of a civil affairs monitoring unit deployed in major Afghan cities; additional offices/outposts including Tehran. SG net estimated requirements for 1999 were $6,883,600 (gross $7,399,600), proposed to be offset by the 1998 unencumbered balance of $332,300, yielding an additional appropriation request under section 3 of $6,551,300. ACABQ recommends a 10% reduction, resulting in $5,896,200 under section 3, and $412,100 under section 32 (staff assessment), the latter to be fully offset by income. Key cost components noted by ACABQ: Item 2 Civilian personnel $4,321,400 (assumes 20% vacancy rate; Special Envoy on WAE basis up to 9 months); premises/accommodation $181,400 (rentals include $88,000 for HQ, $18,000 for five new locations, $7,200 for Tehran; utilities $41,200 incl. $1,200 Tehran and $3,000 new offices); transport operations $590,200 (vehicles $395,600 for 3 replacements and 16 new; maintenance $100/month per 37 vehicles); air operations $672,000 (fixed-wing hire/charter, up from $630,000 in 1998); communications $223,200 for additional equipment. Staffing: 1999 structure would total 82 mission posts (besides the Special Envoy), including Head of Mission at D-2, one upgraded D-1 (Deputy Head/Senior Political Affairs), 4 Political Affairs Officers, 4 military advisers (down from 5), and elimination of 2 civilian police; plus a new civil affairs unit (40 GTA posts: 1 P-5, 6 P-4, 6 P-3, 1 GS, 1 Field Service, 25 Local). SG also proposed 2 GTA HQ posts (P-4 and GS); ACABQ cautioned against proliferation of HQ political officers and considered the 1999 staffing/costing assumptions ambitious given security and the timing of the assessment mission. 1998 context: Section 3 appropriation $4,007,700 with $332,300 unencumbered; Section 32 staff assessment $327,300 with $58,100 unencumbered (staff assessment is fully offset by income). Establishment of the civil affairs unit is contingent on security and the late-January 1999 assessment team.
stages.posts_levels: P-5, P-4, P-3, General Service, Field Service, Local level
stages.budget_section: Section 3, Peacekeeping operations and special missions

stages.notes: Secretary-General statement (A/C.5/79/25) requests an additional appropriation of $5,951,100 for 2025 (potential charge against the contingency fund) excluding staff assessment, plus $572,200 under section 36 to be fully offset by income from staff assessment. Breakdown for 2025: Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) $1,560,400; Section 9 (DESA) $3,775,000; Section 28 (Global Communications) $20,100; Section 29B (Department of Operational Support) $328,100 (office space alterations for 22 long-term GTA positions); Section 29C (OICT) $103,600; Section 29D (Administration, Nairobi) $14,400; Section 34 (Safety and Security) $149,500. No absorption identified for 2025; requires additional appropriation. Multi-year mandate covers 2025–2027 (organizational session in Feb 2025; three substantive sessions per year). Temporary positions: (a) DESA: 23 general temporary assistance positions — 10 effective 1 March 2025 (1 D-1, 1 P-5, 3 P-4, 2 P-3, 3 GS (OL)) and 13 effective 1 April 2025 (2 P-5, 4 P-4, 6 P-3, 1 GS (OL)); required through 31 Dec 2027 except one Human Resources Officer (P-3) required only for 10 months in 2025. (b) DGACM: 2 temporary positions for six months in 2025 — Intergovernmental Officer (P-4) and Meetings Services Assistant (GS (OL)) — plus related staff travel to service sessions in Nairobi (2025–2026). Conference servicing totals before recosting (for context): 2025 $1,848,000; 2026 $1,828,900; 2027 $1,995,000 (not requested here beyond first-year extraction). Non-conference servicing totals before recosting: 2025 $4,103,100; 2026 $4,436,000; 2027 $4,251,900 (first-year only requested).

Context: Draft resolution A/C.2/79/L.8/Rev.1 establishes an intergovernmental negotiating committee (2025–2027) to draft a UN framework convention on international tax cooperation and two early protocols. Secretary-General (SG) request for 2025: total $6,528,300 including $572,200 staff assessment, implying $5,956,100 net of staff assessment. Section breakdown in SG statement (2025): section 2 $1,560,400; section 28 $20,100; section 29C $103,600; section 29D $14,400; section 34 $149,500; section 9 $3,775,000; section 29B $328,100. ACABQ recommendation (2025): approve $5,225,900 (additional appropriation; charge against contingency fund) under sections: section 2 $1,560,400; section 9 $3,365,800; section 29B $299,700. Recommend $556,658 under section 36 staff assessment (offset by equal income). ACABQ recommends absorption within existing resources for all proposed amounts under section 34 (Safety and security), section 28 (Global communications), section 29C (OICT), and section 29D (Administration, Nairobi). Staffing: SG proposed DESA 23 GTA positions (1 D-1; 3 P-5; 7 P-4; 8 P-3; 4 GS (OL)) through 31 Dec 2027 (one HR Officer only for 10 months in 2025). ACABQ recommends against 1 P-3 Public Information Officer, 1 P-3 Human Resources Officer, and 1 GS (OL), resulting in 20 DESA GTA positions recommended. DGACM: 2 temporary positions for six months in 2025 (P-4 Intergovernmental Officer; GS Meetings Services Assistant) in support of increased meetings; ACABQ did not object. Staff assessment is offset (income section 1) and not new funding.

title: Report of the Human Rights Council : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/62/L.84 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General’s PBI statement for draft resolution A/C.3/62/L.84 (Human Rights Council resolution 5/1, institution-building). Total additional requirements for 2008-2009 are $8,147,600 before recosting, including $448,100 staff assessment which is fully offset by income; net new money excluding staff assessment is $7,699,500 (reported here). Breakdown by section (before recosting): Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management): $3,847,300 (conference servicing for UPR working group: 6 weeks/year). Section 23 (Human rights): $3,149,000 (includes 18 posts: 17 P-4 for UPR documentation/support; 1 P-3 to manage special procedures public list/consultative group; plus $95,000 furniture/equipment). Section 27 (Public information): $353,700 (press/radio/TV/photo coverage and webcasting for UPR). Section 28E (Administration, Geneva): $349,500 (support costs incl. technicians and accommodation/telecoms for new posts). Section 35 (Staff assessment): $448,100 (offset by income; not counted in net). Preparatory work in late 2007 to be absorbed within 2006-2007; 2008-2009 costs represent a charge against the contingency fund. Amounts are before recosting and represent biennial totals; no first-year split provided in the document.

ACABQ reviewed the SG’s statement A/C.5/62/12 on PBI for draft resolution A/C.3/62/L.84 (HRC res. 5/1 implementation). SG estimated $8,147,600 for the 2008-2009 biennium across sections: $3,847,300 (Sect. 2), $3,149,000 (Sect. 23, incl. $3,054,000 for 18 posts and $95,000 for furniture/equipment), $353,700 (Sect. 27), $349,500 (Sect. 28E), plus $448,100 staff assessment (offset). SG also indicated 2006-2007 requirements would be absorbed. ACABQ recommends approval of $2,117,600 under Section 23 only and $302,400 staff assessment (offset), with no net additional requirements under Sections 2, 27 and 28E (including a $276,000 reduction for premises under Section 28E). ACABQ recommends establishing 12 P-4 posts (reducing SG’s 17 P-4 proposal) and recommends against 1 P-3 post for special procedures candidate list functions. The recommended requirement would be a charge against the contingency fund under GA resolutions 41/213 and 42/211. ACABQ notes conference servicing capacity recommended in A/62/7/Add.25 could offset meeting support needs under Section 2.

Fifth Committee decided to inform GA that, if draft resolution A/C.3/62/L.84 is adopted, additional requirements of $3,988,800 would arise under sections 23 ($3,114,800), 2 ($874,000) and 35 ($438,600). Staff assessment ($438,600) is offset by income section 1 and excluded from the main total above. Amounts pertain to the 2008-2009 biennium and would be considered under the contingency fund procedures. Primary section impacted is Section 23 (Human rights).

Includes provision to continue 1995 temporary posts and add new posts: Special Mission adds four Political Affairs Officers (P-5) and eight local-level staff (interpreters/drivers/guards) for outstations; OSGA adds one Field Service Communications Officer and two local-level posts (vehicle/generator mechanic and administrative assistant). Military observers: two, with costs included in personnel. Composition across both entities funded in 1996: one Under-Secretary-General, one D-2, five P-5, two General Service, two Security Officers, three Field Service, and 19 local-level (mix of continued and new). No absorption possible under section 3; requirements should be dealt with outside the contingency fund per GA res. 41/213, annex I, para. 11. Staff assessment of $269,200 under section 32 is fully offset by income section 1 and not counted as new money. Operational cost highlights: aircraft rental via UNOCHA ($325,000), communications ($109,500), maintenance of vehicles/equipment ($93,400), furniture/equipment ($106,000), rental/maintenance of premises and utilities ($68,000 total), MSA ($242,700), travel of reps ($25,000), staff travel ($142,700). Budget period covered: 1 Jan–31 Dec 1996. Related to medium-term plan programme 1 (Good offices, preventive diplomacy, peacemaking) and programme 37 (Emergency humanitarian assistance). OSGA to support Special Mission and maintain presence; UNOCHA’s humanitarian coordination remains voluntarily funded.

Fifth Committee decision: should the GA adopt draft resolution A/50/L.60, an appropriation not exceeding $2,684,400 would be required under section 3 for the 1996-1997 biennium. Staff assessment of $269,200 under section 32 is fully offset by income section 1. The appropriation would be considered by the GA in the context of the proposed programme budget for 1996-1997.

title: United Nations Human Rights Training and Documentation Centre for South-West Asia and the Arab Region : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/68/L.52 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

Section 24 breakdown (2014): posts (1 P-5, 1 P-4, 3 P-3) $927,700; general temporary assistance for three local support staff $289,800; travel of participants for two regional training workshops $260,300; travel of participants for one regional consultation $97,400; staff travel $14,700; contractual services $46,000; general operating expenses $12,000; supplies and materials $12,000; furniture and equipment $15,000. Staff assessment of $328,800 (section 36) is fully offset by income from staff assessment and is not new money.

First-year (2014) requested additional requirements total $1,146,700, comprising $1,116,300 under section 24 (Human rights) and $30,400 under section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) for post-session documentation. Biennium total indicated is $2,166,600, which would be a charge against the contingency fund; no absorption is possible within sections 2 and 24. Activities funded include two regional training sessions per year and one biennial regional consultation, plus operating costs (travel, contractual services for Arabic website, external translation, communications/utilities, supplies, furniture/equipment). Extrabudgetary resources expected: approximately $1.1 million per annum ($2.2 million per biennium) for two P-3 posts, two GS/LL support staff, and activity/operating costs; these are outside the regular budget request.

Secretary-General’s statement of programme budget implications for draft resolution A/C.3/68/L.52/Rev.1 (United Nations Human Rights Training and Documentation Centre for South‑West Asia and the Arab Region). First-year (2014) regular budget request totals $1,146,700, comprising $1,116,300 under section 24 (Human rights) and $30,400 under section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management). Biennium (2014–2015) totals: $2,166,600 overall, including $2,136,200 under section 24 and $30,400 under section 2. Year-by-year totals: 2014 = $1,146,700; 2015 = $1,019,900. Section 24 detailed biennium breakdown: Posts (1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 P-3) $1,196,200; General temporary assistance (1 GS/Local) $161,600; Travel of participants for two regional training workshops per year $520,600; Travel of participants for one regional consultation per biennium $97,400; Travel of staff $29,400; Contractual services $92,000; General operating expenses $12,000; Supplies and materials $12,000; Furniture and equipment $15,000. Regular-budget training/consultations subtotal equals $618,000 (workshops + consultation). Other regular-budget operating per para. 11 totals $160,400 (staff travel, website maintenance/translation, communications, utilities/maintenance, supplies, furniture/equipment). Conference servicing (section 2) is $30,400 in 2014 only for post-session documentation of the report to the 69th session. One GS/Local is financed via general temporary assistance (not a post); locally recruited staff contracts for OHCHR regional/subregional offices are administered by UNDP and reflected under GTA. Extrabudgetary resources expected: approx. $2.2 million for the biennium ($1.1 million per annum), comprising $985,600 for two P-3 and two GS/Local, and $1,214,400 for activities and related operating expenses (2014–2015). No provisions exist in the proposed programme budget and requirements cannot be absorbed; the amount constitutes a charge against the contingency fund. Section 36 staff assessment: $149,800 (fully offset by income from staff assessment).

Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, should draft resolution A/C.3/68/L.52/Rev.1 be adopted, net additional resources of $2,166,600 would be required in the 2014-2015 biennium, charged against the contingency fund. Breakdown: Section 24, Human rights: $2,136,200; Section 2, General Assembly and ECOSOC affairs and conference management: $30,400. Staff assessment of $149,800 under section 36 is fully offset by income section 1 and is not new money.

ACABQ recommends that, should the GA adopt draft resolution A/C.3/68/L.52/Rev.1, resources of $2,166,600 (net) would be required for the biennium 2014-2015. Breakdown by section per SG statement: Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management): $30,400; Section 24 (Human rights): $2,136,200. Regular budget posts proposed: 3 Professional (1 P-5 Head of Centre, 1 P-4, 1 P-3) and 1 General Service (Local level). ACABQ notes the P-5 Head of Centre should be treated as a conversion of an existing extrabudgetary post, applying recruitment rules as for new posts. Additional staffing funded from extrabudgetary sources: 2 P-3 and 2 GS (Local) positions (not part of regular budget). Non-post regular resources total $940,000, including $647,400 for travel ($520,600 for participants in two regional training workshops per year; $97,400 for participants in one biennial regional consultation; $29,400 for staff travel). Extrabudgetary resources projected at $2.2 million for 2014-2015; host Government in-kind contribution valued at $138,600 per year (premises and utilities) and voluntary contributions of $350,000 in 2010 and 2012. ACABQ requests clearer rationale for distribution between regular and extrabudgetary financing and asks SG to report in the 2016-2017 budget submission. title: Oceans and the law of the sea : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/62/L.27 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Scope: SG statement on draft resolution A/62/L.27 (Oceans and the law of the sea) for the biennium 2008-2009. The SG estimates additional requirements of $1,962,100 under Section 8 (Legal affairs) to enhance GIS laboratory services (includes one-time licensed software $1,241,600; data-processing services $302,600; training $192,000; infrastructure support $225,900). Conference servicing for the three-day extensions of the Commission’s 21st and 22nd sessions is estimated at full cost $174,300: Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) $143,100 and Section 28D (Office of Central Support Services) $31,200. Table shows a minor discrepancy listing Section 28D as 32.2 (thousand) while paragraph 10 states 31.2 (thousand); the total in the table (2,136.4 thousand) aligns with the 31.2 figure. Total full-cost requirements across sections would be $2,136,400 for 2008-2009, but the SG indicates efforts would be made to absorb Section 2 ($143,100) and Section 28D ($31,200) within existing resources. Action required (para. 15): $1,962,100 under Section 8 would be charged to the contingency fund. ACABQ recommends approval of additional requirements totaling $1,962,100 under section 8 for the biennium 2008-2009, as a charge against the contingency fund. The one-time provision covers: specialized GIS software ($1,241,600); data-processing services ($302,600); related training ($192,000); and support to data-processing infrastructure ($225,900). The Secretary-General indicated efforts would be made to meet any additional requirements under section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) and section 28 D (Office of Central Support Services) within existing provisions; no additional appropriation amounts were identified for those sections.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/62/L.27 (Oceans and the law of the sea), additional requirements of $1,962,100 would be required under Section 8 for the biennium 2008-2009. This additional appropriation would be considered under the contingency fund procedures.

Staff assessment of $175,800 under Section 36 is to be offset by an equivalent increase in income section 1. The ad hoc intergovernmental committee would have an organizational session (10 meetings/5 days) and two sessions (30 meetings/15 days) with conference services (interpretation in six languages and translation of four pre-session and three post-session documents). DESA requires GTA for 12 months for 1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 P-3 and 1 GS (Other level), plus contractual services and operating expenses. Support to commence no later than January 2024; no provision exists in the 2024 proposed programme budget and absorption is not feasible. The additional appropriation represents a potential charge against the contingency fund.

Fifth Committee decision adopted on 22 December 2023: if the GA adopts draft resolution A/C.2/78/L.18/Rev.1, additional resources of $1,495,500 would be required for 2024, comprising $753,200 under section 2 (General Assembly and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) and $742,300 under section 9 (Economic and social affairs). Representing a charge against the contingency fund. An additional $175,800 under section 36 (Staff assessment) is to be fully offset by income and is not new money.

title: Preventing and combating corrupt practices and transfer of assets of illicit origin and returning such assets, in particular to the countries of origin, consistent with the United Nations Convention against Corruption : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/64/L.64 : statement  / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: This is a Secretary-General PBI statement for the biennium 2010-2011. The SG requests regular budget resources only for posts and related support, amounting to $1,689,300 gross ($1,460,000 net) for the biennium, charged to the contingency fund; first-year split is not provided in the document. Breakdown of the regular budget ask: 9 additional posts (1 D-1, 1 P-5, 2 P-4, 1 P-3, 3 P-2, 1 GS (Other level)) costing $1,409,600 net after recosting; computer maintenance $23,400; communication costs $27,000. Staff assessment of $229,300 (section 36) is fully offset under Income section 1 and not counted as new money. A total of 14 posts are required to support the mechanism; 5 posts will be accommodated through existing staff in the Corruption and Economic Crime Section (absorbed), leaving 9 posts to be established. Operational requirements (to be funded through voluntary contributions pending the Implementation Review Group decision): country visits $678,000 (40 per biennium, interpretation in 15 countries); translation for individual reviews $1,787,400 (approx. 4,000 pages per biennium); Implementation Review Group conference servicing $393,600 and translation $395,400 (note: conference servicing provision for 40 meetings is already available under section 2 and can be used); LDC representatives’ travel and DSA $459,000 (avg. 31 representatives); training for governmental experts $267,800 (one five-day course per year for 30 experts from developing countries); videoconference costs $67,800. The table totals $5,738,300 gross ($5,509,000 net) for the biennium, but only $1,460,000 net is requested from the regular budget; the remainder is proposed to be funded by voluntary contributions until an IRG decision. Additional outputs are added under Section 16, subprogramme 1 (Rule of law). Also references Section 28F (Administration, Vienna) for $27,000 communications. Charge against the contingency fund for 2010-2011 is indicated.
stages.posts_levels: D-1, P-5, P-4, P-4, P-3, P-2, P-2, P-2, GS (Other level)
stages.budget_section: Section 16, International drug control, crime and terrorism prevention and criminal justice

stages.notes: Secretary-General estimates additional regular budget resources for the biennium 2010-2011 of $1,689,300 gross ($1,460,000 net), comprising: Section 16 ($1,433,000: posts net $1,409,600 + computer maintenance $23,400) and Section 28F ($27,000: communications); plus Section 36 staff assessment $229,300 offset by income. This is proposed as a charge against the contingency fund. The total biennial requirements presented (gross $5,738,300; net $5,509,000) include operational items proposed to be funded through voluntary contributions until the Implementation Review Group decides: country visits $678,000; translation for individual reviews $1,787,400; travel and DSA for representatives of least developed countries $459,000; training $267,800; videoconference costs $67,800. Conference servicing for the Implementation Review Group (20 meetings per year) is to be absorbed under existing Section 2 provisions; tabled amounts (interpretation/conference servicing $393,600 and translation of 100 pages per year $395,400) are covered by that provision per para. 25. Of the 14 total posts needed, 5 are to be accommodated by existing staff; 9 new posts are requested as listed. First-year (2010) split of the requested net amount is not provided; amount shown reflects the biennial net request.

title: Committee against Torture : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/67/L.45 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: PBI for draft resolution A/C.3/67/L.45 (Committee against Torture). Temporary authorization for an additional week per session from May 2013 to end-November 2014 to address backlogs. First-year (2013) additional requirements total $1,444,600: Section 2 $1,143,900 (conference services, including interpretation, summary records, documentation), Section 24 $294,600 (GTA P-3 for 14 working months; DSA $79,400 for 10 members for 14 extra days), Section 29E $6,100 (support services in Geneva). No travel costs; DSA only for extended stay. 40 additional meetings in total over 2013-2014; estimated documentation: 600 pages pre-session, 480 in-session, 680 post-session (280 pages in all official languages; remainder in the Committee’s three working languages). Additional staffing via general temporary assistance at P-3: 14 working months per year (28 working months over 2013-2014); no establishment of posts. Charge against the contingency fund for 2013 requires an additional appropriation; 2014 requirements ($1,444,600) to be considered in the proposed 2014-2015 programme budget. Staff assessment ($20,300 in 2013 and $20,300 in 2014 under section 37) is offset by income and not counted as new money. Budget sections involved: Section 2, Section 24, and Section 29E.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.3/67/L.45 authorizes the Committee against Torture to continue one additional week per session from May 2013 to end-November 2014 to address backlogs. 2013 additional requirements total $1,444,600, comprising Section 2: $1,143,900; Section 24: $294,600 (DSA $79,400; GTA P-3 for 14 working months $215,200); Section 29E: $6,100. No additional travel costs; DSA for 10 members for 14 days; 40 additional meetings requiring interpretation, summary records, and extra documentation (approx. 600 pre-, 480 in-, 680 post-session pages). Charge against the contingency fund for 2012-2013; 2014 requirements of equal amount to be considered in the proposed programme budget for 2014-2015.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: SG estimated additional requirements of $1,444,600 per year for 2013 and 2014, broken down as: $294,600 under Section 24 (Human rights), $1,143,900 under Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management), and $6,100 under Section 29E (Administration, Geneva). For 2013, the $1,444,600 would be a charge against the contingency fund and require an additional appropriation; for 2014, the same amount would be included in the proposed programme budget for 2014-2015. ACABQ recommends the Assembly request the SG to absorb the additional requirements; failing absorption, reflect expenditures in the second performance report. Activities include 40 additional sessional meetings (interpretation, summary records, documentation, per diem) and general temporary assistance equivalent to 14 work-months per year at P-3 for 2013-2014.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should draft resolution A/C.3/67/L.45 be adopted, additional requirements of $1,444,600 (net) would arise for the biennium 2012-2013. This represents a charge against the contingency fund and would require appropriation for 2012-2013. The document does not record final GA approval.

title: Developments in the field of information and telecommunications in the context of international security : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/68/L.37 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: SG statement (rule 153) on draft resolution A/C.1/68/L.37. Total additional requirements for the 2014-2015 biennium: $1,439,400, to be charged to the contingency fund. ACABQ recommends that, if GA adopts draft resolution A/C.1/68/L.37, additional resources of $1,439,400 for the 2014-2015 biennium be appropriated as a charge against the contingency fund. title: Situation of human rights in Myanmar : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/67/L.49/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Net additional requirements: $1,397,800 (gross $1,519,900) for 1 Jan–31 Dec 2013 to continue the Secretary-General’s good offices in Myanmar via the Special Adviser. Resources cover salaries of the Special Adviser (USG) and four staff (2 P-4, 1 P-3, 1 GS (Other Level)), official travel, consultants, and miscellaneous services; other support provided by DPA. Included in SPM estimates A/67/346/Add.1 under Section 3, Political affairs, for the 2013 appropriation. Also noted: a perennial provision of $73,600 per annum under Section 24, Human rights, for the Special Rapporteur; no additional resources requested under Section 24.
stages.posts_levels: USG, P-4, P-4, P-3, GS (Other Level)

stages.notes: SG estimates $1,397,800 net ($1,519,900 gross) for 1 Jan–31 Dec 2013 to continue good offices on Myanmar through the Special Adviser. Resources cover salaries of the Special Adviser (USG) and four staff (2 P-4, 1 P-3, 1 GS), official travel (Myanmar, region, Europe, North America, including UNHQ), consultants, and miscellaneous support. Included under the 2013 appropriation for special political missions (section 3) via A/67/346/Add.1. Additionally, a perennial provision of $73,600 per annum for the Special Rapporteur is already budgeted under section 24, Human rights; no additional resources are requested for section 24.

ACABQ states that, if the GA adopts draft resolution A/C.3/67/L.49/Rev.1, resources of $1,397,800 net ($1,519,900 gross) are required for 1 Jan–31 Dec 2013 to continue the Secretary-General’s good offices via the Special Adviser on Myanmar. These requirements would be charged against the 2013 appropriation for special political missions under section 3 and have already been submitted in A/67/346/Add.1 (paras. 25–27); related ACABQ observations are in A/67/604. Requirements for the Special Rapporteur are perennial and provided for at $73,600 per annum under section 24, Human rights, with no additional resources requested under that section.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.3/67/L.49/Rev.1, $1,397,800 would be required for 2013 under Section 3 for continued efforts of the SG’s good offices on Myanmar. Staff assessment of $122,100 under Section 37 is fully offset by Income Section 1. Approval sought in SG report A/67/346/Add.1.

title: Implementation of the United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification in those Countries Experiencing Serious Drought and/or Desertification, particularly in Africa : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/58/L.7 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

title: Developments in the field of information and telecommunications in the context of international security : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/70/L.45 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.1/70/L.45 would establish a Group of Governmental Experts on ICTs/international security. SG estimates additional non-recurrent resources across 2016-2017 totaling $1,329,500, to be charged to the contingency fund and requiring an additional appropriation; absorption within existing resources is not possible. First-year (2016) breakdown: Section 2 conference servicing $277,700 (interpretation $195,400; pre-session documentation $18,000; post-session documentation $64,300). Section 4 disarmament $382,000 (consultant $25,000; ad hoc expert groups $357,000 for experts’ travel/DSA/terminal for 25 members and secretary). Second-year (2017) indicative amounts: Section 2 $287,800 (interpretation $195,400; post-session documentation $92,400), Section 4 $382,000 (consultant $25,000; ad hoc expert groups $357,000). Activity plan: four 5-day sessions (two in New York and two in Geneva), 40 meetings total with six-language interpretation and related documentation; one final report. No posts are proposed.
stages.budget_section: Sections 2 (General Assembly and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) and 4 (Disarmament)

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.1/70/L.45 establishes a Group of Governmental Experts (GGE) on developments in information and telecommunications in the context of international security. Activities span 2016 and 2017 with four five-day sessions (two per year; New York/Geneva), totaling 40 meetings, requiring six-language interpretation and documentation. Additional non-recurrent requirements: Section 2 (Conference management) $277,700 in 2016 and $287,800 in 2017 (total $565,500), including interpretation ($195,400 per year), pre-session documentation ($18,000 in 2016), and post-session documentation ($64,300 in 2016; $92,400 in 2017). Section 4 (Disarmament) $382,000 in 2016 and $382,000 in 2017 (total $764,000), comprising ad hoc expert group costs ($357,000 per year for travel/DSA/terminal expenses for experts and the secretary) and a consultant ($25,000 per year, including related travel). No posts are established. The total biennial additional resources amount to $1,329,500. The Secretariat indicates no capacity for absorption; an additional appropriation is required and would be a charge against the contingency fund. Primary affected budget sections: Section 4 (Disarmament) and Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management).

SG estimated $1,329,500 for the biennium 2016-2017: $565,500 under section 2 (conference servicing) and $764,000 under section 4 (non-conference servicing). ACABQ recommends reducing consultants by $8,000, yielding $1,321,500 total, split as $565,500 (section 2) and $756,000 (section 4). Establishes a Group of Governmental Experts (25 members) meeting in New York and Geneva; includes travel for 25 experts plus one staff and UNIDIR consultancy support. The recommended amount would require an additional appropriation and represent a charge against the contingency fund. Amounts pertain to the biennium; no posts created.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.1/70/L.45, resources of $1,321,500 would be required under sections 2 and 4 of the 2016-2017 proposed programme budget, representing a charge against the contingency fund. Plenary item: Developments in the field of information and telecommunications in the context of international security.

title: Follow-up to and implementation of the outcomes of the International Conferences on Financing for Development : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/78/L.59 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Breakdown of 2024 additional requirements (excluding staff assessment): Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management: $362,800 (Interpretation services: $249,700; Documentation services: $113,100). Section 9, Economic and social affairs: $866,900 (General temporary assistance for five positions: $759,300; Consultants: $57,600; Travel of staff: $20,000; Contractual services: $20,000; Supplies and materials: $10,000). Section 29C, Office of Information and Communications Technology: $54,100 (meetings support, including closed captioning). Total 2024 excluding staff assessment: $1,283,800. Staff assessment: $137,300 in 2024 (offset by income under section 36; not new money). 2025 indicative amounts (not part of first-year request): $1,257,700 excluding staff assessment; staff assessment $137,100. Documentation workload: 2024 – 15 documents, 39,200 words, $113,100; 2025 – 6 documents, 16,000 words, $46,200. The 2025 requirements would be included in the proposed programme budget for 2025. title: Third United Nations Conference on the Least Developed Countries : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/56/L.78 : report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

stages.notes: ACABQ reviewed the SG’s statement (A/C.5/56/29) for draft resolution A/C.2/56/L.78. SG’s proposals: total requirements $3,906,400, comprising $3,055,600 under new Section 9A (Office of the High Representative for LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS) and $850,000 under Section 27D (Office of Central Support Services). Offsetting reductions: $709,900 under Section 10 (Africa: New Agenda for Development) and $1,975,800 under Section 11A (Trade and development), yielding net additional requirements of $1,220,700. Non-post resources total $1,231,400 (of which $850,800 for premises: $522,800 rental, $328,000 alterations, under Section 27D). Staffing proposed by SG: 13 regular budget posts (9 Professional, incl. 1 USG, and 4 GS), achieved by establishing 1 USG and 1 D-2, and redeploying 3 posts from Section 10 (1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 GS) and 8 from Section 11A (1 P-5, 2 P-4, 1 P-3, 1 P-2, 3 GS). Additionally, it is anticipated six other posts would be seconded or funded from extrabudgetary resources. ACABQ recommends establishment of the Office and a post at the USG level, but to defer a decision on up to 12 other posts (including the proposed D-2 and others) pending a SG report on programmatic implications and resource availability. ACABQ further recommends informing the GA that, should it adopt the draft resolution, net additional appropriations up to $1,220,700 would be required at this stage, subject to contingency fund procedures and subsequent adjustment based on the requested report. Related programmatic links: medium-term plan programmes 8 and 9; budget Sections 10, 11A, and 27D.
stages.budget_section: Section 9A, Office of the High Representative for the Least Developed Countries, Landlocked Developing Countries and Small Island Developing States

stages.notes: SG statement for draft resolution A/C.2/56/L.78 establishing the Office of the High Representative for LDCs/LLDCs/SIDS. Total biennial requirements: $3,906,400, comprising $3,055,600 under new Section 9A and $850,800 under Section 27D (rental of premises $522,800 and alterations $328,000). Funded by redeployments of $709,900 from Section 10 (Africa: New Agenda for Development) and $1,975,800 from Section 11A (Trade and development), leaving net additional requirements of $1,220,700 charged to the contingency fund ($369,900 for Section 9A; $850,800 for Section 27D). Staff assessment increase of $74,900 is fully offset by income. New regular-budget posts established: 1 USG and 1 D-2. Additional posts in the new office are redeployed: from Section 10 (1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 GS) and from Section 11A (1 P-5, 2 P-4, 1 P-3, 1 P-2, 3 GS). It is proposed to abolish one D-1 and one P-5 in Section 11A to offset resources. Anticipated secondments/extrabudgetary: 1 D-1, 1 P-5, 1 P-3 and 3 GS. Non-post estimates (biennial): Other staff $103,000; Consultants $42,000; Travel $60,000; Contractual services $20,000; General operating $77,600; Hospitality $2,000; Supplies $2,400; Furniture/equipment $73,600; Rental/alterations $850,800. The resolution requests implementation within the approved 2002-2003 budget level and seeking voluntary contributions; however, a contingency fund charge is still required. Annual split is not provided; figures are for the 2002-2003 biennium.
stages.budget_section: Section 9A, Office of the High Representative for Least Developed Countries, Landlocked Developing Countries and Small Island Developing States

title: Oceans and the law of the sea : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/75/L.39 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: 2021 requested amounts (excluding staff assessment): Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) $623,900; Section 8 (Legal affairs) $584,200. Staff assessment for 2021 totals $119,900 ($74,900 conference + $45,000 non-conference) and is fully offset under income section 1. The $1,208,100 requires an additional appropriation for 2021 and represents a charge against the contingency fund. Conference servicing includes a two-week fourth session of the intergovernmental conference (20 meetings over 10 days with interpretation in six languages) and added documentation and publications; meetings services for the Ad Hoc Working Group are on an 'if available' basis. Two temporary posts (P-3 and GS (Other level)) are extensions from 2020; one new temporary post (P-2) is established in 2021 to support the third cycle of the Regular Process. The third cycle runs 2021–2025; out-year resource needs will be included in future budget proposals. The Secretariat indicates no capacity for absorption within the 2021 budget (additional resources must be provided).

ACABQ reviewed the SG statement A/C.5/75/19 on draft resolution A/75/L.39 (Oceans and the law of the sea) and recommended the full additional requirements of $1,208,100 for 2021 as a charge against the contingency fund. Breakdown: Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) $623,900; Section 8 (Legal Affairs) $584,200. Within Section 2: meetings services $156,000 for 20 meetings (10 days) with interpretation for the fourth session of the intergovernmental conference; meetings services for the Ad Hoc Working Group of the Whole (AHWG) on an 'if available' basis; documentation services $467,900 (pre-, in-, and post-session documents for the IGC and post-session documents for the AHWG) in six languages. Within Section 8: post resources $319,900 for the extension of two temporary posts (1 P-3 and 1 GS (Other level)) and establishment of one new temporary post of Associate Programme Management Officer (P-2); the P-2 post budgeted with a 50% vacancy rate in 2021; non-post resources $264,300 (hospitality, travel of experts and staff, contractual services, general operating expenses, stationery, furniture and equipment, alteration of premises). Overall estimated requirements for the third cycle (2021–2025) total $6,434,900 ($1,647,100 conference-servicing and $4,787,800 non-conference-servicing); amounts for 2022–2025 to be included in future proposed programme budgets. Staff assessment for 2021: $119,900 under section 36, fully offset by income section 1.
stages.posts_levels: P-2 (new temporary), P-3 (temporary extension), General Service (Other level) (temporary extension)
stages.budget_section: Section 8, Legal Affairs (also Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management)

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decision on draft resolution A/75/L.39 (Oceans and the law of the sea). title: Future operations of the International Research and Training Institute for the Advancement of Women : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/59/L.26 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.3/59/L.26 seeks to ensure INSTRAW can function for one year (1 Jan–31 Dec 2005). Total 2005 requirements are estimated at $1,183,760, comprising: $620,530 for 10 posts (1 D-2, 1 P-3, 3 P-2/1 and 5 General Service); $423,000 for substantive activities (including GTA, consultancy, travel); and $140,230 for administrative costs. Projected Trust Fund balance as of 31 Dec 2004 is $91,330, sufficient only through Jan 2005. Therefore, contingent on voluntary contributions, up to $1,092,430 may be required from the regular budget for 2005. Any such amount would be a charge against the contingency fund and require an additional appropriation for the biennium 2004-2005. Background: the Fifth Committee had earlier set aside $234,700 in the contingency fund under GA res. 58/244 but it was not drawn at that time.

Draft resolution A/C.3/59/L.26 seeks to ensure INSTRAW can function for one year (1 Jan–31 Dec 2005). Total 2005 requirements are estimated at $1,183,700, comprised of: $620,500 for 10 posts (1 D-2, 1 P-3, 3 P-2/1, and 5 GS); $423,000 for substantive activities (general temporary assistance, consultancy, travel); and $140,200 for administrative costs (general operating expenses, supplies, materials, equipment). The INSTRAW Trust Fund balance projected at 31 Dec 2004 is $91,300, sufficient only through January 2005. Consequently, up to $1,092,400 may be required from the regular budget to maintain operations through 2005, contingent on voluntary contributions. Any allocation would be an additional appropriation and a charge against the contingency fund (per GA res. 41/213). The 2004-2005 programme budget provides no resources to support INSTRAW operations in 2005. Context: the Fifth Committee previously set aside $234,700 in the contingency fund for 2004-2005 for potential complementary funds to INSTRAW (A/58/649), and the SG reported then that no immediate additional resources were required (A/59/433).

Secretary-General’s statement (A/C.5/59/16) indicated total 2005 resource requirements of $1,183,700 for INSTRAW. The Trust Fund balance was projected at $91,300 as at 31 December 2004, with additional pledges of $12,900 received on 11 November 2004. If insufficient voluntary contributions are received by 31 January 2005, continued operation through 31 December 2005 would require additional regular budget resources of $1,092,400 under section 9; voluntary funds would offset any regular budget requirement. ACABQ recommends informing the GA that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.3/59/L.26, an additional provision of up to $1,092,400 would arise under section 9 and should be considered in the contingency fund procedures. Note: $234,700 set aside in the contingency fund for 2004-2005 remained unutilized.

title: Modalities, format and organization of the High-level Plenary Meeting of the 60th session of the General Assembly : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/59/L.53 : report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

stages.notes: ACABQ reviewed the SG’s PBI statement (A/C.5/59/25) for draft resolution A/59/L.53. ACABQ identified further possible economies: general temporary assistance (e.g., 9 months GTA at P-2 for a project assistant), protocol, overtime, staff travel, equipment rental, and publication costs; and urged greater use of in-house language services. Recommendation: make every effort to absorb; no additional appropriation at this time; any necessary resources to be addressed in the second performance report for 2004-2005. The GTA at P-2 mentioned is temporary assistance, not an established post.

Secretary-General’s statement (rule 153) for draft resolution A/59/L.53. Net additional resource requirements requested for 2005 total $1,060,700 at 2004-2005 rates, to be a charge against the contingency fund (appropriations increase). Section 29D: $195,100 (OCSS: event support totaling $180,100 for bilateral rooms, off-site accreditation, group photo, special conference setups, LAN connectivity, rentals, temp sound engineers, plant engineering overtime; plus $15,000 sound engineers for weekend general debate). Items to be absorbed (not included in the $1,060,700): $317,100 under Section 2 for additional interpreters due to moving the 2005 ECOSOC substantive session from Geneva to New York; and up to $350,000 under Section 28 from within existing PI resources. Other notes: High-level Dialogue on Financing for Development (27–28 June 2005) requires no additional resources (already in calendar). Interactive hearings with NGOs/civil society/private sector in June 2005 are considered GA meetings and require no additional resources unless parameters change. Any further requirements will be reported in the second performance report for 2004-2005.

title: Future operations of the International Research and Training Institute for the Advancement of Women : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/60/L.15 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.3/60/L.15 seeks funding to enable INSTRAW to carry out core functions for at least three years. The SG estimates 2006 core requirements at $1,314,352, with an anticipated Trust Fund balance of ~$272,200 (after a $200,000 reserve for potential closure costs), yielding a shortfall of ~$1,067,200 that may be required from the regular budget for 2006. The proposed 2006-2007 programme budget includes no resources for INSTRAW; any 2006 allocation would be an additional appropriation charged to the contingency fund (resolution 41/213) and subject to Fifth Committee review. Funding for 2007–2008 would imply an annual subvention and requires an explicit GA decision and amendment of article VII of INSTRAW’s statute (which currently precludes extra costs to the regular budget). Composition of 2006 requirements: $705,300 for nine posts (1 D-2, 1 P-3, 2 P-2/1, 5 GS); $423,500 for substantive activities (GTA, consultancy, travel, contractual services); and $185,552 for administrative costs (GOE, supplies, equipment). Administrative and budgetary matters should be handled by the Fifth Committee per GA resolution 45/248 B.

This SG statement (A/C.5/60/16) relates to draft resolution A/C.3/60/L.15/Rev.1 on INSTRAW. 2006 core requirements are estimated at $1,314,352 (table shows $1,314,400 due to rounding). Available balance as at 1 January 2006 is estimated at $272,200 (after a $200,000 reserve set aside for potential closure liabilities). The resulting shortfall for 2006 is approximately $1,042,200, which may be required from the regular budget, contingent on voluntary contributions. The proposed programme budget for 2006-2007 provides no resources for INSTRAW; any allocation would be an additional appropriation charged to the contingency fund (resolutions 41/213 and 42/211). Funding for 2007 would imply an annual subvention subject to an explicit GA decision and amendment of article VIII of the INSTRAW statute (currently provides that no extra cost to the regular budget is incurred). Staffing reflected in the 2006 budget comprises 9 posts (4 Professional: 1 D-2, 1 P-3, 2 P-2; and 5 General Service) with $705,300 in post costs; plus $423,500 for substantive activities and $185,552 for administrative costs. Historical context: in 2005 a subvention of $1,092,400 was authorized, of which about 50 percent ($546,200) was used; an amount of $234,700 set aside for 2004 was not used. The SG’s quantified request here covers 2006 only; 2007 requirements are indeterminate at this stage.

Fifth Committee decision informs GA that, if draft resolution A/C.3/60/L.15/Rev.1 is adopted, additional requirements of up to $1,042,200 would arise under section 9; requests appropriation of that amount against the contingency fund for INSTRAW's functioning in 2006. Marked as an exceptional measure.

Mandate: Establish a UN Group of Governmental Experts (GGE) of up to 25 Member States; hold two 2‑week sessions in Geneva (one in 2023, one in 2024); organize a two‑day open‑ended intersessional informal consultative meeting in New York in 2024; transmit the GGE report to GA (79th session) and the Conference on Disarmament (prior to its 2025 session). 2023 requested (non‑staff assessment): $568,900, to be charged against the contingency fund, under: Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) $323,500; Section 4 (Disarmament) $234,700; Section 29E (Administration, Geneva) $10,700.
