Conference‑servicing additions: 2023 $334,200 (Section 2 interpretation $184,000; Section 2 documentation $139,500; Section 29E sound technician/recording $7,000; Section 29E Webex support $3,700); 2024 $387,600 (Section 2 interpretation $206,600; Section 2 documentation $168,100; Section 28 webcasting $1,200; Section 29C IT servicing $4,700; Section 29E sound technician/recording $7,000). Total conference servicing 2023–2024: $721,800. Non‑conference additions (Section 4): 2023 $234,700 (consultants $26,800; experts $200,100; staff travel $7,800); 2024 $238,800 (consultants $26,800; experts $204,200; staff travel $7,800). Total non‑conference 2023–2024: $473,500. Summary (including staff assessment): 2023 $618,900; 2024 $683,700; total $1,302,600. Absorption: The SG indicates no provision exists in 2023 and activities cannot be absorbed; requires additional appropriation.

ACABQ reviewed the SG’s PBI (A/C.5/77/14) for draft resolution A/C.1/77/L.70. SG indicated total additional resources of $1,302,600 for 2023-2024, with $568,900 for 2023 (DGACM $323,500; ODA $234,700; UNOG $10,700) and $683,700 for 2024 to be reflected in the 2024 budget. ACABQ recommended a 25% reduction ($2,700) to the UNOG line under section 29E (reducing $10,700 to $8,000), resulting in $566,200 additional appropriation for 2023 across section 2 (DGACM $323,500), section 4 (Disarmament $234,700) and section 29E (Administration, Geneva $8,000). This would be a charge against the contingency fund. ACABQ encouraged the Secretariat to absorb requirements where feasible but did not recommend full absorption. Activities include: interpretation and documentation for 20 meetings (10 days) in Geneva; travel/DSA/terminal expenses for up to 25 State representatives and a consultant for substantive support (ODA); sound technicians/recording and Webex support (UNOG). Staff assessment of $49,600 (section 36) is offset by income and not new money. Mandate spans sessions in 2023 and 2024, with an intersessional meeting in New York in 2024 and report transmission to GA (seventy-ninth session) and CD (prior to 2025 session).

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.1/77/L.70, additional resources for 2023 would be required: $323,500 under section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management), $234,700 under section 4 (Disarmament), and $8,000 under section 29E (Administration, Geneva), representing a charge against the contingency fund. Staff assessment of $49,600 under section 36 to be fully offset by income section 1. Amount pertains to 2023 only and is contingent on GA adoption of the draft resolution.

title: Implementation of Agenda 21, the Programme for the Further Implementation of Agenda 21 and the outcomes of the World Summit on Sustainable Development : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/64/L.59 : statement  / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General states total additional conference-related requirements of $976,500 for the biennium 2010-2011 for two Preparatory Committee meetings, with $460,400 to be absorbed, leaving a net requirement of $516,100 to be appropriated under Section 2 as a charge against the contingency fund. Pre-absorption breakdown: Meeting servicing $175,700; Documentation: pre-session $230,000, in-session $259,400, post-session $283,400; Other conference support (Section 28D) $28,000. The 2012 United Nations Conference on Sustainable Development and the third PrepCom (to be held in Brazil) will be handled in the 2012-2013 budget; Brazil to reimburse additional costs of holding sessions away from headquarters pursuant to GA res. 40/243. The document does not provide a year-by-year split of the $516,100 within the 2010-2011 biennium; the amount recorded here reflects the biennial net request with first-year breakdown unspecified.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: Scope: Costs relate to the first (May 2010) and second (March 2011) meetings of the Preparatory Committee for the United Nations Conference on Sustainable Development, arising under Section 2 and Section 28D of the proposed programme budget 2010-2011. Gross additional requirements for the two meetings total $976,500 ($948,500 under Section 2: $175,700 meeting services + $772,800 documentation; and $28,000 under Section 28D: other conference support). Absorption plan: (a) Meeting services ($175,700) and other conference support ($28,000) fully absorbed using resources allotted for servicing the General Assembly, provided the Assembly does not meet in parallel; (b) Half of pre-session and post-session documentation costs ($256,700) absorbed through contractual translation. Net charge against the contingency fund after absorption for the biennium is $516,100 under Section 2. First-year (2010) share of the net request is derived from the meeting-specific breakdown: meeting services and other support fully absorbed; in-session documentation ($129,700) remains; pre-session and post-session documentation are assumed evenly absorbed, leaving $57,500 (pre) and $70,850 (post) for 2010, totaling $258,050. Future years: The 2012 Conference and the third Preparatory Committee meeting will be budgeted in 2012-2013; Brazil, as host, will reimburse actual additional off-site costs per GA resolution 40/243.

title: The illicit trade in small arms and light weapons in all its aspects : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/56/L.47 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: SG statement under rule 153 for draft resolution A/C.1/56/L.47 (illicit trade in small arms and light weapons). Additional requirements for 2002-2003 total $559,800 gross, including $43,800 staff assessment (offset; not new money). Net new resources requested: $516,000, comprised of: posts $217,000 (1 P-4 Political Affairs Officer and 1 GS (Other) for 24 work-months), consultants $29,100 (biennial meeting: $10,200; expert group: $18,900), travel of ad hoc experts $210,000 (20 participants to three five-day expert group meetings), and common service costs $59,900 (Section 27 D). Charge against the contingency fund. No provision made under Section 4 for these activities and they cannot be absorbed despite 'within existing resources' language; if funds are not provided, mandated activities may not be implemented. Conference-servicing costs estimated at full cost: $715,100 for three sessions of the group of governmental experts and $362,600 for the 2003 biennial meeting of States (total $1,077,700), but no additional appropriation required for conference services per existing capacity planning. A future review conference to be convened no later than 2006 will have resource requirements reflected in the relevant future budget outline/proposed programme budget. Programmes referenced: Programme 2 (Disarmament) and Programme 6 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference services). Sections impacted: Section 4 (Disarmament) $456,100, Section 27 D (Office of Central Support Services) $59,900, Section 32 (Staff assessment) $43,800 (offset). The annex confirms non-conference-servicing subtotal $516,000 and staff assessment $43,800. First-year (2002) vs. second-year (2003) split is not specified in the document.

ACABQ reviewed SG statement A/C.5/56/13 for draft resolution A/C.1/56/L.47. Additional resource requirements total $559,800 for the biennium 2002-2003, broken down as: $456,100 under section 4 (Disarmament), $59,900 under section 27D (Office of Central Support Services), and $43,800 under section 32 (Staff assessment, offset by income section 1). Staff assessment is fully offset; net new resources are $516,000. ACABQ recommends charging the additional requirements against the contingency fund per GA resolutions 41/213 and 42/211. ACABQ has no objection to establishing the two new posts (one P-4 and one GS). Related requirements also include five work-weeks of consultancy services and travel for up to 20 experts. Conference-servicing costs estimated for a biennial meeting of States ($362,600) and three sessions of a group of governmental experts ($715,100) are already provided within the proposed programme budget 2002-2003; no additional appropriation is required for those. Costs to service a Conference to be convened no later than 2006 cannot be estimated at this stage. The SG indicated these requirements cannot be absorbed within existing resources under section 4.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt A/C.1/56/L.47, a total provision of $499,900 would be required for the biennium 2002-2003, comprising $456,100 under section 4 (Disarmament) and $43,800 under section 32 (Staff assessment), the latter offset by income section 1. The requirement would be a charge against the contingency fund. Any additional office accommodation needs under section 27D would be considered in the first performance report for 2002-2003.

title: Comprehensive study of the question of nuclear-weapon-free zones in all its aspects : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/79/L.68/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Mandate: Convene up to 25 experts to prepare a comprehensive study on nuclear-weapon-free zones, with three meetings (total 3 weeks) and two 2-day informal consultative meetings in New York; study to be transmitted to GA at its eighty-first session. 2025 breakdown (excluding staff assessment): Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) $137,100 — interpretation in six languages ($79,500) for one 5-day session (10 meetings) and documentation ($57,600) for one pre-session document (20,000 words); Section 4 (Disarmament) $283,300 — other staff costs ($20,500, including a General Temporary Assistance position GS (Other level) to administer travel), experts’ travel ($159,100) and consultant support ($103,700); Section 29C (OICT) $20,200 — technical and conference services for one 5-day session (10 meetings) and one 2-day informal session (4 meetings). Conference-servicing total in 2025: $157,300; non-conference-servicing total: $283,300; combined (excl. staff assessment): $440,600. Staff assessment (Section 36) for 2025: $21,100, offset by income and not new money. The SG notes no provision exists in the 2025 proposed programme budget; resources cannot be absorbed and require an additional appropriation, potentially charged to the contingency fund. 2026 planning figures (not part of the 2025 request): Section 2 $302,800; Section 4 $457,400; Section 29C $34,700; Section 36 staff assessment $43,900; total including staff assessment $838,800.

SG statement for PBI of A/C.1/79/L.68/Rev.1. 2025 requested (excluding staff assessment): $440,600 broken down as Section 2 (DGACM) $137,100; Section 4 (Disarmament) $283,300; Section 29C (OICT) $20,200. Staff assessment (Section 36) $21,100 is offset by income. 2026 indicative requirements (not part of 2025 request): total $838,800 including $43,900 staff assessment. Mandate includes convening up to 25 experts (travel for experts and Chair), two 2-day informal consultations, consultant support, and one GTA GS (Other level) position to administer travel. No absorption possible in 2025; requires additional appropriation and is a potential charge against the contingency fund.

ACABQ recommends approval of $420,400 for 2025: $137,100 under section 2 (DGACM) and $283,300 under section 4 (Disarmament). The Secretary-General’s request totaled $440,600 ($157,300 conference-servicing + $283,300 non-conference-servicing). ACABQ recommends absorption within existing resources of $20,200 requested for OICT IT servicing, reducing the total by that amount. Section 2 (DGACM $137,100): one pre-session document (~20,000 words) in six languages and interpretation in six languages for one 5-day session (10 meetings) in New York in 2025. Section 4 (Disarmament $283,300): includes travel for up to 25 experts for meetings in New York (airfare/terminal $79,400 + DSA $75,000) and Chair travel for two 2-day informal meetings (airfare/terminal $3,100 + DSA $1,500); consultancy for eight months ($92,900) plus consultant travel/terminal ($10,800); and general temporary assistance ($20,500) for one GS (Other level) position to administer expert travel. OICT ($20,200) for IT servicing of one 5-day session (10 meetings) and one 2-day session (4 meetings) was recommended to be absorbed. An additional appropriation would be required for 2025 and would represent a potential charge against the contingency fund. Staff assessment of $21,100 (section 36) is offset by income and not new money.

Transfers to the new Office of Counter-Terrorism from DPA include the CTITF Office and the UNCCT with all associated regular and extrabudgetary resources: projected regular budget balance of $590,500 as at 30 June 2017 against the revised appropriation of $2,044,500, cash balance of $93,547,000 in the Trust Fund for Counter-Terrorism, and six regular budget posts (1 D-2, 1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 P-3, 2 GS (Other level)) plus 32 trust fund‑funded posts/positions. Section 1 breakdown: $162,900 for consultancy fees (one Eminent Person and one Special Assistant, P-4, up to six work-months each in 2018–2019, plus one month specialized forensic/document analysis consultant) and $83,173 for travel (Eminent Person and Special Assistant to New York, Africa, Western Europe; specialized consultant to South Africa). ACABQ recommends a 10 per cent reduction, approving $321,600 total, comprising $221,500 under section 1 and $100,100 under section 2, to be provided through an additional appropriation for 2018–2019 and charged to the contingency fund. Context: In 2016–2017 the GA approved $326,300 (resolution 71/260); actual expenditures were $180,050, with modalities changed from GTA posts (ASG Eminent Person and P-4 Programme Reporting Officer) to consultancy; specialized consultant engagement was not required due to lack of documentation from South Africa.
stages.budget_section: Section 1, Overall policymaking, direction and coordination

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decision adopted (A/C.5/72/L.15, sect. G) informing GA that, should it adopt draft resolution A/72/L.19, resources of $321,600 would be required in the 2018–2019 biennium as a charge against the contingency fund. Breakdown: Section 1 $221,500; Section 2 $100,100. No posts mentioned.
stages.budget_section: Section 1, Overall policymaking, direction and coordination; Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/50/L.17/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Secretary-General estimates additional resources of $320,300 for 1996 to support Central America peace process activities (staff costs $275,300; travel $45,000). Requires continuation of three temporary posts (1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 GS other level) for 12 months in 1996. Not absorbable within section 3; to be dealt with outside contingency fund procedures per para. 13. Staff assessment of $60,900 under section 32 is fully offset by income and not new money.
stages.budget_section: Section 3, Peace-keeping Operations and special missions

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that an appropriation not exceeding $320,300 would be required under section 3 for the biennium 1996-1997. An additional $60,900 under section 32 (Staff assessment) would be offset in full by income section 1. Appropriation to be considered in the context of the 1996-1997 proposed programme budget. Amount is stated as 'not exceeding'.

title: United Nations African Institute for the Prevention of Crime and the Treatment of Offenders : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/79/L.10/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.3/79/L.10/Rev.1 requests increasing the UN grant to UNAFRI to fully cover costs of five core Professional staff as from 2025. Additional requirement is $309,700 beyond the $154,500 already proposed in the 2025 budget, for a total grant of $464,200. Position costs (USD): Director General 122,700; Deputy Director General 101,900; Director of Training and Programs 84,700; Director of Research and Documentation 84,700; Administrative/Finance Officer 70,200. Costs are under grants and contributions of Section 18 and would recur 'for 2025 and beyond'. Salary levels referenced in A/79/247. These are Institute posts funded by a grant, not UN Secretariat posts.
stages.posts_levels: Director General, Deputy Director General, Director of Training and Programs, Director of Research and Documentation, Administrative/Finance Officer
stages.budget_section: Section 18, Economic and social development in Africa

stages.notes: SG statement (A/C.5/79/22) for draft resolution A/C.3/79/L.10/Rev.1 requests an additional appropriation of $309,700 in 2025 under grants and contributions of Section 18 to fully fund five core Professional staff at UNAFRI. Current grant proposed in the 2025 budget is $154,500; total annual grant required is $464,200. Resources are recurrent ("for 2025 onwards"), cannot be absorbed within Section 18, and would represent a potential charge against the contingency fund. Position titles listed (Director General, Deputy Director General, Director of Training and Programs, Director of Research and Documentation, Administrative/Finance Officer) with proposed annual costs per A/79/247; no new UN Secretariat posts are established.
stages.budget_section: Section 18, Economic and social development in Africa

stages.notes: ACABQ recommends that, if the General Assembly adopts draft resolution A/C.3/79/L.10/Rev.1, an additional recurrent appropriation of $309,700 be approved for 2025 under section 18, representing a potential charge against the contingency fund. The Secretary-General indicated this raises UNAFRI’s total annual grant to $464,200 going forward, to cover costs of five core Professional staff; the request was interpreted as a one-time adjustment to cover the current core staff complement, though the resources would be recurrent.
stages.budget_section: Section 18, Economic and social development in Africa

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.3/79/L.10/Rev.1, $309,700 would be required in 2025 under section 18; charge against the contingency fund.
stages.budget_section: Section 18, Economic and social development in Africa

title: Oceans and the law of the sea : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/58/L.19 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules and procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Additional requirements relate to servicing a three-day intergovernmental meeting (two meetings per day, total six) with interpretation in all six languages. Charge against the contingency fund is envisaged. Operative paras. 64(a) and 64(d) are already covered under Section 8 (Legal affairs); no additional appropriation required for those. Any extra costs from hosting in Reykjavik versus New York to be borne by the host Government. No provision existed in the proposed 2004-2005 budget; capacity for absorption to be considered by the Fifth Committee.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly affairs and conference services

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, if it adopts draft resolution A/58/L.19, $305,600 is required under section 2; this would be a charge against the contingency fund.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly affairs and conference services

title: Open-ended Working Group on Security of and in the Use of Information and Communications Technologies 2021-2025 established pursuant to General Assembly resolution 75/240 : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/78/L.13 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General requests an additional appropriation of $318,200 for 2024 (net of staff assessment), to be charged against the contingency fund. Breakdown for 2024: Section 4, Disarmament $190,500; Section 29C, OICT $127,700 (includes $7,700 conference videoconference services and $120,000 for portal development and maintenance). Non-post details for 2024 include: consultants $84,000; contractual services (S4) $1,800; supplies $200; furniture/equipment $2,300; OICT development $100,000 and annual maintenance $20,000. Conference servicing: 2024 $7,700 (and $7,700 in 2025) for videoconference services for up to 10 intersessional meeting days across 2024–2025 (no interpretation or documentation). One new P-3 Programme Officer post (regular) effective 1 Jan 2024 under Section 4. Ongoing costs anticipated for 2025 and subsequent years: Section 4 $270,100; Section 29C $27,700; plus staff assessment $17,800 (offset). No absorption possible within existing 2024 resources.

ACABQ recommends: (a) a 10% reduction ($8,400) to ODA consultancy resources and (b) absorption of $4,300 in non-post resources linked to the post establishment. Resulting recommended 2024 amounts: Section 4 (Disarmament) $177,800; Section 29C (OICT) $127,700; total $305,500 requiring an additional appropriation in 2024, charged to the contingency fund. Staff assessment of $9,900 (section 36) is fully offset by income. SG’s 2024 proposal included establishing one P-3 Programme Officer (directory manager) effective 1 Jan 2024; consultants for directory setup; and OICT costs for an online portal ($100,000 development, $20,000 annual maintenance) plus $7,700 for audiovisual support for intersessional meetings (no interpretation or documentation). Draft decision envisages up to 10 intersessional meeting days across 2024–2025 and directory manager functions beginning in 2024.

Fifth Committee decision indicates adoption of A/C.1/78/L.13 would require $305,500 in 2024, comprising $177,800 under Section 4 (Disarmament) and $127,700 under Section 29C (Office of Information and Communications Technology); charge against the contingency fund. Staff assessment of $9,900 under Section 36 to be offset by income section 1. Establishment of one P-3 post under Section 4 effective 1 January 2024. Decision A/C.5/78/L.17 was adopted without a vote.

title: High-Level Plenary Meeting on Addressing Large Movements of Refugees and Migrants : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/70/L.54 : statement submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Non-recurrent conference-servicing costs for the 19 September 2016 high-level meeting on large movements of refugees and migrants. Breakdown: $72,000 meetings services (interpretation), $186,000 verbatim services, $47,000 documentation (one document of 10,700 words in six languages). Two plenary meetings covered from existing entitlements; additions total 12 meetings with interpretation and 6 with verbatim records. Resources would be a charge against the contingency fund and require an additional appropriation for 2016-2017.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

title: Request for an advisory opinion of the International Court of Justice on the obligations of Israel in relation to the presence and activities of the United Nations, other international organizations and 3rd states : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/79/L.28 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General requests an additional appropriation of $298,900 in 2025 under Section 7 (ICJ) for temporary assistance (interpreters, translators, text-processing), documentation services, external translation, rental of equipment, security, supplies, and other services to support ICJ advisory proceedings. No absorption is possible within the 2025 proposed programme budget; the amount would represent a potential charge against the contingency fund. Additional future requirements linked to the same mandate are indicated for 2026 ($68,800) and 2027 ($95,400) and would be reflected in those years’ budgets. Section 36 staff assessment of $19,000 in 2025 is fully offset by income section 1 and is not new money. No new posts are established; support is via temporary assistance and contractual services.

Secretary-General requests an additional appropriation for 2025 of $298,900 under Section 7 for ICJ to support advisory opinion proceedings (temporary assistance for translators, interpreters, text-processing; security support; external translation and printing; rental of audiovisual equipment and press room setup; supplies). Staff assessment for 2025 is $19,000 under Section 36 and is fully offset. No capacity for absorption identified; resources would represent a potential charge against the contingency fund. Additional requirements projected for 2026 ($68,800) and 2027 ($95,400) to be reflected in future programme budgets.

ACABQ recommends approval of the full amount as an additional appropriation for 2025, representing a potential charge against the contingency fund. Breakdown (per SG statement): interpretation services $90,000; meeting services $68,600; external translation $80,000; other contractual services $4,500; rental of furniture and equipment $31,800; security services $20,000; supplies and materials $4,000. Staff assessment ($19,000) under section 36 to be fully offset by income from staff assessment.

Fifth Committee draft decision (A/C.5/79/L.3) informs GA that, if draft resolution A/79/L.28/Rev.1 is adopted, $298,900 for 2025 under section 7 (ICJ) would be required as a charge against the contingency fund. An additional $19,000 for section 36 (Staff assessment) is fully offset by income (no new appropriation). This reflects the Fifth Committee decision, pending final GA action.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/79/L.28/Rev.1, additional requirements of $298,900 would be required in 2025 under section 7 (ICJ), representing a charge against the contingency fund. An additional $19,000 under section 36 (Staff assessment) would be fully offset by income section 1. Amount is contingent on GA adoption of the draft resolution.

title: Treaty Banning the Production of Fissile Material for Nuclear Weapons or Other Nuclear Explosive Devices : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/71/L.65 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: One-time additional resources split across 2017 and 2018 for meetings and documentation of a high-level preparatory group on a fissile material cut-off treaty. 2017 requirements: $289,000 under section 2 and $6,900 under section 29F, to be charged to the contingency fund via additional appropriation; 2018 requirements ($295,900) to be included in the 2018-2019 proposed programme budget. No capacity to absorb within 2016-2017. No posts established. Involves meetings in Geneva (40) and informal consultative meetings in New York (8), with six-language interpretation and related documentation.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: One-time additional workload for meetings and documentation to support a high-level expert preparatory group on a fissile material cut-off treaty. Total additional resources across 2017–2018: $591,800. Annual breakdown: 2017 $295,900 and 2018 $295,900. Section 2 components: Interpretation $158,000 per year ($316,000 total); Pre-session documentation $36,300 per year ($72,600 total); In-session documentation $2,500 per year ($5,000 total); Post-session documentation $92,200 per year ($184,400 total). Section 29F (Administration, Geneva): Sound technician/recording $6,900 per year ($13,800 total). Activities include 40 meetings in Geneva (two sessions of 10 days each, 2017 and 2018) and two two-day informal consultative meetings in New York (2017 and 2018), with interpretation in six official languages. Outputs under Section 4, Disarmament, are amended to include servicing and documentation for these meetings. For 2017, the $295,900 would require an additional appropriation and represent a charge against the contingency fund; the 2018 $295,900 would be included in the proposed programme budget for 2018–2019.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: Secretary-General requests additional one-time resources tied to meetings and documentation for a high-level expert preparatory group on a fissile material cut-off treaty. First-year (2017) requirement: $295,900 comprised of Section 2 DGACM ($289,000: interpretation $158,000; pre-session documentation $36,300; in-session documentation $2,500; post-session documentation $92,200) and Section 29F Geneva ($6,900: sound technician/recording). An equal amount ($295,900) is required for 2018 to be included in the 2018-2019 proposed budget. Activities include two two-week sessions in Geneva (2017 and 2018) and two two-day informal consultative meetings in New York (2017 and 2018) with interpretation in six official languages. 2017 amount requires an additional appropriation and represents a charge against the contingency fund. Related programme sections mentioned: Section 4, Disarmament (outputs amended), and Section 29F, Administration, Geneva (support services).
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: SG estimated total additional resources of $591,800 for 2017-2018 ($295,900 per year), comprising: meetings services $164,900 per year (including $6,900 per year for sound technicians) and documentation $131,000 per year. ACABQ recommended against $6,900 per year for sound technicians and thus recommended $289,000 for 2017 as an additional appropriation under section 2, to be charged against the contingency fund for 2016-2017. The SG indicated a further $295,900 for 2018 to be included in the proposed programme budget for 2018-2019. Activities relate to sections 2, 4 (Disarmament) and 29F (Administration, Geneva). The mandate spans 2017 and 2018 (two sessions), so it is time-bound, not ongoing.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decision informs the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.1/71/L.65/Rev.1, resources of $289,000 would be required under the 2016-2017 programme budget, representing a charge against the contingency fund. Amount pertains to the biennium; specific year split not provided.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/44/L.12 and draft decision A/44/L.13 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: SG estimates additional requirements of $288,000 in 1990 under: Section 1 ($195,800) for travel of representatives of least developed countries per GA res. 41/213; Section 20 ($13,200) for travel and DSA for five staff from the United Nations Office at Vienna to attend the special session; Section 27 ($79,000) for public information (media kit $59,000 and post-session booklet $20,000). Conference-servicing full costs estimated at $672,700 (including $489,300 for the four-day special session) are expected to be absorbed within existing capacity under Section 29; no additional resources requested under Section 29 for 1990-1991. The two-day Preparatory Committee session in December 1989 would require an exception to GA res. 40/243 and be serviced from existing resources without impeding GA work. If contingency fund cannot finance these additional requirements, SG indicates potential suspension of the annual International Day against Drug Abuse public information event (Section 27) and that postponement of the special session may be required.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, should it adopt draft resolution A/44/L.12 and draft decision A/44/L.13, additional resource requirements would arise totaling $288,000, broken down as: $195,800 under Section 1 (Overall policy-making, direction and coordination); $13,200 under Section 20 (International drug control); and $79,000 under Section 27 (Public information). These constitute additional expenditures not provided for in the proposed programme budget for the biennium 1990-1991 and are to be dealt with in accordance with the contingency fund guidelines (GA res. 42/211). If it is not possible to meet these from the contingency fund, no redeployment or modification of activities is envisaged, except in respect of Section 27; postponement of the special session may be required. Holding the first session of the preparatory committee in December 1989 would be an exception to GA res. 40/243 (Section I). Any additional appropriations to be requested will be included in a consolidated statement to be submitted towards the end of the current session per res. 42/211, annex, section C, paras. 5-6.
stages.budget_section: Section 1, Overall policy-making, direction and coordination

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/47/L.49 : report of the 5th Committee : General Assembly, 47th session.

stages.notes: Request relates to strengthening the interim secretariat of the Framework Convention on Climate Change for 1993. The $738,000 consists of $379,000 for general temporary assistance to finance seven posts (one D-2, one D-1, one P-4, and four General Service) for six work-months each, plus $359,000 in non-staff resources (consultants $48,000; overtime $5,000; expert groups $35,000; staff travel $150,000; external printing $5,000; communications $40,000; hospitality $6,000; miscellaneous $2,000; supplies and materials $48,000; furniture and equipment $10,000; EDP equipment $10,000). Interim secretariat total staffing plan is five Professional (D-2, D-1, P-4, P-3, P-2) and four General Service posts; two posts (P-3 and P-2) are available for temporary redeployment and are not included in the GTA financing. Conference servicing for two assumed 10-day sessions in 1993 is estimated at $990,000 at full cost, but no additional resources are requested under section 41 because it is expected to be absorbed within permanent capacity. If contingency fund resources are not available, activities would be postponed per resolutions 41/213 and 42/211. Participation of developing countries is financed from a voluntary trust fund; several staff continue to be funded from extrabudgetary sources (WMO and bilateral). The interim secretariat would be located in Geneva and placed within the forthcoming Department for Policy Coordination and Sustainable Development; redeployment proposals will be presented in revised estimates early next year.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/47/L.49, an additional appropriation of $279,250 would be required under section 11 F for the 1992-1993 biennium, subject to contingency fund guidelines, with total requirements to be reviewed in revised estimates at the resumed 47th session. An additional $59,600 under section 36 (Staff assessment) would be fully offset by income. ACABQ report was presented orally.

title: Situation of human rights in Myanmar : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/56/L.55 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Covers 1 Jan–31 Dec 2002 for the Secretary-General’s good offices on Myanmar via the Special Envoy. Breakdown: personnel $154,100 (Special Envoy at USG level on WAE basis for up to 270 days), travel $122,000 (approx. 26 two-day trips), operating costs $1,800. Substantive/administrative support provided by DPA within existing proposed 2002-2003 resources. Requirements to be charged against the $93,691,600 provision for special political missions under Section 3 in the proposed programme budget 2002-2003.

Costs for one-year period (1 Jan–31 Dec 2002) to support the Secretary-General’s good offices via the Special Envoy for Myanmar. Breakdown: $154,100 for WAE salary of Special Envoy (USG level) up to 270 days; $122,000 for travel (approx. 26 trips, avg. 2 days; airfare, DSA, terminal); $1,800 for miscellaneous services (telephone, fax). To be charged against the provision of $93,691,600 for special political missions under Section 3 in the proposed programme budget 2002-2003. Substantive/administrative support from DPA within existing resources. Draft resolution referenced: A/C.3/56/L.55.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.3/56/L.55, $277,900 would be charged against the provision for special political missions under Section 3 for the 2002-2003 biennium. Charge is against the existing SPM provision rather than a new appropriation.

The amount would require an additional appropriation for 2021 and constitute a charge against the contingency fund. Resource needs for 2022–2025 to be included in future budget proposals.

Planned sessions: 2021 one 2-day organizational (4 meetings) and one 5-day substantive (10 meetings); 2022–2023 two 5-day substantive sessions each year; 2024–2025 three 5-day substantive sessions each year. Other affected sections: Section 4 (Disarmament) and Section 28 (Global communications).

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, should it adopt A/C.1/75/L.8/Rev.1, additional resources of $269,900 for 2021 would be required, representing a charge against the contingency fund. Breakdown: $241,800 under Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management), $25,100 under Section 4 (Disarmament), and $3,000 under Section 28 (Global communications). Decision adopted as part of A/C.5/75/L.15, section C.

title: Cooperation between the United Nations and the League of Arab States : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/73/L.23 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Additional appropriation of $287,400 (including $20,300 staff assessment) requested for 2019; charge against the contingency fund. Staff assessment is offset by income section 1. No potential for absorption identified.

SG statement breakdown under section 3: posts (1 P-5 Senior Political Affairs Officer to manage the liaison office and develop a stakeholder network; 1 Administrative Assistant for administrative support), official travel $29,700, general operating expenses $62,600, supplies $700, furniture and equipment $22,800. Logistical support to be provided by UNDP Cairo on a cost-reimbursable basis. The SG indicated no capacity to absorb within existing provisions.

Fifth Committee decision indicating establishment of 2 posts (1 P-5 and 1 Local level) effective 1 January 2019 and additional resources of $267,100 under Section 3 for the 2018–2019 biennium. title: A global call for concrete action for the total elimination of racism, racial discrimination, xenophobia and related intolerance and the comprehensive implementation of and follow-up to the Durban Declaration and Programme of Action : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/75/L.50/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General requests additional appropriations of $262,300 for 2021 (non-recurrent) under sections 24 ($23,300 for travel of four mandate holders) and 28 ($239,000 for webcasting and information campaign). Staff assessment of $600 under section 36 is offset by income. Conference-servicing costs under section 2 cannot be estimated at this time and would be submitted upon decision on modalities. Charge against the contingency fund per resolution 72/266 A. Activities under the public information campaign complement OHCHR requirements already integrated in A/75/588.

Non-recurrent 2021 costs tied to a one-day high-level meeting and a public information campaign for the 20th anniversary of the Durban Declaration. Section 2 (conference servicing) costs cannot be estimated pending decisions on modalities for the Permanent Forum and the high-level meeting. Breakdown: Section 24 Human rights: $23,300 for travel/DSA of four mandate holders. Section 28 Global communications: $239,000 comprising webcasting ($6,400 other staff costs + $2,100 contractual) and information campaign ($200,500 printing/web services + $30,000 supplies/seed funding to UNICs). Staff assessment of $600 (Section 36) is offset and not counted in new appropriations. Charge against the contingency fund per para. 17.

ACABQ recommends approval of $262,300 in 2021 as an additional appropriation, to be charged against the contingency fund, comprising $23,300 under section 24 (Human rights) and $239,000 under section 28 (Global communications). An additional $600 under section 36 (Staff assessment) is to be offset by income from staff assessment. Requirements are non-recurrent and relate to a high-level meeting and a public information campaign (webcasting, multimedia content, website, social media, and seed funds for UN information centres). Conference-servicing costs for the Permanent Forum and the high-level meeting are not estimated at this stage and would be submitted once modalities are decided. The SG indicated no provisions exist in the 2021 budget and no activities could be absorbed/curtailed to cover these costs.

Breakdown: $23,300 under section 24 (Human rights) and $239,000 under section 28 (Global communications). Charge against the contingency fund. Conditional upon GA adopting draft resolution A/C.3/75/L.50/Rev.1. A proposed amendment to set figures to zero was rejected; the draft decision was adopted without a vote, with the US and Israel disassociating.

title: Situation of human rights in Myanmar : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/60/L.53 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Request covers one-year (1 Jan–31 Dec 2006) continuation of the Secretary-General’s good offices on Myanmar via the Special Envoy. Net: $210,400; Gross: $254,000. Costs include salaries for the Special Envoy (when-actually-employed) and one local staff member, official travel (Myanmar, region, Europe, North America including UNHQ), one consultant, and miscellaneous services; DPA provides substantive/administrative support. Technical assistance, if requested, would be accommodated within OHCHR technical cooperation; Special Rapporteur mandate is perennial and already budgeted. Requirements would be charged against the $355,949,300 provision for special political missions under Section 3 (Political affairs) of the proposed programme budget for 2006-2007.

Estimated resources cover: Special Envoy (when-actually-employed), one local staff member, official travel (Myanmar, region, Europe, North America incl. UNHQ), one consultant, and miscellaneous services; substantive/administrative support provided by DPA. Technical assistance, if requested, to be accommodated within OHCHR technical cooperation; Special Rapporteur activities are perennial and already budgeted. ACABQ indicates that, should the draft resolution be adopted, up to $254,000 gross ($210,400 net) is required for 1 Jan–31 Dec 2006 to continue the Secretary-General’s good offices in Myanmar, to be charged against the existing provision of $355,949,300 for special political missions under Section 3 (no additional appropriation). Technical assistance to the Government to be accommodated within OHCHR technical cooperation; assistance to the Special Rapporteur requires no additional resources as provision already exists. Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.3/60/L.53, up to $254,000 gross ($210,400 net) would be required for the period 1 January to 31 December 2006 for the continuation of the good offices of the Secretary-General relating to the situation in Myanmar. title: Permanent memorial to and remembrance of the victims of slavery and the transatlantic slave trade : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/65/L.36 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Charge against the contingency fund for 2010-2011. Activities to facilitate erection and maintenance of the permanent memorial proceed only with sufficient extrabudgetary funding; long-term running costs to be borne by voluntary contributions. Ongoing biennial requirements estimated at $503,000 gross ($251,500 per annum). Summary (para. 13) breaks down $251,500 total as $239,400 under Section 27 and $12,100 under Section 36 (staff assessment, offset). Earlier text (para. 8) refers to $251,500 under Section 27; treat $251,500 as gross total with $12,100 staff assessment offset.

ACABQ recommends informing GA that, should A/65/L.36 be adopted, additional resources of $251,500 would be required, comprising $239,400 under section 27 (Public information) and $12,100 under section 36 (Staff assessment), to be offset by the same amount under income section 1 (Income from staff assessment). Charge against the contingency fund per GA resolutions 41/213 and 42/211. SG’s costing includes GTA for one Professional and one General Service staff for five months ($72,900); contractual services for a cultural event/concert ($20,000); technical requirements for student videoconference and teachers’ conference ($18,000); film screenings ($1,500); travel of panellists; and educational outreach ($15,000). No absorption possible within section 27 for 2010-2011. Activities are annual and recurring; estimated at $251,500 per annum for 2012-2013 and thereafter. Construction and long-term maintenance of the permanent memorial at UNHQ to be funded entirely from extrabudgetary voluntary contributions (no regular budget charge).

Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, should it adopt draft resolution A/65/L.36, additional resources of $251,500 would be required for the 2010-2011 biennium: $239,400 under section 27 (Public information) and $12,100 under section 36 (Staff assessment), the latter to be fully offset under income section I. This would be a charge against the contingency fund and would require appropriation for the biennium.

title: Future operations of the International Research and Training Institute for the Advancement of Women : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.36 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rule of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Conditional request: Up to $234,700 from the regular budget (charge against the contingency fund) may be required only if insufficient voluntary contributions are received before September 2004 to maintain INSTRAW operations through 31 December 2004. Total 2004 requirements estimated at $970,300 (posts: $556,000 for 9 existing posts [1 D-2, 1 P-3, 2 P-2/1, 5 GS]; substantive activities: $211,000; administrative: $203,300). Projected INSTRAW Trust Fund balance as of 31 Dec 2003: $735,600, expected to cover operations through September 2004. The 2004-2005 programme budget provides no resources for INSTRAW operations in 2004; any allocation would require an additional appropriation under the contingency fund. Consideration of the budgetary paragraph in Third Committee was noted as potentially contravening GA resolution 45/248 B.

Draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.36 seeks complementary funds, if needed, to ensure INSTRAW can operate for 1 Jan–31 Dec 2004 and prepare the report requested in GA resolution 57/311. Total 2004 operating requirements are estimated at $970,300, comprising $556,000 for nine current posts (1 D-2, 1 P-3, 2 P-2/P-1, and 5 GS), $211,000 for substantive activities (including GTA, consultancy, travel), and $203,300 for administrative costs (GOE, supplies, equipment). The INSTRAW Trust Fund balance projected at $735,600 as at 31 Dec 2003 is expected to allow operations through September 2004; therefore, an additional $234,700 may be required from the regular budget to maintain operations to end-2004 if voluntary contributions are insufficient. The 2004-2005 programme budget provides no resources for INSTRAW operations in 2004; any amount approved would be an additional appropriation, charged to the contingency fund (GA resolution 41/213) and considered by the Fifth Committee in the consolidated statement of potential charges. The SG notes the Third Committee’s consideration of an administrative/budgetary paragraph may contravene GA resolution 45/248 B.

SG estimates INSTRAW 2004 costs at $970,300; projected INSTRAW Trust Fund balance of $735,600 as at 31 Dec 2003 would cover operations through end-September 2004; therefore an additional $234,700 would be required to maintain operations to end-2004 if no further voluntary contributions are received. ACABQ advises that, should A/C.3/58/L.36 be adopted, there is a potential additional requirement of up to $234,700 over and above proposed regular budget resources under Section 9, contingent upon the availability of additional voluntary contributions, and recommends reconsideration at a resumed session due to uncertainty (including staffing/Director appointment). Background: $500,000 had been approved in 2003 from the contingency fund toward INSTRAW core activities, contributing to the $735,600 projected balance.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, should it adopt A/C.3/58/L.36, $234,700 would be set aside in the contingency fund for the 2004-2005 biennium to provide INSTRAW with complementary funds, if needed. The SG was requested to indicate at the second resumed 58th session whether these resources would be required and to report at the 59th session on the Institute’s overall financial situation. This is a contingency fund set-aside, not an immediate charge; GA plenary approval of the draft resolution is still required.

The $233,800 requires an additional appropriation for 2024 and constitutes a potential charge against the contingency fund. title: Matters relating to activities under the United Nations Programme on Space Applications in 2016 : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.4/70/L.9/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: SG estimates additional resources of $197,800 for 2016 to implement four workshops/symposia under the United Nations Programme on Space Applications. Breakdown: $17,800 for travel of one staff member (managing and logistical support across four activities) and $180,000 for grants and contributions (participant travel). No provision exists in the proposed 2016-2017 budget to absorb these costs; adoption would entail an additional appropriation charged to the contingency fund. Outputs in section 6.19(c)(ii) increase from 11 to 15 for 2016.
stages.budget_section: Section 6, Peaceful uses of outer space

stages.notes: Additional resources requested for 2016 total $197,800 under Section 6 to implement four activities (Costa Rica Human Space Technology workshop; South Africa Basic Space Technology symposium; Kenya Space Technology for Wildlife/Biodiversity workshop; Iran (Islamic Republic of) Dust Storm and Drought Monitoring workshop). Breakdown: Travel of staff $17,800; Grants and contributions (participants’ travel) $180,000. No provisions exist in the proposed 2016-2017 budget to cover these requirements; would represent a charge against the contingency fund. An underexpenditure of the same amount occurred in 2014-2015 and is to offset the estimated requirements in the second performance report. Outputs rise from 11 to 15 for 2016.
stages.budget_section: Section 6, Peaceful uses of outer space

stages.notes: Activities in 2016 comprise four events: Costa Rica (Human Space Technology), South Africa (Basic Space Technology), Kenya (Space Technology for Wildlife/Biodiversity), and Iran (Dust Storm/Drought Monitoring). SG breakdown: $17,800 for staff travel (one OOSA staff member to each host country; air ticket, 6 days DSA, terminal), and $180,000 for grants and contributions ($45,000 per event to fund travel for ~22 participants at ~$2,100 each; up to 25 participants if in-kind/extrabudgetary support allows). Expected host/extrabudgetary support includes ~$20,000 total for Costa Rica, South Africa and Kenya (~$6,600 each) and potential reprogramming of a 2015 Kenya workshop contribution. Events programmed in English only; any interpretation costs to be borne by host Governments. ACABQ notes that underexpenditures from 2014-2015 cannot be carried forward; language in the SG statement and draft resolution suggesting use of 2015 funds is inconsistent with UN Financial Regulations and Rules. If the draft resolution is adopted, the $197,800 would require an additional appropriation for 2016-2017 and represent a charge against the contingency fund.
stages.budget_section: Section 6, Peaceful uses of outer space

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.4/70/L.9/Rev.1, resources of $197,800 would be required under section 6 of the proposed programme budget for 2016-2017, representing a charge against the contingency fund.
stages.budget_section: Section 6, Peaceful uses of outer space

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/60/L.34 and its amendment (A/60/L.35) : statement submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the  rules of procedure of the General Assembly : 27th report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

stages.notes: ACABQ reviewed SG statement A/C.5/60/20. SG estimated $196,200 under Section 27 (production costs and general temporary assistance) for the 2006-2007 biennium to establish the outreach programme “The Rwanda Genocide and the United Nations.” ACABQ notes significant existing resources in Section 27 ($172.8 million; 747 posts; $4.7m GTA; ~$1m overtime/PSCs) and recommends no additional appropriation at this time; any requirement, if it materializes, should be reported in the performance report for 2006-2007.

Fifth Committee decision informs the GA that, should it adopt draft resolution A/60/L.34 and amendment A/60/L.35, additional resources of $196,200 would be required under Section 27 (Public information) for the 2006-2007 biennium. Appropriations would be considered under contingency fund procedures. The document does not record a final GA appropriation decision or any posts created.

title: Strengthening and enhancing the effective functioning of the human rights treaty body system : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/68/L.37 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Net additional appropriation of $194,300 for 2015 results from increases in sections 24, 28 and 29F offset by savings in section 2 due to word and language limits. Posts proposed include: 10 P-3 in OHCHR regional offices; 1 P-4, 4 P-3, 1 GS for capacity-building/training/roster/best practices; 1 P-3 and 1 P-2 for SPT; 16 P-3 and 1 GS for treaty body session support; 2 P-3 Press Officers (UNIS); 1 GS videoconference technician (UNOG). Temporary needs under the 15% ‘margin’ are to be covered by GTA (3 P-3 and 0.8 GS) and are not established posts. For 2016-2017, indicative net requirements are $1,293,700; staff assessment is fully offset and not new money. Charge against the contingency fund in 2014-2015 is $194,300.

Fifth Committee decision for biennium 2014-2015: net additional resources of $194,300, derived from increases under section 24, Human rights ($9,855,200); section 28, Public information ($327,400); and section 29F, Administration, Geneva ($317,100), partially offset by a net decrease under section 2, GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management ($10,305,400). Staff assessment of $751,200 (income offset) under section 36. Charge against the contingency fund. Establishes 38 posts (1 P-4, 33 P-3, 1 P-2, 3 GS (Other level)) effective 1 January 2015. Applies if GA adopts draft resolution A/68/L.37.

title: Countering the use of information and communications technologies for criminal purposes : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/74/L.11/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Resources requested for 2020 to organize a three-day organizational session (Aug 2020, New York, six languages). Section 2: $138,300 (interpretation/conference servicing $50,400; pre-session documentation $54,000; post-session documentation $33,900). Section 16: $55,000 (general temporary assistance $34,000 for four months at GS level; travel of three UNODC staff from Vienna to New York $21,000). Staff assessment $4,400 (section 36) is offset by income and not new money. No absorption possible; requires additional appropriation and charge against the contingency fund. Travel support for representatives of developing countries to be funded from extrabudgetary contributions. Future meetings in 2021–2022 anticipated but modalities and costs to be determined.
stages.budget_section: Section 16, International drug control, crime and terrorism prevention and criminal justice

stages.notes: SG statement (A/C.5/74/12) for draft resolution A/C.3/74/L.11/Rev.1 requests an additional appropriation of $193,300 for 2020, chargeable to the contingency fund: $138,300 under Section 2 (conference servicing and documentation) and $55,000 under Section 16 (general temporary assistance for 4 months at GS level and travel of 3 UNODC staff from Vienna to New York). An additional $4,400 under Section 36 (staff assessment) is fully offset by income and not part of new appropriations. UNODC to provide substantive and administrative servicing for the organizational session in August 2020; conference servicing includes interpretation in 6 languages and specified documentation volumes. Travel of representatives of developing countries is to be funded from extrabudgetary (voluntary) contributions. Future meetings in 2021–2022 may incur costs, but modalities and related estimates are not yet determined.
stages.budget_section: Section 16, International drug control, crime and terrorism prevention and criminal justice

stages.notes: SG requested $193,300 net (excluding $4,400 staff assessment) for 2020: $138,300 under section 2 (interpretation, conference servicing, pre-/post-session documentation) and $55,000 under section 16 (GTA for four months GS administrative support; travel of three UNODC staff from Vienna to New York). ACABQ determined the $138,300 under section 2 could be fully absorbed within the 2020 programme budget (August low peak). ACABQ recommends an additional appropriation of $59,400 charged to the contingency fund, comprising $55,000 under section 16 and $4,400 staff assessment (to be offset under income section 1). The activities had no prior provision in the proposed 2020 budget. Donor support is invited for developing countries’ participation, outside the regular budget.
stages.budget_section: Section 16, International drug control, crime and terrorism prevention and criminal justice

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the GA that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.3/74/L.11/Rev.1, additional resources for 2020 would be required: $138,300 under section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) and $55,000 under section 16 (International drug control, crime and terrorism prevention and criminal justice). The document also cites $4,400 under section 36 (Staff assessment), which is fully offset and therefore excluded from the main total above. The Fifth Committee adopted draft decision A/C.5/74/L.17 without a vote.

title: The situation in Central America :#procedures for the establishment of a firm and lasting peace and progress in fashioning a region of peace, freedom, democracy and development :#programme budget implications of draft resolution A/55/L.42 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General states that, if A/55/L.42 is adopted, an additional appropriation of $191,800 is required under Section 3 for 2001 to continue two staff (1 P-4 Political Affairs Officer and 1 GS) supporting Central America peace-related activities, including backstopping MINUGUA and follow-up in El Salvador. The amount covers salaries and common staff costs. Existing provision for special political missions in Section 3 would be fully utilized by other approved charges; therefore this would be an additional appropriation.

SG requested continuation in 2001 of one P-4 Political Affairs Officer and one General Service post to support post-conflict peace-building in Central America, including monitoring the Guatemala peace process, support to MINUGUA, and good offices/verification related to El Salvador. Estimated salary and common staff costs for these two posts: $191,800. ACABQ recommends approving an additional appropriation of $191,800 under Section 3. Context: GA previously approved $90,387,200 for special political missions; $66,074,600 utilized with $24,312,600 unallocated. If $17,069,400 (good offices/preventive diplomacy/post-conflict PB missions per A/55/645) and $7,243,200 (partial MINUGUA 2001 per A/C.5/55/24) are charged, the SPM provision would be fully utilized, hence the need for an additional appropriation.

Salary/common staff costs for 2003 total $202,800 net ($244,200 gross), but after applying the expected 2002 unencumbered balance of $19,900 from a $188,500 2002 appropriation, the additional requirements for 1 Jan–31 Dec 2003 are $182,900 net ($224,300 gross). Staff assessment of $41,400 under section 32 is fully offset by income section 1. The special political missions appropriation under section 3 ($98,338,700 per GA resolution 56/254 A) is fully allocated; therefore, these additional requirements must be treated under the procedures in paragraph 11 of annex I to GA resolution 41/213.

The $182,900 reflects $202,800 net 2003 salary/common staff costs less an estimated $19,900 unencumbered balance from 2002 appropriations.

title: The situation of human rights in Myanmar : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/57/L.48 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Estimated total cost for 1 Jan–31 Dec 2003: $307,400 net ($372,600 gross), comprising personnel ($170,900), travel ($127,200 for ~30 trips averaging 2.5 days), and operating costs ($9,300). After applying an expected unencumbered balance from 2002 of $143,500 (from a 2002 appropriation of $277,900), the additional provisions requested for 2003 are $163,900 net ($229,100 gross). Staff assessment of $65,200 under section 32 is fully offset by income and not counted as new money. Substantive and administrative support will be provided by DPA. Special political missions appropriation under section 3 for 2002-2003 ($98,338,700) is fully allocated; therefore, the additional requirements must be treated under paragraph 11 of annex I to GA resolution 41/213 (additional appropriations procedure). The Special Envoy (USG level) is engaged on a when-actually-employed basis for up to 270 days in 2003; staffing support consists of one P-3 Political Affairs and one local staff.

Related to Third Committee draft resolution A/C.3/57/L.48 on the situation of human rights in Myanmar. One-year period 1 Jan–31 Dec 2003. Total estimated 2003 cost: $307,400 net ($372,600 gross). After taking into account an estimated 2002 unencumbered balance of $143,500 from an appropriation of $277,900, the additional requirement for 2003 is $163,900 net ($229,100 gross). Personnel: Special Envoy at USG level on a when-actually-employed basis for up to 270 days; two support staff (one P-3 Political Affairs Officer and one local staff). Travel: $127,200 for approximately 30 trips averaging 2.5 days. Operating costs: $9,300 (miscellaneous services). Additional provision of $163,900 would be required under section 3; staff assessment of $65,200 under section 32 is to be fully offset by income section 1. The $98,338,700 appropriated for special political missions under section 3 for 2002-2003 is fully allocated; therefore, additional appropriations would be required under the procedures of paragraph 11 of annex I to GA resolution 41/213.
stages.posts_levels: USG (Special Envoy, WAE), P-3 Political Affairs Officer, Local staff

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/46/L.30 : report of the 5th Committee : General Assembly, 46th session.

stages.notes: Request pertains to part A of draft resolution A/46/L.30 supporting the SG’s peace efforts in Central America. Full-cost net requirements for 1992 total $761,500, broken down as: Consultants $278,400; Temporary posts $186,400; Common staff costs $71,800; Travel of staff $219,900; Maintenance of office automation equipment $800; Acquisition of office automation equipment $4,200. Establishment of two Professional temporary posts (P-5 and P-4) to support negotiations; frequent travel and consulting services envisaged. The SG indicates these requirements cannot be absorbed within section 2 and should be treated outside the Contingency Fund as extraordinary expenses related to peace and security. Prior related expenditures of $964,400 were incurred in 1990-1991 under the unforeseen and extraordinary expenses authority. Staff assessment of $54,800 (section 36) is fully offset by income and not additional net resources.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/46/L.30, an additional appropriation of $163,300 is required under section 2 for the biennium 1992-1993. The Committee further decided these additional requirements should be provided without recourse to the contingency fund. Staff assessment of $35,000 under section 36 is fully offset by income section 1. Pending adoption of A/46/L.30 by the Plenary.

stages.notes: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/47/L.34. title: Revitalization of the work of the General Assembly : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/59/L.69 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/59/L.69 requests strengthening the Office of the PGA, space, protocol services, an ad hoc GA working group, consolidated rules of procedure issuance, and consideration of optical scanners. The SG indicates: (a) $133,000 in general temporary assistance (GTA) for the remainder of 2004-2005 to support senior-level capacity in the Office of the PGA (linked to GTA for three positions: 1 D-2, 1 D-1, 1 GS (Other level)); (b) $875,800 GTA for the biennium 2006-2007 (first-year split not provided) for the same Office support; (c) $138,100 for a new P-3 post dedicated to protocol services for the PGA; (d) $60,300 GTA for issuing the consolidated GA rules of procedure (3 work-months Professional and 2 work-months GS). The $198,400 (=$138,100 + $60,300) for protocol and rules issuance is in addition to the initial 2006-2007 budget proposals; $875,800 GTA for 2006-2007 is already reflected in initial proposals. No additional requirements are foreseen at this stage for office/conference space, the ad hoc working group (conference servicing within existing entitlements), or optical scanners (no immediate costs). The SG notes that despite the resolution’s call to provide resources from within existing resources, no existing capacity is identified; the Assembly should consider this in reviewing the 2006-2007 proposed budget (A/60/6 (Sect.1), para. 1.13). The para. 8 reference to 'paragraph 4' pertains to the rules issuance in paragraph 14.
stages.budget_section: Section 1, Overall policymaking, direction and coordination; Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

stages.notes: Breakdown of estimated additional requirements: 2004-2005 biennium: $116,300 under Section 1 for general temporary assistance (GTA) to support two further additional positions (1 D-2 and 1 P-5) in the Office of the President of the General Assembly (PGA). 2006-2007 biennium: total $1,021,700 comprising Section 1: $823,300 for GTA for the same D-2 and P-5 positions in the PGA Office, plus $138,100 for a new P-3 post in Protocol and Liaison Service dedicated to the PGA; Section 2: $60,300 for issuance of the GA rules of procedure (GTA equivalent to 3 work-months Professional and 2 work-months General Service). The resolution requested funding 'from within existing resources' for para. 3(b), but the Secretary-General states no existing capacity is available and cites GA res. 54/249 regarding accurate reporting of available resources. Space for the PGA (para. 3(c)) will be accommodated without immediate cost; if not feasible, proposals will be made later. The ad hoc working group (para. 4) will be serviced within existing conference entitlements; no simultaneous meetings are assumed and no new costs arise. Optical scanners for secret ballot counting (para. 15) have no immediate financial implications pending further GA consideration. A new P-3 post for protocol services is proposed (ongoing) starting in the next biennium; the two PGA Office positions are to be filled annually via GTA rather than established posts. The $1,021,700 figure is for the 2006-2007 biennium (not a single-year amount) and is excluded from the 'original_request' first-year capture per extraction rules.
stages.budget_section: Section 1, Overall policymaking, direction and coordination

stages.notes: Context: ACABQ review of SG’s PBI for draft resolution A/59/L.69/Rev.1 (Office of the President of the General Assembly and related services). For 2004-2005, ACABQ recommends informing GA that up to $116,300 would be required under Section 1; SG would seek to absorb within the biennial appropriation and report actual costs in the second performance report. For 2006-2007, adoption would give rise to: up to $961,400 under Section 1 (breakdown: $823,300 for Office of the PGA to fund D-2 and P-5 via general temporary assistance; $138,100 for Protocol and Liaison Service for a proposed P-3), and up to $60,300 under Section 2 (DGACM) for consolidating the GA rules of procedure (GTA equivalent to 3 work-months Professional + 2 work-months GS). ACABQ remarks: (a) ‘Management and senior’ levels do not necessarily mean D-2 and P-5; justification should be provided during 2006-2007 budget consideration; (b) For the Protocol P-3, ACABQ was not convinced a dedicated established post is required at this time; (c) For the $60,300 DGACM cost, every effort should be made to absorb within existing resources. The resolution text sought provision ‘from within existing resources’; SG indicated no current capacity to absorb. Any support for the Office of the PGA should be subject to review after an appropriate period of time. Primary sections affected: Section 1 (OPPDC) and Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management).
stages.budget_section: Section 1, Overall policy-making, direction and coordination

stages.notes: SG statement (A/C.1/79/L.84) requests an additional appropriation of $61,400 for 2025 (Section 4: $9,500; Section 28: $5,900; Section 29C: $46,000). No absorption possible in 2025; charge against the contingency fund. Multi-year activities continue: in 2026, conference servicing/documentation and communications/ICT costs recur; in 2027, large documentation costs are foreseen. Secretariat flags constraints on servicing virtual/hybrid meetings (no remote simultaneous interpretation mandate; legal/technical parameters required per GA res. 77/262). No posts are created.

SG statement for draft resolution A/C.1/79/L.39 requests an additional appropriation of $61,400 for 2025 (potential charge against the contingency fund). Breakdown for 2025: Section 4 Disarmament $9,500 (Experts/travel for Panel Chair), Section 28 Global communications $5,900 (webcasting), Section 29C OICT $46,000 (videoconference services). No staff posts are established; the 21 Panel members serve in personal capacity. Multi-year activities include consultations up to 10 days in 2025 and 10 days in 2026, documentation in 2026 (approx. 15,000 words) and 2027 (approx. 40,000 words). Additional later-year requirements (not part of 2025 request) include Section 2 documentation: $43,200 (2026) and $115,100 (2027); webcasting $5,900 (2026); videoconference services $46,000 (2026); staff assessment $5,200 (2026) and $13,800 (2027). Secretariat notes limitations and mandate requirements for servicing virtual/hybrid meetings; absorption in 2025 is not possible.

SG statement (A/C.5/79/16) requested additional 2025 resources totaling $61,400: $9,500 for ODA (expert/Chair travel), $5,900 for DGC (webcasting), and $46,000 for OICT (videoconferencing). This comprises $51,900 conference-servicing and $9,500 non-conference-servicing. ACABQ recommends against these new appropriations and that the Secretariat absorb the requirements within the 2025 proposed programme budget; if absorption is not possible, related expenditures should be reflected in the 2025 financial performance report. Activities include up to 10 meeting days in 2025 and a further 10 days in 2026 for the Panel; briefings at GA 80th and 81st sessions and final report at the 82nd session. Other affected sections: Section 28, Global communications (webcasting) and Section 29C, OICT (videoconferencing).

Fifth Committee decision: should the General Assembly adopt draft resolution A/C.1/79/L.39, every effort should be made to absorb the additional resource requirements within the 2025 budget; if absorption is not possible, any additional expenditures should be reported in the 2025 financial performance report. Breakdown: $9,500 for experts under Section 4 (Disarmament); $5,900 for contractual services under Section 28 (Global communications); $46,000 under Section 29C (Office of Information and Communications Technology).

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/SPC/43/L.26 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: The draft resolution concerns continued servicing of the Special Committee to Investigate Israeli Practices Affecting the Human Rights of the Population of the Occupied Territories. Activities fall under the Centre for Human Rights, subprogramme 1 (Implementation of international standards, instruments and procedures), programme element 1.3. The Secretary-General indicates this is a perennial activity already provided for in the 1988-1989 programme budget; therefore, no additional appropriation or programmatic changes are required under section 23 (Human rights). Conference-servicing costs for 1989 are estimated at full cost of $519,000 (annex), but per para. 19 these are expected to be absorbed within section 29 (temporary assistance for meetings) based on the approved calendar and five-year average; thus no additional cost under section 29. 1989 workplan: three Geneva meetings (Jan/Feb ~5 working days; May/June ~10 days; Sept ~10 days) and a field mission to Europe/Middle East (late spring/early summer, up to 21 days). Interpretation from/into English and French (and Arabic/Hebrew when witnesses are heard); translation of Arabic/Hebrew materials; transcripts of testimony. Conference-servicing annex totals: Jan/Feb meeting $56,100; May/June meeting $105,700; field mission $225,100; Sept meeting $132,100; grand total $519,000. Temporary staffing to assist the Special Committee: one P-2 and two General Service staff (indexing and preparing monthly summaries, servicing), plus one P-3 for translation and documentation (12 work-months each). Itemized salary/common staff cost estimates indicated: P-2 $61,000; 2 GS $75,000; overtime for secretarial staff $20,000; P-3 $102,000; plus general operating expenses (subscriptions, publications, cartographic services, maintenance of word-processing equipment). Field mission general operating expenses include rental of conference/office space, local transportation, communications, air freight (sound equipment, tapes), and miscellaneous/unforeseen (travel/subsistence of witnesses, notices in newspapers). Travel/subsistence for three Committee members is foreseen for meetings and mission; travel/subsistence for six substantive/administrative staff during the mission is also foreseen. Overall, the Secretary-General indicates requirements can be met within existing resources (absorption).

However, for 1989 the Secretary-General estimates no additional appropriations required under section 29 (temporary assistance for meetings) as costs are expected to be absorbed within existing capacity based on the five-year average; for 1990, provision to be made in the next biennium’s programme budget. Under Section 6A, at least 72 Professional work-months are required for new activities, to be covered by redeployment from existing programme elements; no additional 1988-1989 resources requested. Relevant programmes: Global development issues and policies (subprogramme 1: Development perspectives; subprogramme 6: Monitoring and assessment of emerging trends and problems); UNCTAD Section 15, programme 1, subprogramme 2 for contributions. Note: September 1989 session requires an exception to GA resolution 42/243 (reporting deadlines).
