title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/42/L.41 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.3/42/L.41 requests, using existing resources, a two‑week Intergovernmental Expert Group meeting (25 Jan–5 Feb 1988) and administrative arrangements for a 1988 plenipotentiary conference to sign the convention. Additional requirements are solely for conference servicing; travel of participants to be borne by governments. Full‑cost estimate for the Expert Group session totals $716,900 (pre‑session docs $224,600; meeting services $146,900; in‑session docs $110,900; post‑session docs $223,300; other $11,200). For the plenipotentiary conference, no additional appropriation was requested (requirements previously drawn to GA in revised estimates A/C.5/42/16). Programme outputs fall under Section 20 (International drug control), but financial implications pertain to Section 29.

Draft resolution A/C.3/42/L.41 requests the Secretary-General to use existing resources to convene a two-week Intergovernmental Expert Group meeting (Vienna, 25 Jan–5 Feb 1988) and to make administrative arrangements for a Plenipotentiary Conference in 1988. Full-cost conference-servicing estimate for the Expert Group meeting totals $716,900 (pre-session documentation $224,600; meeting services $146,900; in-session documentation $110,900; post-session documentation $223,300; Office of General Services $11,200; all in A/C/E/F/R/S). Travel costs for participants are to be borne by Governments. For the Plenipotentiary Conference, no additional appropriation has been requested for conference servicing. The Secretary-General estimates that, given provision for temporary assistance for meetings and pending the approved 1988 conference calendar, no additional appropriation under section 29 would be required as a result of adoption of the draft resolution. Activities fall under Section 20 (International drug control) for substantive servicing, with conference servicing under Section 29; no change is required in Section 29.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/42/L.11 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Conference-servicing requirements for the Ad Hoc Committee of the Whole (6–19 September 1988, 10 working days; 20 meetings with simultaneous interpretation; 100 pages pre-session, 25 pages in-session, 40 pages post-session; Office of General Services support) estimated at $285,100, but the Secretary-General indicates these can be covered within existing temporary assistance for meetings provisions in section 29 based on five-year averages; therefore no additional cost is expected. Travel of representatives entails no additional requirements per GA resolution 1798 (XVII). No modifications to the approved work programme (sections 1 and 5A) and no new posts.

Conference-servicing requirements for the Ad Hoc Committee of the Whole are estimated at $285,100 (pre-session documentation $99,200; meeting servicing $92,000; in-session documentation $29,200; post-session documentation $39,700; Office of General Services $25,000), but the Secretary-General estimates that no additional cost would be incurred under section 29 (conference services) for 1988-1989 because provision for temporary assistance for meetings has been included in the proposed programme budget (five-year average). Travel of representatives under section 1 would not give rise to additional requirements. Activities fall under section 5A; meeting scheduled for 6–19 September 1988 for 10 working days with simultaneous interpretation in A,C,E,F,R,S. Reports of ACABQ and the Committee on Conferences were presented orally; no additional resource amounts or posts were specified.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.6/42/L.10 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/47/L.20 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Secretary-General requests authority and resources to establish UNOVER (United Nations Observer Mission to Verify the Referendum in Eritrea). Total estimated requirements for 1992-1993 are $2,807,000, broken down as $486,000 for 1992 and $2,321,000 for 1993. Costs are not absorbable within the approved 1992-1993 budget; financing would require either additional appropriation or voluntary contributions via the UN Trust Fund for Electoral Observation. SG intends to inform the GA before action whether sufficient voluntary contributions have been received; if not, an additional appropriation of up to $2,807,000 would be required (staff assessment of $220,000 is fully offset). Mission expected to wind up by May 1993. Additional staffing includes 123 locally recruited GTA staff (3 Secretary/Interpreter for regional offices; 4 Drivers for HQ/regions; 5 Drivers and 5 Interpreters for observer teams for ~6 months; 106 Drivers/Interpreters for ~2 weeks) and augmentation by 100 election monitors for 5–10 days (20 UN Secretariat, 50 UNDP/UN agencies, 30 provided by Member States). Efforts will be made to fill the 10 P-2/3 Political/Electoral Observer posts with UN Volunteers. Operational items include rental of premises, acquisition of 9 4x4 vehicles, limited aircraft charter, purchase of 10 HF transceivers, and public information activities ($42,000).

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/42/L.84 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

Activities also relate to Section 15, programme 8, subprogramme 1 (Least developed countries), with no modification of the programme of work required.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/42/L.53 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

title: Convention on the Prohibition of the Development, Production, Stockpiling and Use of Chemical Weapons and on Their Destruction : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/47/L.1/Rev.2 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Secretary-General states there are no programme budget implications for the UN regular budget; costs are to be met by States signatories to the Chemical Weapons Convention. However, due to timing, he requests authorization for an exceptional reimbursable advance from the General Fund of up to $626,600, to be reimbursed by the Preparatory Commission within 90 days of the start of services (by 15 April 1993). Breakdown of estimated costs: (a) First session of the Preparatory Commission at The Hague (8–12 Feb 1993), on a full-cost basis: conference servicing $199,300; travel and DSA for 49 conference-servicing staff from Geneva $141,600; travel and DSA for one Professional and one General Service staff from the Office for Disarmament Affairs $11,100; administrative costs (pouch, communications, etc.) $21,000; subtotal (a) $373,000. (Note: the text earlier cites $337,000 for (a), which appears inconsistent; the itemized subtotal is $373,000.) (b) Assistance to the Executive Secretary for six months (15 Jan–14 Jul 1993): salary and common staff costs for three Professional staff $142,200; travel and DSA for these three staff $111,400; subtotal (b) $253,600. Grand total (a+b) $626,600. Conference servicing assumes up to 8 meetings with interpretation in six official languages and processing of 50 pages pre-session, 100 pages in-session, 50 pages post-session; potential for local recruitment/training may reduce costs for subsequent sessions. The Secretary-General intends to seek advance payments from signatories against their prospective assessed contributions. The requested amount is a one-time, reimbursable cash advance, not a regular budget appropriation.

Secretary-General states there are no programme budget implications for the regular budget; costs are to be borne by the States parties/Preparatory Commission for the OPCW. Estimated costs for the first session of the Preparatory Commission (8–12 Feb 1993, The Hague) are itemized at $373,000 (conference-servicing $199,300; travel/DSA for 49 conference-servicing staff $141,600; travel/DSA for one Professional and one GS from ODA $11,100; administrative $21,000). Earlier line states $337,000, but detailed total shows $373,000 (apparent typographical inconsistency). Additional support for up to six months to the Executive Secretary includes salaries/common staff costs for three Professional staff ($142,200) plus their travel/DSA (amount not specified). The GA is asked to authorize, on an exceptional basis, provision of services via a reimbursable advance from the General Fund of up to $626,600, to be reimbursed by the Preparatory Commission within 90 days of the start of services (by 15 April 1993). Actual conference-servicing costs may be reduced if qualified local staff are used; UN staff presence would be used to train local hires. Under standard practice, services are provided only when sufficient external resources have been received; an exception is requested here due to timing after opening for signature (13 Jan 1993).

title: Implementation of the Declaration on the Denuclearization of Africa : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/47/L.14 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: SG statement on draft resolution A/C.1/47/L.14 (First Committee). (b) Substantive costs subtotal $58,700 comprising travel and DSA for 11 experts $45,700, plus travel and DSA for 2 staff members (1 Professional, 1 GS) from the Office for Disarmament Affairs $13,000. No posts requested; Office will use existing staff for substantive support. Not anticipated that costs can be absorbed within section 37; reliance on contingency fund noted, with possible postponement if not available.

Costs relate to convening a five-day 1993 meeting in Harare, Zimbabwe, for a group of 11 experts designated by the UN in cooperation with OAU. No provision exists in the 1992-1993 budget, and costs cannot be absorbed within section 37; implementation may depend on contingency fund availability and could be postponed if funds are insufficient. Meeting would not be included in the 1993 calendar of conferences; the regular budget would bear related costs (conference-servicing, travel/DSA, and substantive support). title: Transparency in armaments : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/47/L.48 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/SPC/47/L.7 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/47/L.16, entitled "United Nations African Institute for the Prevention of Crime and the Treatment of Offenders" : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.3/47/L.16 (Third Committee) requests that sufficient resources for UNAFRI be provided within the overall appropriations of the 1992-1993 programme budget (i.e., absorption; no new appropriation). The Secretary-General does not provide an exact estimate for 1993 and instead seeks concurrence to continue using the grant approved at the forty-sixth session to finance the posts of Director and Deputy Director through 1993. If the balance of the 1992 grant proves insufficient, the Secretary-General would seek ACABQ concurrence to redeploy necessary resources within existing appropriations for 1992-1993. UNAFRI’s 1992 budget totaled $1,171,457, comprising $746,457 from UNDP, $250,000 from assessed contributions of African Member States, and $180,000 from the United Nations regular budget grant under Section 23 (intended to cover the Director and Deputy Director posts). Due to vacancies and recruitment difficulties in 1992, there was underexpenditure, including of the grant appropriated in 1991. No new posts are proposed or established in this statement.

title: Regional confidence-buidling measures : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/47/L.2 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Costs relate to convening two five-day meetings of the Standing Advisory Committee on security questions in Central Africa in 1993 (Bujumbura, Burundi and Libreville, Gabon). Participants: 22 experts (one civilian and one military from each ECCAS State) for six days, plus 11 ministerial-level representatives for three days at each meeting; UN staff travel included (Secretary of the Committee, two GS from Office for Disarmament Affairs, and one staff from the Lomé regional centre). Activities now under the Department of Political Affairs (Section 37) following restructuring; prior organizational meeting (July 1992) financed from the Trust Fund for Global and Regional Disarmament Activities. No provision exists in the approved 1992-1993 budget; the $203,200 cannot be absorbed within Section 37 and would need the contingency fund. If contingency fund resources are insufficient, implementation may be postponed per GA resolution 42/211. GA would need to grant exceptions to hold meetings away from established UN headquarters sites (normally Addis Ababa) and to pay travel and subsistence for governmental representatives.

Meetings will not be included in the UN calendar and no UN conference services will be provided; they will be held in the chairing countries. Payment of travel and subsistence to governmental representatives requires a General Assembly exception to the usual rule. The Department of Political Affairs (including the Regional Centre for Peace and Disarmament in Africa) will provide substantive support using existing staff resources. Costs cannot be absorbed under section 37 and would need to be met from the contingency fund; otherwise, implementation may be postponed.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/47/L.60/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/47/L.67 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: SG indicates full-cost additional requirements for 1993 of $362,300 (Consultants $19,600; Overtime $300; Temporary posts $209,300; Common staff costs $71,200; Travel $29,500; Contractual services $22,600; GOE $8,800; Supplies $1,000), but proposes temporary redeployment of 2 Professional posts (P-5 and P-4) and 1 GS (Other) to avoid the temporary post/common staff cost appropriations. Net additional appropriation requested under section 1 is $247,700, comprising General temporary assistance $165,900 (bridge financing: $114,600 for 6 work-months covering the two Professional posts; $51,300 for 12 work-months GS), Consultants $19,600; Overtime $300; Travel $29,500; Contractual services $22,600; GOE $8,800; Supplies $1,000. Other costs (consultants, overtime, travel, contractual services, general operating expenses, supplies and materials) comprise the remainder of the $247,700 and cannot be absorbed under section 1. Implementation depends on availability within the contingency fund; if insufficient, action may be postponed per resolutions 41/213 and 42/211. If posts are not identified for redeployment by 30 June 1993, the Secretary-General would report to ACABQ. title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/47/L.51 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

title: Conference on Straddling and Highly Migratory Fish Stocks : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/47/L.62 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: SG estimates full-cost additional requirements of $1,048,800 for 1993: $854,200 conference servicing; $194,600 other (GTA $127,800; staff travel $21,000; travel of representatives $18,000; public information $27,800). However, SG indicates no additional appropriation is required: conference servicing to be covered by permanent capacity under section 41; other $194,600 to be absorbed under section 10/proposed section 38 via savings from shortening sessions of the Preparatory Commission and existing travel provisions ($188,200 + $6,400). A voluntary trust fund for participation of developing States is to be established (extrabudgetary); travel for OAU-recognized national liberation movements ($18,000) included in absorbable requirements.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolutions A/C.2/47/L.46, A/C.2/47/L.47 and A/C.2/47/L.61 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Secretary-General’s statement (A/C.2/47/L.90) on draft resolutions A/C.2/47/L.46, L.47, and L.61. Requested appropriation for 1993 (activities other than conference servicing) totals $2,536,000, allocated as: Section 11.G $1,306,000; Section 11.H $1,113,000; Section 31 $117,000. Staff assessment of $212,000 (section 36) is fully offset by income and not additional. Conference-servicing requirements were costed at full cost of $2,553,400 (comprised of: L.46 $1,606,600; L.61 $620,400; L.47 $326,400), but the statement estimates that no additional resources are required under section 41 for 1992-1993 because needs would be met within permanent capacity; thus the $2,553,400 is to be absorbed. Staffing: interim secretariat arrangements funded via general temporary assistance for six work-months per post. Overall in 1993, 17 GTA-funded posts (1 D-2, 3 D-1, 2 P-5, 3 P-4, 8 GS) plus 1 redeployed P-3 (internal redeployment) will support activities. Ad hoc secretariat for the desertification negotiations (effective 1 Jan 1993) comprises 1 D-2, 1 D-1, 1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 P-3 (redeployed) and 4 GS; $439,000 requested for GTA (six months) for the eight funded posts, plus $271,000 non-staff; multidisciplinary panel of experts (12 consultants, 36 work-months P-4 equivalent) $596,000; related DPI information activities $32,000. For institutional arrangements (CSD): GTA $380,000 (six months for 2 D-1, 1 P-5, 2 P-4, 4 GS) plus $180,000 non-staff; travel of 53 CSD members to two sessions in 1993 $235,000 and observers under res. 3280 $30,000; High-level Advisory Board $288,000; DPI activities $85,000 (part of Section 31 total). For SIDS conference preparations (L.47), substantive work in 1993 is to be covered within the new structure’s resources (no additional 1993 substantive funds); conference-servicing at full cost $326,400 is part of the overall conference-servicing amount expected to be absorbed. L.46 notes funding of the negotiating process also via a voluntary trust fund, in addition to regular budget resources. Sessions outside established headquarters may require an exception to GA res. 40/243.
stages.posts_levels: 1 D-2, 3 D-1, 2 P-5, 3 P-4, 8 GS (Other Level)
stages.budget_section: Section 11 (Development and international economic cooperation) – subsections 11.G and 11.H; also Section 31 (Public information)

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/47/L.91 submitted to the General Assembly for adoption by the 2nd Committee : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes:
- Total additional appropriation sought under Section 34 for the 1992-1993 biennium: $15,989,000.
- Breakdown:
  • Debt cancellation and coverage of UNITAR 1992 obligations: $11,822,500 (comprised of $10,970,800 debt as at 30 June 1992 plus $851,700 for July–December 1992).
  • 1993 annual building maintenance and security: $647,000 (recurring).
  • Repairs and improvements to the building: $2,907,000 (one-time).
  • Transitional arrangements (1 Jan–30 Jun 1993): $612,500, consisting of $300,500 staff costs for 10 UNITAR NY staff to be redeployed, $15,500 office space rental, and $296,500 general operating costs.
- Staff: 10 existing UNITAR NY staff (1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 P-2, 7 GS) to be absorbed within the UN system without loss of rank/benefits per GA res. 42/197; not new posts.
- Secretary-General indicates training programmes in New York can be organized within existing resources; research activities implications not fully known; senior fellows funded by voluntary contributions (no regular budget provision for their office/support).
- Costs should be met outside the contingency fund per GA res. 42/211.
- From 1 Jan 1993, UNITAR administrative budget and training programmes to be financed from voluntary contributions, donations, special-purpose grants and executing agency overheads.

SG states total additional appropriation for the 1992-1993 biennium of $15,989,000 under section 34, Special expenses. Breakdown: (a) $11,822,500 for cancellation of UNITAR’s debt and coverage of 1992 obligations (as at 31 Dec 1992); (b) $647,000 for building maintenance and security in 1993 (described as annual cost); (c) $2,907,000 for repairs and improvements to the building (one-time); (d) $612,500 for transitional arrangements from 1 Jan to 30 Jun 1993 (staff redeployment $300,500; office rental $15,500; general operating costs $296,500). UNITAR staff (10 total: 1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 P-2, 7 GS) to be absorbed within the UN system without loss of rank/benefits per GA res. 42/197; no new posts created. Liaison officer and coordination of existing training programmes in New York to be handled within existing resources; senior fellows to be funded from voluntary contributions; research coordination implications not costed due to insufficient information. SG proposes meeting the additional requirements outside the contingency fund per GA res. 42/211. From 1 Jan 1993, UNITAR’s administrative budget and training programmes to be funded from voluntary contributions, donations, special-purpose grants and executing agency overheads.

title: United Nations Conference on Human Settlements (Habitat II) : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/47/L.75 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: 1993 additional appropriation requested under section 19 totals $247,100; staff assessment of $20,200 (section 36) is fully offset by income. Estimated full cost for conference servicing of the 3‑day organizational session in March 1993 is $118,400, but no additional resources are required under section 41 (Administration and management) for 1992‑1993 as it is expected to be accommodated within existing capacity. Resource needs for 1994–1995 and 1996–1997 to be considered later; overall multi‑biennium conference estimates noted as $2–2.5 million (substantive and public information) plus about $3 million (conference servicing) are not requested here. Two trust funds to be established: one for preparatory and conference activities and one to support participation of developing countries. Host country (Turkey) to bear any additional costs of holding the 1996 conference away from UN Headquarters per GA resolution 40/243.
stages.budget_section: Section 19, United Nations Centre for Human Settlements (Habitat)

stages.notes: 1993 request under Section 19 covers: GTA D-1 (7 work-months, $70,500); consultancy P-5 (5 work-months, $40,000); staff travel ($59,400, including $34,500 for Nairobi–New York travel for the organizational session and $24,900 for five regional commissions’ representatives); observers’ travel ($12,200 per GA res. 3280); communications ($15,000); public information ($50,000). Conference servicing for the 3-day organizational session in March 1993 estimated at $118,400 is to be absorbed within existing capacity under Section 41; no additional resources requested there. Two trust funds to be established: one for preparatory/Conference activities and one to support developing countries’ participation. Overall multi-year indicative costs noted (not part of 1993 request): $2–2.5 million for substantive/public information plus about $3 million for conference servicing at full cost across 1992–1997. Host country (Turkey) to bear additional costs of holding the 1996 Conference away from established UN headquarters per GA res. 40/243. If contingency fund resources are insufficient, activities would be deferred per GA res. 42/211.
stages.budget_section: Section 19, United Nations Centre for Human Settlements (Habitat)

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolutions A/47/L.17 and A/47/L.18 : report of the 5th Committee : General Assembly, 47th session.

stages.notes: SG statement (rule 153) for draft resolutions A/47/L.17 and A/47/L.18 estimates 1993 full-costs but indicates absorption within existing appropriations; thus no additional appropriations requested. Key figures: regular activities full cost in 1993 = $247,600 (Special Committee programme); seminar in Pacific Region (Section 37) = $293,800 (breakdown: survey mission $37,800; travel/DSA for participants and 8 staff $214,200; general operating expenses $41,800); public information related to seminar (Section 31) = $28,400 (to be absorbed within section 31); conference servicing for seminar (Section 41) additional requirements = $393,100 (to be met from approved appropriations). Applying 70% utilization, estimated 1993 requirements = $379,000, which fall within uncommitted resources under Section 37 ($448,600). Prior appropriation for Special Committee activities under Section 6 for biennium 1992-1993 was $538,600, with relevant resources proposed for transfer to Section 37 due to restructuring. Conclusion in para. 17: No additional appropriations required under sections 31, 37 or 41.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, if draft resolutions A/47/L.17 and A/47/L.18 are adopted, implementation would require modification of the programme of work initially programmed under section 6 (as referenced in para. 10 of the Secretary-General’s statement A/C.5/47/48). It also decided to inform the GA that no additional appropriation would be required under sections 31, 37 and 41 of the 1992-1993 programme budget. ACABQ advice was presented orally; no specific amounts or posts are stated in this document.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolutions A/47/L.29, A/47/L.31, A/47/L.32, A/47/L.44 and A/47/L.45 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: No additional appropriations are requested; all 1993 requirements are to be absorbed within existing 1992-1993 appropriations. Key amounts for 1993 within Section 37: (1) Regular activities of the Special Committee: $438,000, absorbable within a balance of $503,300. (2) Special projects of the Special Committee: GA draft resolution A/47/L.29 envisages a special allocation of $450,000 for 1993; full cost of special projects is estimated at $567,500, absorbable from an available balance of $613,600. (3) Grants to national liberation movements (African National Congress of South Africa and Pan Africanist Congress of Azania) to maintain offices in New York: $384,000, fully covered by the 1993 portion of related appropriations. Subtotal full cost for Section 37 items in 1993: $1,389,500, all covered within existing appropriations. Activities of the Intergovernmental Group to Monitor the Supply and Shipping of Oil and Petroleum Products to South Africa are included under 'Regular activities of the Special Committee'. Department of Public Information activities related to the programme are under Section 31 and require no additional resources. Conference servicing will be accommodated under Section 32 (proposed transfer to Section 41) with no additional resources. Potential electoral assistance mission to South Africa (per A/47/L.32) would be absorbed or, if necessary, funded from the unforeseen and extraordinary expenses mechanism (GA resolution 46/187); no specific amount is stated.

Fifth Committee decided, based on the Secretary-General’s statement A/C.5/47/74 and the ACABQ’s oral recommendations, that should the General Assembly adopt draft resolutions A/47/L.29, A/47/L.31, A/47/L.32, A/47/L.44 and A/47/L.45, no modifications to the programme of work and no additional appropriation would be required at this stage for the 1992-1993 biennium under sections 31, 37 and 41.
stages.budget_section: Sections 31 (Public information), 37 (Department of Political Affairs) and 41 (Administration and management)

title: Transparency in armaments : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/47/L.18 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: The SG estimates additional appropriations of $79,500 under section 37 in 1993, composed of $50,000 for start-up computer hardware/software and $29,500 in general temporary assistance for one GS (other level) staff member. A P-4/P-5 post would be temporarily redeployed in 1993 under vacancy treatment; no new established posts are requested for 1993. At full cost for ongoing operation and maintenance of the Register, the SG estimates $171,300 (including $50,000 hardware/software and $121,300 for three additional posts: 1 P-5, 1 P-2, 1 GS), to be considered in the 1994-1995 budget. Convening of a group of governmental experts is planned for 1994; longer-term staffing and other requirements will be addressed in the 1994-1995 proposals. Costs are subject to availability in the contingency fund; if not available, implementation may be postponed.

title: Programme budget for the biennium 1994-1995 : Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.5/48/L.63/Rev.2 : draft report of the 5th Committee : General Assembly, 48th session.

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/48/L.63/Rev.2, the Secretary-General would be authorized to enter into commitments of $7.5 million under section 4 for the initial period of MINUGUA ending 31 December 1994. Full authorization for the remaining mandate period to be determined following review of revised budget proposals. The Secretary-General is requested to reduce mission costs (including staffing and related costs) without hindering the mandate; submit a revised budget no later than 30 November 1994; ensure full internal audit and management review services from Headquarters; and, if the Assembly approves the procedure in decision 48/496, include provisions in the revised budget for payments to other missions for transfer of vehicles and surplus equipment. Enhanced UN system coordination is requested to implement the mandate cost-effectively.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution contained in document E/1993/L.34 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 31 of the rules of procedure of the Economic and Social Council : review of the arangements for consultation with non-governmental organizations.

stages.notes: Full-cost estimate for one-week working group at UN Headquarters serviced in six official languages is US$ 164,200 (1993). However, based on available temporary assistance for meetings under section 41 for the 1992-1993 biennium, no additional resources are required; costs to be absorbed.

title: Report of the Committee for Development Planning : general review of the list of the least developed countries : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/49/L.16 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.2/49/L.16 would add Angola and Eritrea to the LDC list and graduate Botswana, with immediate effect. The Secretary-General estimates additional travel costs for the two new LDCs of $109,800 for the 1994-1995 biennium ($54,100 in 1994 and $55,700 in 1995), offset by reduced costs from Botswana’s graduation of $66,600 ($32,800 in 1994 and $33,800 in 1995), for a net biennial increase of $43,200. Despite these net costs, the SG indicates that no additional appropriation under section 1 is required at this stage; absorption will depend on actual travel expenditure patterns for the forty-ninth session. Activities relate programmatically to programme 15 (UNCTAD) subprogramme 1, but the budget impact is under section 1.
stages.budget_section: Section 1 (travel of representatives of least developed countries to sessions of the General Assembly)

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/47/L.56 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Secretary-General’s statement on draft resolution A/47/L.56 establishing the United Nations component of the joint UN/OAS International Civilian Mission to Haiti. 1993 net full-cost requirements estimated at $23,694,900 (personnel $18,083,200; operational $5,611,700). However, pending agreement with OAS on cost-sharing for local support, the Secretary-General proposes an interim additional appropriation of $19,112,600 under section 2 (plus $2,925,000 staff assessment offset under income section 1). The $19,112,600 equals the full costs of UN international staff plus 35% of estimated costs of local personnel, goods and services for the joint mission. A Trust Fund for Haiti will be solicited and may offset regular budget needs; $1,000,000 in commitments for initial requirements (Special Envoy office, advance team, survey group, 3 human rights experts) were authorized under resolution 46/187 and are incorporated in the 1993 estimates. Mission HQ in Port-au-Prince with 14 regional offices; UN contingent includes 133 human rights observers within 198 international temporary posts; estimated local-level posts total 254 (40 secretaries; 48 interpreters/translators; 16 administrative assistants; 4 communications technicians/storekeepers; 2 receptionists; 106 drivers; 38 security guards). Key line items at full cost for 1993: Temporary posts $12,468,800; Consultants $79,400; Mission subsistence allowance $5,360,300; Travel of staff $174,700; Rental/maintenance of premises $443,700; Rental of aircraft $360,000; Rental/maintenance of vehicles $333,900; Communications $108,000; Human rights education/public information $200,000; Miscellaneous supplies/services $341,000; Freight/related costs $80,000; Acquisition of vehicles $2,464,000; Acquisition of furniture/equipment $670,100; Acquisition of communications equipment $611,000. Staff assessment of $2,925,000 under section 36 is fully offset by income and not counted as new money.
stages.posts_levels: USG, ASG, D-2, D-1, P-5, P-4, P-3, P-2, FS, GS, SS

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/49/L.8, as amended in document A/49/L.12 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Secretary-General estimates total full-cost requirements for convening a 5-day international meeting of experts on mine clearance in New York in early 1995. Conference-servicing costs of $137,400 (pre-session, meeting servicing with interpretation in A,C,E,F,R,S, in-session, post-session documentation) are expected to be absorbed within Section 25E (Conference services), resulting in no additional appropriation for that part. Non-conference-servicing costs of $70,000 (travel and subsistence for 15 experts serving in their individual capacity) cannot be absorbed within Section 23 and require an additional appropriation under Section 23. Implementation is subject to the availability of the contingency fund; if insufficient, implementation may be postponed per resolutions 41/213 and 42/211. No posts are requested or created, and the requirement appears one-time linked to the 1995 meeting and report preparation.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/49/L.23 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Full-cost resources identified for 1995 total $850,200: $99,900 under Section 21 (travel/honoraria for 10 members for one additional three-week session and one pre-sessional working group) and $750,300 under Section 25E (conference services: meetings, documentation, summary records, GS support). The Secretary-General proposes to redeploy $99,900 within Section 21 from resources approved for the Committee on Migrant Workers (not meeting in 1994-1995) and to meet $750,300 within existing Section 25E conference-servicing resources. Therefore, no additional appropriation is required. No new staff posts are requested; previously approved posts (one P-4, one P-2, one GS; plus one P-4, one P-3, two GS for Migrant Workers) are redeployed to support the increased workload. The decision increases the Committee on the Rights of the Child to three sessions annually as from 1995 (ongoing).

title: Establishment of an African nuclear-weapon-free zone : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/49/L.2/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Full-cost estimate for a five-day 1995 meeting in Pretoria totals $112,300 (conference servicing $27,200; travel/DSA for 17 experts $60,000; travel/DSA for 2 staff $13,100; consultant services $12,000). The Centre for Disarmament Affairs would absorb all these costs within existing resources; therefore, no additional appropriation under subsection 3B for 1994-1995. Meeting at Pretoria requires an exception to the principle of holding UN meetings at established headquarters (normally Addis Ababa). The Office of Conference and Support Services would not be requested to provide conference services directly; regular budget bears related costs.
stages.budget_section: Section 3, Political Affairs (subsection 3B: Department of Political Affairs I; programme of work: Disarmament)

stages.notes: Full estimated cost for a five-day 1995 meeting in Pretoria totals $112,300 (conference servicing $27,200; non-conference servicing $85,100, comprising $60,000 travel/DSA for 17 experts, $13,100 travel/DSA for 2 staff, and $12,000 for 3 work-months of consultant services at P-3). The Secretary-General indicates these costs would be fully absorbed within existing resources of subsection 3B; therefore, no additional appropriation is required. An exception to the UN principle of holding meetings at established headquarters is required to convene in Pretoria (normally Addis Ababa). Languages/services noted: interpretation (English, French); translation (Arabic, English, French).
stages.budget_section: Section 3, Political Affairs (subsection 3B - Department of Political Affairs I, Disarmament)

title: Comprehensive nuclear-test-ban treaty : programme budget implications of draft resolutions A/C.1/49/L.17/Rev.1 and A/C.1/49/L.22/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Full-cost additional provision estimated at $161,000 for general temporary assistance in 1995: $93,500 for one P-3 (10 work-months) and $67,500 for one GS (Other level) (10 work-months). Conference-servicing requirements for seven weekly inter-sessional meetings (May, July, Nov-Dec 1995) with interpretation/translation in six official languages are included in the 1995 calendar and assumed to be met within resources under subsection 25E (Office of Conference Services); no additional conference servicing requirements. Prior GA appropriation of $184,000 under section 3B (resolution 48/231 A) yielded savings of $105,500 to offset the $161,000 estimate; net additional requirements of $55,500 are anticipated to be met from resources available under section 3 (i.e., absorbed; no new appropriation requested).

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.5/49/L.4 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: SG estimates total requirements of $987,200 for two sessions (four weeks) of a 25-expert working group in early 1995: $442,000 for travel/subsistence of experts and $545,200 for conference-servicing. Draft resolution specifies funding from existing resources and invites voluntary contributions; a trust fund will be established. SG will endeavor to absorb costs; for conference servicing, meetings would be added to the calendar and covered under section 25 if consistent with patterns. No additional appropriation required at this stage. One-time activity in 1995; no posts created.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/49/L.59 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.2/49/L.59 requests the Secretary-General to provide UNITAR office space in Geneva and liaison office space in New York, plus administrative and logistical support, at fair costs. The Secretary-General clarifies that UNITAR, as an 'other user' funded outside the regular budget, must reimburse the United Nations for actual costs based on commercial rates; therefore there are no additional requirements for the UN regular budget. In Geneva, 'other users' at Petit Saconnex are charged at 70% of the Palais des Nations rate, covering reimbursement of rental, general operating costs, and furniture rental; rates are adjusted to actual expenses. Any New York liaison office space would be charged similarly to UNITAR. All UNITAR costs are to be covered from voluntary contributions, donations, special-purpose grants and executing agency overheads.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/49/L.37/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Secretary-General requests $460,500 for 1995, comprising staff costs ($380,500) for 7 new posts and $80,000 in non-post operational resources for the Small Island Developing States Unit. Staff assessment of $95,100 under section 28 is fully offset by income. New posts (effective 1 Jan 1995): DPCSD Small Island Developing States Unit (1 P-5 Chief, 1 P-4 Economic Affairs Officer, 1 GS (OL)); UNCTAD Division for LDCs, LLDCs and Island Developing Countries (1 P-4); regional commissions (1 P-4 each in ESCAP Pacific Operations Centre/Vanuatu, ECLAC Subregional HQ for the Caribbean/Trinidad and Tobago, and ECA HQ/Addis Ababa). Sectional breakdown of $460,500: Section 8 DPCSD $230,500; Section 11A UNCTAD $66,800; Section 15 ECA $66,000; Section 16 ESCAP $53,400; Section 18 ECLAC $43,800. Financing is subject to availability under the contingency fund; if not available, implementation would be postponed to the 1996-1997 biennium.

title: Regional confidence-building measures : Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/48/L.6 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Full cost of proposed 1994 activities estimated at $206,800, comprising: (1) Brazzaville meeting (5 days) $84,200; (2) Luanda meeting (5 days) $122,600. The Secretary-General indicates provision exists in the 1994-1995 proposed programme budget; therefore no additional appropriation is required (to be absorbed). GA would need to grant exceptions to hold meetings away from established UN headquarters locations and to pay travel and subsistence for governmental representatives. Historical reference: 1993 meetings cost $148,400 from the regular budget.
stages.budget_section: Section 3B, Disarmament (subprogramme 4: Assistance to developing countries on disarmament issues: regional centres; fellowships, training and advisory services)

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolutions A/C.1/48/L.40 and A/C.1/48/L.41 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Request covers general temporary assistance in 1994 for services of one P-5 and one GS (Other level) for 10 work-months each (total $184,000). Staff assessment of $51,500 is fully offset by income and not additional. Conference services for inter-sessional meetings to be provided within existing resources under subsection 25E (Office of Conference Services); no additional conference servicing costs. No absorption foreseen under subsection 3B; financing expected via the contingency fund or by redeployment if needed. Requirements for 1995 to be reviewed in 1994; a further report may be submitted depending on workload.
stages.budget_section: Section 3, Political affairs (subsection 3B, Department of Political Affairs – Disarmament)

stages.notes: General temporary assistance in 1994 to support the Ad Hoc Committee on a Nuclear-Test Ban: one P-5 ($118,100) and one GS (Other level) ($65,900), each for 10 work-months. Conference services for inter-sessional meetings (documentation and interpretation in six languages; seven meetings per week during specified 1994 periods) to be met from within subsection 25E, Office of Conference Services; no additional conference-servicing requirements. If contingency fund resources are insufficient, implementation would require redeployment from lower-priority subprogrammes (subprogrammes 2 and 3 of subsection 3B) or postponement per resolutions 41/213 and 42/211. Requirements for 1995 will be reviewed during 1994 and may be the subject of a further report.

title: Establishment of a nuclear-weapon-free zone in Africa : Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/48/L.19 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Full cost requirements for 1994 total $135,600 to convene two sessions of the Group of Experts (Windhoek two weeks: $85,800; Addis Ababa five days: $49,800). Windhoek costs include conference room rental ($6,000), interpretation (E,F) ($12,000), office equipment rental ($1,000), translation (E,F) ($1,900), travel/DSA for 12 experts ($51,000), and travel/DSA for 2 staff (1 Professional, 1 GS) from the Centre for Disarmament Affairs ($13,900). Addis Ababa costs include interpretation (E,F) ($6,000), travel/DSA for 12 experts ($33,700), and travel/DSA for 2 staff ($10,100). The Centre for Disarmament Affairs will absorb these costs from existing resources: ad hoc expert groups ($84,700), staff travel ($24,000), and general operating expenses ($26,900); therefore, no additional appropriation is required under section 3B for 1994. Office of Conference Services will not be requested to provide services directly; the regular budget will bear all related costs. The General Assembly would need to grant an exception to its principle of holding UN meetings at established headquarters for the Windhoek meeting.
stages.budget_section: Section 3B, Political Affairs (Department of Political Affairs – Disarmament)

stages.notes: Full-cost estimate for 1994 activities totals $135,600 for two sessions of the Group of Experts: Windhoek (two weeks) $85,800 and Addis Ababa (five days) $49,800. Windhoek breakdown: conference-servicing $20,900 (room rental $6,000; interpretation (E,F) $12,000; office equipment rental $1,000; translation (E,F) $1,900) and non-conference servicing $64,900 (travel/DSA for 12 experts $51,000; travel/DSA for 2 staff from Centre for Disarmament Affairs $13,900). Addis Ababa breakdown: conference-servicing $6,000 (interpretation (E,F)) and non-conference servicing $43,800 (travel/DSA for 12 experts $33,700; travel/DSA for 2 staff $10,100). The Secretary-General indicates these costs would be absorbed within existing resources of the Centre for Disarmament Affairs: ad hoc expert groups $84,700; travel of staff $24,000; general operating expenses $26,900; thus no additional appropriation is required under section 3B for 1994-1995. Note: exception needed to the principle that UN bodies meet at established headquarters for the Windhoek session; Office of Conference Services would not be requested to provide services directly; regular budget would bear all costs.
stages.budget_section: Section 3, Political Affairs (subsection 3B: Department of Political Affairs – Disarmament)

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/48/L.8 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: The Secretary-General estimates the full-cost servicing of a 10‑working‑day mid-term global review conference in 1995 at $526,000 (pre-session documentation $34,700; meeting servicing for 40 meetings $249,400; in-session documentation $185,900; post-session documentation $56,000), but states that no additional appropriation is required under the 1994-1995 budget, as conference servicing is expected to be absorbed within existing capacity/temporary assistance under section 25. Substantive and organizational preparations are already budgeted under Section 11A (UNCTAD), programme 15, subprogramme 1 (Least developed countries). Participation of least developed countries in preparatory meetings is to be funded from extrabudgetary resources.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.6/48/L.16 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Sixth Committee draft resolution A/C.6/48/L.16 would have the Secretary-General convene a one-week United Nations congress on public international law in 1995 and proceed with preparations and inform Member States. The activity relates to programme 9, International law, subprogramme 3, Progressive development and codification of international law (Section 7). The Secretary-General states no additional requirements during the 1994-1995 biennium; the congress should be held within existing resources and assisted by voluntary contributions. It would be convened at UN Headquarters, New York, in 1995, for five working days with two plenary meetings daily. Interpretation/translation in Arabic, Chinese, English, French, Russian and Spanish; estimated documentation: 10 pages pre-session, 10 pages in-session, 35 pages post-session. Any temporary conference-servicing assistance, if needed, would be covered under Section 25 E provisions for subsequently authorized meetings; thus no additional appropriation under Sections 7 or 25 for 1994-1995.

title: Question of responsibility for attacks on United Nations and associated personnel and measures to ensure that those responsible for such attacks are brought to justice : Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.6/48/L.12 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Substantive servicing by the Office of Legal Affairs (Codification Division) and conference servicing under section 25E are to be absorbed within existing resources. Two sessions of an Ad hoc Committee authorized (28 March–8 April 1994 and, if decided by the Committee, 1–12 August 1994) at UN Headquarters, with services in six languages and approximately 20 meetings per session with related documentation. The Secretary-General states that no additional appropriations are required under the proposed programme budget for 1994-1995. Related to programme 9, International law, subprogramme 3.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution contained in the report of the Chairman of the Working Group of the 3rd Committee (A/C.3/48/L.85) : General Assembly, 48th session : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Creation of the post of High Commissioner for Human Rights (rank: Under-Secretary-General). Immediate office in Geneva: 1 USG (High Commissioner), 1 P-5 special assistant, 2 GS; non-post items: travel $50,000, overtime $5,000, office automation equipment $7,000. Liaison Office in New York: establishment of 1 P-5 and 1 GS (in addition to the existing Centre for Human Rights liaison office staffed by 1 P-4 and 1 GS). Total additional requirements (excluding staff assessment) are not expected to be absorbed within Section 21; financing depends on availability in the contingency fund; if insufficient, implementation may be postponed per GA resolutions 41/213 and 42/211. Staff assessment ($397,600) is fully offset by income and not new money.
stages.posts_levels: USG, P-5, P-5, GS, GS, GS

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/48/L.59 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Secretary-General estimates additional requirements of $1,471,400 under section 21 for establishing the Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights in Geneva and reinforcing the Human Rights Liaison Office in New York. Breakdown: Geneva (1 USG, 1 P-5, 2 GS) = $1,051,500; New York Liaison Office (1 P-5, 1 GS) = $357,900; non-post: travel $50,000, overtime $5,000, office automation equipment $7,000 (total non-post $62,000). Staff assessment of $397,600 under section 28 to be fully offset under income section 1 and should not be counted as new money. The proposed programme budget for 1994-1995 under section 21 has no provision to absorb these costs; funding would need to come from the contingency fund or by redeployment, otherwise implementation may be postponed. Amounts are presented for the biennium 1994-1995 at full cost; the document does not provide a first-year-only breakdown.
stages.posts_levels: Under-Secretary-General, P-5, P-5, General Service, General Service, General Service

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/48/L.70 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.2/48/L.70 would authorize UN field offices in Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus, Eritrea, Georgia, Kazakhstan, the Russian Federation, Ukraine and Uzbekistan. Public information activities are already in place and would continue under these offices. No provision exists under Section 24 to absorb the $2,356,400; therefore, full additional appropriation is indicated for the biennium 1994-1995. Implementation is subject to availability under the contingency fund; if not available, activities may be postponed per GA resolutions 41/213 and 42/211. Staff assessment is fully offset by income. Note: The document contains an internal inconsistency on staff assessment—paragraph G cites $466,800 while paragraph I (para. 12) cites $446,800; $466,800 is used here as stated earlier in the text. The $2,356,400 figure is presented as a biennial total; the document does not provide a first-year split.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/48/L.40 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Related to major programme VII (Human rights, fundamental freedoms and humanitarian affairs), programme 35 (Promotion and protection of human rights), subprogramme 2 (Elimination and prevention of discrimination and protection of minorities and vulnerable groups). Estimated cost totals $15,000 for expert travel: $11,500 for three trips to Geneva (five days each) and $3,500 for one trip to New York (five days) to present the progress report to the forty-ninth session. The Secretary-General indicates these costs could be absorbed within the 1994-1995 resources under section 21; no additional appropriation would be required.

Costs cover three 5‑day trips to Geneva and one 5‑day trip to New York by an appointed expert to collaborate with the Centre for Human Rights and UNICEF and to present a progress report. The Secretary-General indicates the $15,000 can be absorbed within Section 21 resources for 1994-1995; no additional appropriation requested.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/48/L.38 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: This is a Secretary-General (rule 153) statement for draft resolution A/C.3/48/L.38 (Third Committee). The SG estimates full-cost additional requirements of $1,987,400, comprised of: $268,000 for treaty ratification activities (temporary assistance: 9 work-months P-4 and 6 work-months GS totaling $137,000; plus $131,000 for travel of 15 participants to two regional meetings), $468,400 for racism/racial discrimination (establishment of 1 P-5 and 1 GS), $431,100 for indigenous peoples (establishment of 1 P-4 and 1 GS), and $819,900 for rights of the child (1 P-4, 1 P-3, 2 GS). However, the SG identifies absorption: one P-4, one P-3 and two GS posts already provided for the International Convention on the Protection of All Migrant Workers and their Families (not expected to enter into force in 1994-1995) would be redeployed to the rights of the child work. Therefore, the SG requests additional appropriations of $1,167,500 under section 21 (covering ratification activities, racism/racial discrimination team, and indigenous peoples team). Staff assessment associated with the request is $287,100, to be fully offset by income (do not count as new money). Programmatic activities for the International Decade of the World’s Indigenous People would be financed from a voluntary trust fund; assessed-budget costs here relate to staffing/coordination. Contingency fund language notes that, if costs cannot be met from the fund and no redeployment from low-priority areas is identified, implementation may be postponed. Biennial figures are provided for 1994-1995; the document does not specify a first-year (1994) split.

Secretary-General’s statement on programme budget implications of Third Committee draft A/C.3/48/L.38 (Vienna Declaration and Programme of Action). Biennium 1994-1995. Additional requirements at full cost totalled 1,987,400 for: (a) Ratification activities: 268,000 (temporary assistance: 9 work-months P-4 and 6 work-months GS = 137,000; travel of 15 participants to 2 regional meetings = 131,000); (b) Racism and racial discrimination team: 468,400 (1 P-5, 1 GS); (c) Indigenous people: 431,100 (1 P-4, 1 GS); (d) Rights of the child: 819,900 (1 P-4, 1 P-3, 2 GS). Of these, resources for the rights of the child (1 P-4, 1 P-3, 2 GS) would be redeployed from posts already provided in the proposed budget for the International Convention on the Protection of All Migrant Workers and Members of Their Families, reducing the net additional appropriation requested to 1,167,500 under section 21. Staff assessment: 287,100 (to be fully offset by income from staff assessment). Earlier full-cost staff assessment estimate was 505,000 but is not requested due to redeployment. New posts requiring additional appropriations are 1 P-5 and 1 GS (racism) and 1 P-4 and 1 GS (indigenous). Ratification-related temporary assistance and meeting travel are one-time. If costs cannot be met from the contingency fund and no redeployments are identified, implementation may need to be postponed per resolutions 41/213 and 42/211.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/48/L.71 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: SG states that all logistical and administrative expenditures (including senior fellows’ supporting activities and provision of office space in Geneva and New York) must be financed exclusively from UNITAR voluntary contributions, donations, special‑purpose grants and executing agency overheads, not from the UN regular budget. Liaison officer to be designated within existing resources. Senior research fellows continue 'without budgetary implications' and are not UN staff; they are funded from voluntary contributions. If UNITAR cannot cover these costs, the SG is not authorized to use the regular budget. Any such expenditures require prior review/approval of budgets by the UNITAR Board of Trustees.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/48/L.50/Rev.1, A/C.2/48/L.51/Rev.1, A/C.2/48/L.52/Rev.1 and A/C.2/48/L.53/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Document covers four Second Committee drafts: L.50/Rev.1, L.51/Rev.1, L.52/Rev.1, L.53/Rev.1. • L.52/Rev.1 (Second Transport and Communications Decade in Africa): Secretary-General states an additional appropriation of $175,100 under section 15 is required in 1994 to convene four subregional meetings (interpretation $91,800; staff travel/participation $23,300; consultancy $60,000). Two regional workshops in 1995 will be financed by repurposing already programmed subregional workshop resources; assistance to member States in 1995 to prepare/screen projects will be supported from increased resources in section 20 (no new appropriation). If contingency fund resources are insufficient, activities would be deferred per GA resolution 42/211. • L.51/Rev.1 (African Institute for Economic Development and Planning - IDEP): Proposed programme budget (section 15) includes a grant of $942,400 for the biennium 1994-1995 to cover four Professional posts at IDEP (1 D-1, 1 P-5, 2 P-4); this is presented as responding to the draft decision’s request. This amount is included in the proposed budget, not an additional request in this PBI. • L.50/Rev.1 (Strengthening development information systems in Africa): Resources proposed under sections 15 and 20 are considered adequate; no additional appropriation requested. PADIS review to be undertaken in 1994 (no cost stated). • L.53/Rev.1 (Second Industrial Development Decade for Africa): Resources proposed under section 15 (and increased resources under section 20) are considered adequate; no additional appropriation requested.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/48/L.33 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: The Secretary-General indicates no additional regular budget resources are required: (a) Conference servicing for meetings of the Executive Boards of UNDP, UNFPA and UNICEF to be absorbed within Section 25, Administration and management, for the 1994-1995 biennium; (b) Strengthening of the secretariat of the Economic and Social Council to be undertaken within existing resources of Section 8; (c) Any construction of conference-servicing facilities for UNDP, UNFPA and UNICEF would be financed by those entities from their own resources, with no involvement of the United Nations regular budget; technical parameters of meeting changes (number, duration, composition) are not yet known.
stages.budget_section: Section 8, Department for Policy Coordination and Sustainable Development

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided that, if the General Assembly adopts draft resolution A/48/L.33, no additional appropriation is required under the 1994-1995 programme budget. Potential need for additional premises to be examined by the Secretary-General and the Executive Heads of UNDP, UNFPA and UNICEF, with a report to the General Assembly through ACABQ.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/48/L.74/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.3/48/L.74/Rev.1 urges action within existing resources. The Secretary-General states implementation would require $740,300 in 1994: $697,800 for temporary assistance (six P-3 field officers and four locally recruited General Service secretary-translators) and $42,500 in non-post costs (travel Geneva–Belgrade and in the former Yugoslavia, and office equipment). No appropriations are requested under the regular budget; efforts will be made to secure voluntary (extrabudgetary) resources. The six P-3 and four GS are temporary field staffing for 12 work-months in 1994, to be based in Bosnia and Herzegovina, Croatia and the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (Serbia and Montenegro). Prior support included redeployment in 1992 of five posts (1 P-5, 1 P-4, 3 P-3) to the Centre for Human Rights and three extrabudgetary staff in Zagreb; these are existing resources, not new posts under this draft resolution.

Full-cost additional requirements for 1994 total $740,300: $697,800 in temporary assistance (six P-3 field officers for 12 work-months and four locally recruited GS Secretary-translators for 12 work-months) plus $42,500 non-post (travel Geneva–Belgrade and in-theatre, and office equipment). The draft resolution urges action within existing resources; the Secretary-General indicates no additional appropriations would be required for 1994 and that voluntary resources will be sought. Earlier narrative mentions three locally recruited GS staff, but the budget table specifies four; the table figure is used. Related to major programme VII, programme 35, subprogramme 1 (Section 21).

title: World Conference on Natural Disaster Reduction : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/48/L.24 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Conference-servicing full cost is estimated at $709,600 for a 5-day World Conference on Natural Disaster Reduction (Yokohama, 23–27 May 1994) and a 5-day Preparatory Committee session (Geneva, 1994). Per para. 7–8, these costs are expected to be absorbed within existing conference-servicing capacity (Section 25), so no additional appropriation under the 1994–1995 programme budget is required. Additional IDNDR secretariat requirements to support the preparatory process and the Conference total $644,500 (XB): general temporary assistance equivalent to 5 months each for 3 P-5 and 3 GS posts ($272,500), overtime ($10,000), consultants’ fees and travel ($70,000), staff travel ($50,000), hospitality ($25,000), general operating expenses ($67,000), production and dissemination of promotional material ($150,000). Travel and DSA for participants (XB) total $440,200: $206,800 for 25 members of the Scientific and Technical Committee and 10 members of the Special High-level Council, and $233,400 for one representative from each least developed country. The host Government (Japan) will bear additional costs of convening the Conference outside Geneva (GA resolution 40/243). Funding for preparatory process and conference (beyond absorptive conference services) is through voluntary contributions to the Trust Fund for the International Decade for Natural Disaster Reduction.

SG states that no additional appropriation is required under the proposed programme budget for 1994-1995. Conference-servicing full cost estimated at $709,600 (World Conference $588,600; Preparatory Committee $121,000), but expected to be absorbed within existing conference-servicing capacity under section 25; extent of supplementation by temporary assistance depends on the 1994-1995 conference calendar. Per GA res. 40/243, additional costs of convening the Conference outside Geneva are the responsibility of the host Government (Japan). Additional requirements for the Decade secretariat totaling $644,500 (to be funded from extrabudgetary resources) comprise: GTA equivalent to 5 months each for 3 P-5 and 3 GS (other level) posts, overtime, consultants’ fees and travel, staff travel, hospitality, general operating expenses, and production/dissemination of promotional material. Participant travel and DSA estimated at $440,200 (members of the Special High-level Council and the Scientific and Technical Committee, and one representative from each LDC), also to be funded from extrabudgetary resources. The requests relate programmatically to Section 23 (Department of Humanitarian Affairs), while conference servicing falls under Section 25 but is to be absorbed.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.5/48/L.6 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Secretary-General estimates additional requirements totaling $803,800 in 1994 for servicing a 25-member ad hoc working group (three 5-day meetings in New York in 1994). Breakdown: General temporary assistance $321,100; Overtime $60,000; Travel of representatives (experts) $422,700. Conference servicing (interpretation and documentation in six languages) to be absorbed within existing resources under Section 25E, Office of Conference Services; therefore no additional requirement for conference services. A trust fund would be established and voluntary contributions, if received, would reduce regular budget requirements. The staff assessment of $134,800 under Section 28 is fully offset by income under Section 1 and is not new resource requirements. Contingency fund procedures apply; if not accommodated, activities may require redeployment or deferral.
stages.budget_section: Section 25A, Office of the Under-Secretary-General for Administration and Management

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/48/L.88 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: The draft resolution A/C.2/48/L.88 requests: (a) logistical and administrative support for UNITAR training in New York within existing resources; and (b) continuation of full-time senior fellows as an interim measure without budgetary implications until no later than 1 July 1994. The Secretary-General indicates that while a focal point can be provided within existing resources, appropriate logistical/administrative support in New York cannot be provided without additional cost. Continuation of senior fellows entails non-remuneration costs (office space, equipment, communications, miscellaneous services). Pursuant to GA resolution 47/227 and the UNITAR Statute, all such costs must be financed from UNITAR voluntary contributions, donations, special-purpose grants and executing agency overheads, with prior Board of Trustees budget approval, and not from the UN regular budget. If UNITAR cannot cover these costs, the Secretary-General is not authorized to charge the UN regular budget unless the General Assembly decides otherwise. No dollar amounts are provided in the statement; no UN regular-budget posts are created; senior fellows are not UN staff. Support services mentioned cover Geneva and New York.
stages.budget_section: Training and research: United Nations Institute for Training and Research (UNITAR)

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/48/L.51 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: The draft resolution A/48/L.51 would, on an exceptional basis, create a Special Adviser to the Secretary-General for Public Policy at the Under-Secretary-General level funded against general temporary assistance (GTA). The Secretary-General indicates that resources for this function are already included in the proposed programme budget 1994-1995 (section I), covering the Special Adviser and her office with four Professional posts (1 D-1, 1 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 P-3) and seven General Service (Other level) posts, plus GTA, overtime, consultants, travel, communications and office automation. Therefore, if the General Assembly approves those proposed programme budget resources, no additional appropriation is required. If those resources are not approved, an additional appropriation of $2,474,700 would be required under section I for the biennium 1994-1995. The $2,474,700 figure is presented for the biennium and is not broken down by year; first-year (1994) detail is not provided. The Special Adviser’s appointment is already in effect and would be continued.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, if draft resolution A/48/L.51 were adopted, no additional appropriation would be required under the proposed programme budget for 1994-1995. Related SG statement: A/C.5/48/65; ACABQ input provided orally.

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolutions A/48/L.41, A/48/L.42 and A/48/L.43 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolutions A/48/L.41, A/48/L.42 and A/48/L.43, no additional appropriations would be required under the cited sections for the 1994-1995 biennium (based on the Secretary-General’s statement A/C.5/48/43 and ACABQ recommendations presented orally). The Committee also decided the Assembly would need to approve an exception to resolution 40/243 to allow meetings of the Committee on the Exercise of the Inalienable Rights of the Palestinian People away from its established headquarters.
stages.budget_section: Sections 3A (Policy-making organs), 3C (Department of Political Affairs II), 24 (Public information), 25E (Conference services)

title: Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/49/L.67 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.
Strengthening of the United Nations System : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/49/L.68 : report of the 5th Committee.

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/49/L.68, no additional appropriation would be required under the 1994-1995 programme budget. Adoption would require a specific exception to paragraph 7 of resolution 40/243 regarding subsidiary organs meeting at UN Headquarters during a regular session. SG statement referenced: A/C.5/49/71; ACABQ report: A/49/7/Add.14.

SG statement (A/C.5/49/71) estimated total requirements of $451,100 for 1995 and $1,673,400 for 1996 (total $2,124,500), broken down as $288,400 for staff costs, $500,000 for consultants, and $1,336,100 for conference servicing. The draft resolution requests the SG to provide assistance within existing resources supplemented by a trust fund; ACABQ had no information on pledged/received voluntary contributions. ACABQ notes 1995 meetings would be limited; for 1996, up to 78 meetings assumed. ACABQ indicates no additional appropriation is required for the 1994-1995 programme budget (i.e., 1995 costs to be absorbed). On 1996-1997, ACABQ considers the SG’s intention to submit revised estimates for additional staff in 1996 inconsistent with the draft resolution’s instruction to use existing resources/trust fund. ACABQ believes one Professional and one General Service staff member could discharge required functions, and that consultants should be used only if expertise is not available in the Secretariat (and funded from the trust fund). Conference-servicing absorption is contingent on meeting patterns; ACABQ flags that demand may exceed capacity, making impact uncertain.

Full-cost estimate for 1995 totals $451,100 (staff $70,800; consultants $125,000; conference servicing $255,300). SG indicates no additional appropriation is anticipated for 1994–1995: conference servicing to be absorbed within existing conference resources; 1995 consultants to be financed from a voluntary trust fund; 1995 staff costs to be met within existing programme budget resources if possible. For 1996, estimated full costs are $1,673,400 (staff $217,600; consultants $375,000; conference servicing $1,080,800); conference servicing anticipated to be met from section 26E resources in the proposed 1996–1997 budget; 1996 consultants from the trust fund; 1996 staff via revised estimates to the GA. Activity is tied to an Open-ended High-level Working Group; up to 78 meetings in 1996; UN will not fund participant travel/subsistence; press coverage not required. Meetings during the fiftieth session would require an exception to GA resolution 40/243, para. 7.
stages.budget_section: Section 3B (Department of Political Affairs I) – General Assembly affairs

title: Programme budget for the biennium 1994-1995. The situation of democracy and human rights in Haiti. - Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/48/L.54 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly.

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decision (without a vote): If the General Assembly adopts draft resolution A/48/L.60 (in particular para. 8), the maximum requirements for the UN regular budget for the period 1994–1999 could be $25,438,500, and the maximum additional appropriation that may be required for the biennium 1994–1995 would be $439,800. The Committee will revert to the matter at the forty‑ninth session following a review of the work programme by the relevant intergovernmental body and a further ACABQ report (including the budgetary procedure issue noted in para. 4 of A/48/7/Add.16). Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/SPC/44/L.26 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: The Secretary-General states that implementation will be within existing resources; activities are anticipated to be absorbed by the Office for Special Political Affairs. stages.notes: SG states that activities under paras. 5(a)-(c) are within the scope of the Office for Special Political Affairs and can be absorbed within existing resources; activities under para. 5(d) already budgeted under Section 2A (Department of Political and Security Council Affairs).
