stages.notes: ACABQ reviewed SG statement A/C.5/62/21 on draft resolution A/C.3/62/L.65/Rev.1 (as orally revised). It recommends informing the GA that, if adopted, estimated additional requirements up to $6,794,700 for the 2008-2009 biennium would be required as a charge against the contingency fund, allocated under Section 2 ($4,895,900) and Section 23 ($1,898,800). Estimates are preliminary; consultations ongoing; detailed financial information will follow once the structure of the Durban Review Conference and regional meetings is determined. Requirements under operative paras. 2(a) and 2(b) (support to existing mandates including the Special Rapporteur and working/expert groups) would be met within existing 2008-2009 budget provisions (no new money). Annexed preliminary cost elements include: conference services for an intergovernmental working group meeting in 2008 (20 meetings; $1,632,500), temporary assistance staffing (GTA) for 2 P-4 and 2 GS for 18 months ($837,000), five regional preparatory conferences in 2008 (indicative total $3,270,600, with potential host government cost-sharing per GA res. 40/243), travel for 16 Human Rights Council experts to preparatory meetings ($825,100), first substantive session of the Preparatory Committee in April 2008 ($260,000), and staff travel to regional conferences ($236,600). Some small amounts under Section 28E (Administration, Geneva) are included in the annexed estimates. The amount reflects biennium total; first-year-only detail is not provided.
stages.budget_section: Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management; Section 23, Human rights

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decision (A/62/622) informs the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.3/62/L.65/Rev.1 (as orally revised), preliminary additional requirements up to 6,794,700 USD would be required in the 2008-2009 biennium: Section 2, General Assembly and ECOSOC affairs and conference management: 4,895,900 USD; Section 23, Human rights: 1,898,800 USD. The Committee recommends the Assembly take note of these preliminary estimates. Detailed financial information will be submitted once discussions/negotiations conclude and the structure of the Durban Review Conference and regional preparatory meetings is determined. No posts are specified and no appropriation/approval is decided in this report.
stages.budget_section: Section 23, Human rights; and Section 2, General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management

title: Khmer Rouge trials : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/57/L.90 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: The draft resolution decides that the expenses of the Extraordinary Chambers to be defrayed by the United Nations shall be borne by voluntary (extrabudgetary) contributions; no regular budget appropriation is requested. The Secretary-General estimates that an amount in excess of USD 19,000,000 would be required for the establishment and operation of the Extraordinary Chambers, the Prosecutors’ Office, the co-investigating judges, the Pre-Trial Chamber and the Office of Administration, plus related Headquarters backstopping. This estimate excludes remuneration of defence counsel for indigent accused and costs for prosecutorial/investigative activities, supplies and materials, printing, miscellaneous contractual services, general temporary assistance, travel of witnesses (domestic and international), alterations/minor improvements to premises provided by Cambodia, and utilities/services for operation of the Chambers and related institutions. Initiation of operations depends on sufficient voluntary contributions being in place for a sustained period. The international judges, international co-prosecutor and international co-investigating judge are to be deemed officials of the United Nations for terms and conditions of service (no grade/levels specified).

title: United Nations African Institute for the Prevention of Crime and the Treatment of Offenders : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.13 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedures of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General states that adoption of draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.13 would have no immediate programme budget implications. Paragraph 10 may lead to additional expenditures only after consideration of the requested report at the fifty-ninth session. The Secretary-General also notes that Third Committee consideration of paragraph 10 may contravene GA resolution 45/248 B regarding the Fifth Committee’s budgetary responsibility.

title: Preparations for the observance of the 10th anniversary of the International Year of the Family in 2004 : Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.2 (as amended by A/C.3/58/L.12) : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: The Secretary-General indicates that requirements related to the family programme will be met from within existing resources (no additional funds requested). The statement also notes that Third Committee consideration of paragraph 6 bis may contravene GA resolution 45/248 B, as it addresses resource issues under the Fifth Committee’s prerogative. Launch of the tenth anniversary of the International Year of the Family is requested for 4 December 2003.
stages.budget_section: Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Division for Social Policy and Development

title: Report of the Special Committee on the Charter of the United Nations and on the Strengthening of the Role of the Organization : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.6/58/L.18 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General’s PBI for draft resolution A/C.6/58/L.18 indicates: (a) Conference-servicing costs for the Special Committee session (29 March–8 April 2004) estimated at $275,714 would be absorbed within existing resources; no additional appropriation required. (b) Additional resource requirements for producing/coordination of the Repertory of Practice of United Nations Organs total $2,357,600 for the biennium 2004–2005 (first-year breakdown not provided). Sectional distribution of the biennium total (thousands of USD): Section 2 (DGACM) $590.1; Section 3 (DPA) $178.3; Section 4 (Disarmament) $30.0; Section 5 (DPKO) $383.3; Section 8 (OLA) $82.5; Section 9 (DESA) $560.9; Section 24 (OHCHR) $410.9; Section 29B (OPPBA) $55.5; Section 29C (OHRM) $66.1. The outputs added include recurrent publications related to the Repertory in multiple sections, indicating ongoing activity. No posts are created or specified in this document.

Secretary-General indicates no additional appropriation is required for the nine-day Special Committee session (29 March–8 April 2004) because it is already programmed; full-cost conference servicing estimate is $275,714 (absorbed). For the Repertory of Practice of United Nations Organs, an additional appropriation of $2,357,600 (biennium 2004-2005 rates) is requested as a charge against the contingency fund; no per-year breakdown is provided. Sectional distribution of the biennial Repertory-related requirements (thousands of USD): Section 2 DGACM $590.1k; Section 3 DPA $178.3k; Section 4 DDA $30.0k; Section 5 DPKO $383.3k; Section 8 OLA $82.5k; Section 9 DESA $560.9k; Section 24 OHCHR $410.9k; Section 29B OPPBA $55.5k; Section 29C OHRM $66.1k. The outputs/narratives across Sections 2, 3, 4, and 8 would be modified to include relevant Repertory-related activities and publications. Requested amount reflects biennial total; first-year (2004) figure is not specified in the document.

ACABQ considered SG statement A/C.5/58/18 on PBI of draft resolution A/C.6/58/L.18. Conference-servicing for the nine-day session of the Special Committee is estimated at $275,714 but already programmed; no additional appropriation required. Additional requirements for the Repertory of Practice total $2,357,600 for 2004-2005, to be charged to the contingency fund, broken down as: Sec. 2 $590,100; Sec. 3 $178,300; Sec. 4 $30,000; Sec. 5 $383,300; Sec. 8 $82,500; Sec. 9 $560,900; Sec. 24 $410,900; Sec. 29B $55,500; Sec. 29C $66,100. ACABQ encourages greater use of the Internet to disseminate the Repertory to speed access and possibly reduce printing costs. ACABQ notes potential charges against the 2004-2005 contingency fund exceed the approved $21.6 million level; SG should revise the consolidated amount per resolution 42/211; ACABQ will revert in the consolidated statement context.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.6/58/L.18, there would be no effect on the United Nations budget estimates. Sources considered: SG statement A/C.5/58/18 and ACABQ report A/58/7/Add.13.

title: Third United Nations Conference on the Least Developed Countries : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/58/L.34 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: The Secretary-General estimates additional requirements of $783,600 for the biennium 2004-2005 to defray transportation costs (airfare only; no subsistence/DSA) for two government representatives from each least developed country to the annual substantive session of ECOSOC (held once per biennium in New York and once per biennium in Geneva). If adopted, the amount would be a charge against the contingency fund for 2004-2005. The text of operative paragraph 8 would require an exception to GA resolution 1798 (XVII) on travel entitlements and its consideration by the Second Committee may contravene GA resolution 45/248 B; decision on this paragraph is proposed to be deferred to the Fifth Committee.
stages.budget_section: Section 10, Least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States

title: Office of the Special Representative for Children and Armed Conflict : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.28 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rule of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.28 would have activities of the Special Representative for Children and Armed Conflict supported through regular budgetary funding. The Secretary-General notes that administrative and budgetary matters fall under the Fifth Committee and the ACABQ per GA resolution 45/248 B; thus the issue should be referred to the Fifth Committee. No specific resource estimates, amounts, or posts are provided in this statement.

Draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.28 would place the Office of the Special Representative for Children and Armed Conflict on regular budget funding. The Secretary-General estimates biennial (2004-2005) gross requirements of $6,053,700, but no first-year (2004) breakdown is provided. As of end-November 2003, $2,123,000 in extrabudgetary resources remained, expected to finance operations through July 2004. The Secretary-General recommends deferring any regular budget funding action until the resumed 58th session (spring 2004), to consider the forthcoming comprehensive assessment requested by GA resolution 57/190. No posts are specified and no ACABQ or Fifth Committee decisions are included in this document.

ACABQ reviewed the SG’s statement (A/C.5/58/28) on the PBI of draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.28 and found it did not meet rule 153 requirements (no breakdown provided). The SG indicated estimated requirements of $6,053,700 for the biennium 2004-2005 to maintain the Office of the Special Representative for Children and Armed Conflict at its current scope, but no single-year breakdown was provided. As of end-November 2003, the Office had $2,123,000 in extrabudgetary resources, expected to fund operations through July 2004. The SG proposed deferring consideration of regular budget funding to spring 2004 in the context of a forthcoming report mandated by GA resolution 57/190. ACABQ stated that whether to fund the Office from the regular budget is a policy decision for the General Assembly and did not recommend a specific amount; it advised that a breakdown of estimated requirements be provided to the Fifth Committee.

Fifth Committee decided that, if the General Assembly adopts draft resolution A/C.3/58/L.28 (as orally revised), any necessary appropriations would be considered no later than the first part of the resumed 58th session, in the context of a report by the Secretary-General pursuant to GA resolution 57/190, section V. No amounts or posts approved in this decision; consideration deferred.

title: Implementation of Agenda 21, the Programme for the Further Implementation of Agenda 21 and the outcomes of the World Summit on Sustainable Development : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/58/L.9 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Draft resolution A/C.2/58/L.9 would require an estimated $55,000 to support participation of Commission on Sustainable Development member States in their respective regional meetings in each implementation cycle; however, this amount would be funded from the balance of resources previously allocated to the former ad hoc intersessional working groups of the Commission (i.e., no additional appropriations requested). The Secretary-General recalls A/C.5/58/10, which proposed using $361,400 of those previously devoted resources to establish two additional posts (P-5 and P-3) and consultants’ requirements to implement the Johannesburg Plan of Implementation, with the balance of $138,900 to be utilized for travel of representatives to attend the Intergovernmental Preparatory Meeting in New York. Paragraph 8 of the draft resolution has no budgetary effect because the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources for Development was abolished (no resources included for 2004-2005). The statement underscores GA resolution 45/248 B (Fifth Committee prerogative) and concludes that adoption of A/C.2/58/L.9 entails no additional requirements over the proposed programme budget for the biennium 2004-2005.

title: Further implementation of the Programme of Action for the Sustainable Development of Small Island Developing States : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/58/L.30 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: No additional requirements to the regular budget for 2004-2005. Conference servicing for the preparatory process and the International Meeting is already included in the proposed programme budget (Section 2, GA affairs and conference services). A provision of $211,300 under Section 28 (Public information) for promotional activities (brochures, press kits, posters, post-conference publications, website, coverage of interregional meeting) is already included in the proposed programme budget. The Secretary-General for the International Meeting is appointed from within the Secretariat; his travel will be met within Section 10, except travel to the International Meeting and its planning process, which will be borne by the host country. If two days of informal consultations (28–29 Aug 2004) are held in Mauritius, subsistence allowances for UN staff servicing the meeting will be borne by the host Government. Additional travel support requirements to facilitate participation, to be funded from voluntary contributions: $617,600 for representatives of small island developing States (covering travel and subsistence per ECOSOC decision 2003/283); $385,400 for other developing countries (travel only, no subsistence); total $1,003,000. Trust fund balance at the time stands at $80,000, with expectation of further contributions. Staffing context for the SIDS Unit (informational): a qualified expert from a SIDS identified to fill the Chief position; one P-2 post deployed to strengthen the Unit; two extrabudgetary posts funded from trust funds working on the SIDS Information Network.
stages.budget_section: Section 10, Least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States

title: Third United Nations Conference on the Least Developed Countries : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/58/L.68 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: SG states that implementation of para. 8 of draft A/C.2/58/L.68 would be funded from extrabudgetary resources and would not require use of any regular budget funds under GA res. 1798 (XVII) framework. Annual ECOSOC assessment participation support implied, but no regular budget provision.

title: The illicit trade in small arms and light weapons in all its aspects : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/58/L.1/Rev.1 : report of the 5th Committee : General Assembly, 58th session

stages.notes: Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.1/58/L.1/Rev.1, a provision of $1,375,600 would be required for the biennium 2004-2005, charged to the contingency fund. Breakdown by section: Section 2 (General Assembly affairs and conference services): $1,004,600; Section 4 (Disarmament): $254,200; Section 29D (Office of Central Support Services): $69,700; Section 34 (Staff assessment): $47,100, to be fully offset by income section 1 (Income from staff assessment). Any unutilized portion to be credited back to the contingency fund. This document is a Fifth Committee report to plenary; it does not record the final GA approval.

title: Implementation of the Brussels Programme of Action for the Least Developed Countries for the Decade 2001-2010 : Programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/63/L.57 : statement  / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: 2009 request comprises $476,400 gross for temporary assistance (2 P-4 and 1 P-3 for 9 months each, plus 9 work months at GS/Other level) and $24,000 for staff travel, totaling $500,400 gross ($437,100 net). Temporary positions would continue for 17 months each in 2010-2011 with $899,900 gross, plus $84,400 for staff travel to inter-agency and regional preparatory meetings; these out-year requirements are to be considered in the 2010-2011 budget. Conference servicing, support services and public information costs cannot be estimated until dates/venue/duration are decided. Under the headquarters principle, any additional costs from a change of venue from New York are to be borne by the host country. Regional preparatory meetings (ECA and ESCAP) will be serviced within existing capacity. The $500,400 gross for 2009 would be a charge against the contingency fund and require an additional appropriation for 2008-2009. The resolution stresses remaining within the budget level proposed for 2010-2011.
stages.budget_section: Section 10, Least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States

title: The world financial and economic crisis and its consequences : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/63/L.22/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General indicates that implementation would generate additional resource requirements in the 2008-2009 biennium, but provides no specific amounts due to insufficient details on modalities. For the main United Nations Summit and the ECOSOC high-level meeting, no additional conference-servicing resources are expected; they would use existing entitlements within the regular work programme. Any extra conference-servicing needs for parallel meetings, round tables, panels, and civil society hearings/dialogues would be identified and costed once modalities are clarified after consultations. Substantive preparatory work requirements are not defined at this stage. A detailed PBI would be submitted later in the session once specific information becomes available.

title: Doha Declaration on Financing for Development : outcome document of the Follow-up International Conference on Financing for Development to Review the Implementation of the Monterrey Consensus : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/63/L.57 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General indicates insufficient detail to determine programme budget implications. No additional conference-servicing resources would be required if the conference is treated as part of the regular GA work programme using already planned entitlements. Any additional requirements for parallel meetings, panels, civil society hearings, etc., would be reviewed once modalities are defined. Substantive preparatory work requirements cannot be determined at this stage. Adoption could give rise to additional resource requirements in the current biennium; a detailed PBI will be submitted once specific information on the format and modalities is available.

ACABQ reviewed SG statement A/C.5/63/19 on PBI for draft resolution A/63/L.57. At this stage, insufficient details on the conference modalities to determine full budget implications. No additional conference services would be required if the conference is conducted as part of the General Assembly’s regular work programme using already planned and budgeted meeting and documentation resources. Any extra servicing for parallel meetings/round tables/panels/civil society events would need separate review once modalities are defined. ACABQ indicates the Fifth Committee may wish to inform the GA that additional resource requirements may be required, but a detailed statement will be submitted during the current session once specific information on the format and modalities becomes available.

Fifth Committee considered the SG statement (A/C.5/63/19) and ACABQ report (A/63/625) on draft resolution A/63/L.57. It decided to inform the General Assembly that, if the draft resolution is adopted, a detailed PBI would be submitted during the current session once specific information on the format and modalities of the conference on the world financial and economic crisis and its impact on development is available. No resource amounts or posts were approved or recommended at this stage.

title: Assessment of the ad hoc advisory groups of the Economic and Social Council on African countries emerging from conflict : programme budget implications of draft resolution E/2004/L.29/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 31 of the rules of procedure of the Economic and Social Council

stages.notes: SG states additional resources of $90,300 are required in the 2004-2005 biennium under section 9 for travel and related expenses: $41,600 for Ad Hoc Advisory Group on Guinea-Bissau participation in donor conference/round table (Nov 2004) and $48,700 for a four-day mission of the Ad Hoc Advisory Group on Burundi. Conference-servicing and substantive secretariat support costs are negligible and to be absorbed within existing resources. The $90,300 would be a charge against the contingency fund and require an increase in appropriations for 2004-2005. SG also notes ECOSOC’s consideration of resource allocation (para. 7 of the draft resolution) may contravene GA resolution 45/248 B, as budget allocation is under the Fifth Committee’s purview.

title: United Nations Regional Centre for Peace, Disarmament and Development in Latin America and the Caribbean : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/59/L.18 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Draft resolution requests support within existing resources. No additional requirements under the 2004-2005 programme budget. Programme activities of the Regional Centre financed from extrabudgetary resources. Existing provision covers one P-5 Director post at Lima under section 4; no new posts created. Reminder of Fifth Committee/ACABQ roles per GA resolution 45/248 B.

title: United Nations regional centres for peace and disarmament : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/59/L.9 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Draft resolution requests support to regional centres within existing resources. No additional requirements under the 2004-2005 programme budget. Activities fall under subprogramme 5, Regional disarmament, of Section 4. Existing provision covers three P-5 Director posts for the regional centres; programmes of activities financed from extrabudgetary resources. Cites GA resolution 45/248 B regarding Fifth Committee/ACABQ roles.

title: United Nations Regional Centre for Peace and Disarmament in Asia and the Pacific : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.1/59/L.20 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General indicates no additional requirements under the 2004-2005 programme budget. The existing P-5 post for the Director of the Regional Centre is already provided under Section 4. Programme activities and the physical operation of the Centre from Kathmandu are to be financed from extrabudgetary resources. No programme modifications required; activities fall under programme 2, subprogramme 5 (Regional disarmament). This is a First Committee SG statement on draft resolution A/C.1/59/L.20.

title: International Convention on the Protection of the Rights of All Migrant Workers and Members of Their Families : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/59/L.31 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: The Secretary-General indicates that the change from a single three-week session in 2005 to two one-week sessions (July and October) for the Committee on the Protection of the Rights of All Migrant Workers and Members of Their Families results in: (a) additional travel requirements of $12,200 to be absorbed within Section 24 resources; (b) estimated savings of $238,100 under Section 2 (General Assembly affairs and conference services) and $3,200 under Section 29E (Administration, Geneva). Actual savings to be reported in the second performance report for 2004-2005. Information only: full cost of the activities envisaged is $891,300 for the biennium 2004-2005, and the previously approved 2005 three-week session cost was $1,120,400 (net reduction of $229,100). Sessions will be held at UNOG; activities relate to programme 19 (Human rights). The narrative of subprogramme 2 under Section 24 would be modified to reflect 'Substantive servicing of meetings: plenary meetings (50)'. No posts are created.

SG states that adoption would not give rise to additional requirements under the 2004-2005 programme budget. Arrangement changes 2005 meetings of the Committee on Migrant Workers to two one-week sessions (Geneva) instead of one three-week session. Full cost of activities under the new arrangement in the 2004-2005 biennium is $891,300, compared to $1,120,400 for the originally approved three-week session. Changes result in: (a) additional travel requirements of $12,200 for Committee members, to be absorbed within section 24; (b) estimated savings of $238,100 under section 2 (General Assembly affairs and conference services) and $3,200 under section 29E (Administration, Geneva). Actual savings to be reported in the second performance report for 2004-2005. No posts are created.

Fifth Committee decided that adoption of draft resolution A/C.3/59/L.31, as orally amended, would not give rise to additional requirements under the programme budget for 2004-2005. It further noted that actual savings resulting from reduced conference servicing requirements would be reported in the second performance report for the biennium 2004-2005. No amounts or posts are specified in this document. References: SG statement A/C.5/59/21 and ACABQ report A/59/597.

title: Committee of Experts on International Cooperation in Tax Matters : programme budget implications of draft resolution E/2004/L.60 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 31 of the rules of procedure of the Economic and Social Council

stages.notes: Per session requirements estimated at $294,900, comprising: $129,000 conference servicing; $156,900 travel and per diem of Committee members; $9,000 staff travel to Geneva. No additional requirements for the 2004-2005 biennium; the 2005 session to be met from existing provisions. From 2006-2007 onward, resources required for two sessions per biennium total $589,800 (i.e., $294,900 per year). Meetings to be held in Geneva (exception to GA res. 40/243 headquarters principle). Secretariat intends to continue current arrangements and meet any additional servicing needs within available resources; amounts to be included in proposed programme budgets for 2006-2007 and subsequent biennia. Related sections: 2 (GA affairs and conference services), 9 (Economic and social affairs), and 28E/29D (Administration Geneva/OCSS depending on biennium).

title: Rights of the child : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/59/L.29/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General’s statement (A/C.5/59/22) for draft resolution A/C.3/59/L.29/Rev.1 requests additional resources in 2005 totaling $1,622,200 gross, including $120,000 staff assessment (offset). Net new money excluding staff assessment is $1,502,200. Breakdown for 2005: Section 1: $800,000 (to maintain the Office of the SRSG for Children and Armed Conflict in 2005); Section 2: $484,900 (conference services for Committee on the Rights of the Child two-chamber work); Section 24: $214,900 (general temporary assistance); Section 29E: $2,400 (sound technician); Section 34: $120,000 staff assessment (offset). An additional $3,000 for the Committee Chair’s oral report is to be funded from extrabudgetary resources (no regular budget impact). For 2006, $4,115,200 is indicated for the Committee on the Rights of the Child and will be included in the 2006-2007 proposed programme budget. Requirements represent a charge to the contingency fund and require additional appropriations for 2004-2005. Funding of the SRSG Office beyond 2005 is contingent upon voluntary contributions. Note: paragraph 26 appears to contain a typographical error showing $1,622,200,000; the correct total is $1,622,200 as per table 3.

title: Comprehensive review on a strategy to eliminate future sexual exploitation and sexual abuse in United Nations peacekeeping operations : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.4/59/L.20 : report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

stages.notes: ACABQ recommends no additional appropriation at this stage for draft resolution A/C.4/59/L.20. Additional human resources should be provided via general temporary assistance (GTA), pending a comprehensive policy and fully justified proposals. Most requirements are to be accommodated within existing mission/support account budgets for 2005/06, with any additional needs considered in the context of those budgets. Indicative amounts to be considered in 2005/06 budgets total up to $3,144,400, broken down as: Support account up to $206,600; UNAMSIL up to $379,600; MONUC up to $1,353,000; UNMIS up to $1,205,200. ACABQ does not reduce the Secretary-General’s estimated requirements but urges avoidance of duplication, development of a coherent policy, and clarity on OIOS investigative roles.
stages.budget_section: Peacekeeping support account and missions (UNAMSIL, MONUC, UNMIS)

stages.notes: Scope: Costs to implement the Special Committee on Peacekeeping Operations recommendations on sexual exploitation and abuse (A/59/19/Add.1; endorsing A/59/710) across HQ and missions for the period 1 July 2005–30 June 2006. Total first-year requirement: $8,091,200; $4,545,500 already included in proposed 2005/06 support account and mission budgets; balance $3,545,700. HQ (support account A/59/730): Personnel Conduct Unit of 8 posts plus HIV/AIDS Adviser (P-4) and an additional Legal Officer (P-3). HQ detailed first-year requirements: Posts $873,200 (of which $795,700 proposed in budget; $77,500 additional for P-3 Legal Officer), GTA $191,600 (P-4 Legal Adviser: $95,800 included + $95,800 additional), Official travel $40,700 ($5,700 included for workshop; $35,000 for database implementation to be absorbed), Facilities & infrastructure $308,300 ($280,100 included; $28,200 additional), Communications $12,500 ($10,800 included; $1,700 additional), IT $34,000 ($30,600 included; $3,400 additional). HQ total in section III.B table: $1,460,300 (of which $1,218,700 proposed; $241,600 balance). HQ ‘Action to be taken’ requests GA approval of: (a) $1,122,900 for PCU (8 posts) + HIV/AIDS Adviser for 12 months (as proposed in support account A/59/730), (b) GTA $95,800 (6 months) P-4 Legal Officer (included), (c) additional GTA $95,800 (6 months) P-4 Legal Officer, (d) establishing a Legal Officer (P-3) post with $110,800 (support account 2005/06). Missions (Personnel Conduct Units): MONUC: 14 posts required; 11 proposed in budget; requirement $1,588,600 total ($1,553,600 civilian personnel + $35,000 travel), proposed $1,353,000; balance $235,600; mission will attempt to absorb $35,000 travel and 3 posts. UNOCI: 7 posts required; 1 proposed (P-4); $806,900 requirement; $192,100 proposed; $614,800 balance. ONUB: 7 posts required; 1 proposed (P-5); $850,700 requirement; $219,000 proposed; $631,700 balance. UNMIL: 7 posts required; 1 proposed (P-5); $916,100 requirement; $181,600 proposed; $734,500 balance. MINUSTAH: 7 posts required; 1 proposed (P-4); $883,900 requirement; $175,900 proposed; $708,000 balance. UNAMSIL: 3 posts required; none proposed (liquidation budget); $379,600 requirement; $379,600 balance. UNMIS: 10 posts required; all 10 proposed; $1,205,200 requirement; $1,205,200 proposed; $0 balance. title: 2005 World Summit Outcome : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/60/L.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General statement on programme budget implications of draft resolution A/60/L.1 (2005 World Summit Outcome). No detailed first-year request is provided. A preliminary total estimate of approximately $80 million is cited for requirements arising from the draft resolution, spanning potential late-2004/2005 needs, the 2006-2007 programme budget, and limited needs under the support account for peacekeeping operations (notably for 1 Jan–30 Jun 2006). A detailed statement with specific amounts and breakdowns was to be submitted later to the Fifth Committee alongside ACABQ review. The statement notes that the major cost of $50 billion for development financing falls outside the UN assessed budget and will be undertaken by the broader development community and extrabudgetary entities. Anticipated assessed-budget impacts include establishment/strengthening of: a Mediation Support Office and DPA regional mediation capacity; standing police capacity in DPKO (support account); servicing the Peacebuilding Commission and a small peacebuilding support office; strengthened UNODC capacity; strengthened OHCHR; rule of law and democracy support; ECOSOC high-level Development Cooperation Forum and annual ministerial reviews; significant strengthening of OIOS audit and investigations; and a DPA focal point for engagement with regional organizations. These measures imply ongoing (recurring) resource needs, but no specific post counts, grades, or year-by-year amounts are provided in this document.

title: The Peacebuilding Commission : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/60/L.40 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General’s statement (A/C.5/60/22) presents additional requirements for the biennium 2006-2007 to establish and service the Peacebuilding Commission. Breakdown (biennial): Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) $2,181,600 for conference-servicing; Section 3 (Political affairs) $4,175,200 for the peacebuilding support office; Section 35 (Staff assessment) $645,600, fully offset by income section 1. Total cited by SG including staff assessment = $7,002,400. Amount recorded above ($6,356,800) excludes staff assessment as it is offset. First-year split is not provided; figures are biennial totals in the 2006-2007 biennial budget context. Further detail referenced to A/60/537 (paras. 19, 40-43).

ACABQ reviewed the SG’s statement (A/C.5/60/22) indicating PBIs identical to revised estimates in A/60/537, which proposed establishing a Peacebuilding Support Office with 21 posts: 1 ASG, 1 D-2, 1 D-1, 3 P-5, 5 P-4, 5 P-3, 1 GS (Principal) and 4 GS (Other). ACABQ recommends no additional appropriation under Section 3 at the present stage and that the activity be staffed from within existing resources (redeployment/absorption). It is unconvinced by the SG’s justification and requests a revised proposal consistent with the Assembly’s intent. ACABQ notes Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) requirements are fluid (e.g., Human Rights Council), and suggests any additional resources for the Peacebuilding Commission be reported in the performance report. The SG has taken interim steps using currently available posts to set up a start-up element of the office. No posts are approved/created at this stage.

Fifth Committee draft decision (A/C.5/60/L.11) on PBI for draft resolution A/60/L.40 states that adoption would not give rise to any additional requirements under the 2006-2007 proposed programme budget at this stage. Any additional resources needed for conference servicing of the Peacebuilding Commission should be reported in the first performance report. Peacebuilding support office to be staffed from within existing resources; therefore no additional appropriation under Section 3 (Political affairs) at this stage. The Secretary-General is requested to report back pursuant to paragraph 7 of ACABQ report A/60/7/Add.25.

Fifth Committee decided that adoption of draft resolution A/60/L.40 (Peacebuilding Commission) would not give rise to additional requirements under the 2006-2007 budget at this stage. Any additional resources for conference-servicing of the Peacebuilding Commission should be reported in the first performance report. Peacebuilding Support Office to be staffed from within existing resources; thus no additional appropriation under Section 3 at this stage. GA requested the Secretary-General to report back pursuant to paragraph 7 of ACABQ report A/60/7/Add.25.

stages.notes: This is a Secretary-General statement to the Third Committee (A/C.3/63/L.77) providing programme budget implications for draft resolution A/C.3/63/L.57/Rev.1 focused on Human Rights Council decision 9/103. Net additional resources requested for the biennium 2008-2009 total $5,898,300, derived from $6,130,200 gross less $231,900 staff assessment (offset). Breakdown of gross additional requirements by section: Section 2: $4,944,200; Section 23: $392,700 (provided via general temporary assistance, no new posts in 2008-2009); Section 27: $221,900 (includes establishment of one P-3 Radio and Television Producer post); Section 28E: $339,500 (includes $225,600 one-time accommodation and equipment and $113,900 recurring annual common support costs). Regional consultations with five organizations entail $442,600 ($142,600 travel/DSA/terminal; $300,000 cost-sharing of conference servicing) and are contingent on voluntary contributions (not regular budget). 2020 and 2021 additional non-recurrent requirements to be considered in proposed programme budgets: total $763,800 (2020) and $325,100 (2021). Breakdown: Section 2 (conference servicing) $345,900 in 2020 (simultaneous interpretation $199,800; pre-session documentation $52,200; in-session $26,400; post-session $67,500) and $115,900 in 2021 (interpretation $84,000; post-session $31,900); Section 29E Administration, Geneva $7,000 in 2020 for sound technician/recording; Section 4 (non-conference servicing) $410,900 in 2020 (consultant $32,100; ad hoc expert groups $378,800) and $209,200 in 2021 (consultant $19,800; ad hoc expert groups $189,400). The requested 2019 appropriation under Section 4 would be charged to the contingency fund. Meetings schedule: GGE sessions — 5 days in 2019 (NY), two 5-day sessions in 2020 (Geneva), and 5 days in 2021 (NY); informal consultative meetings — two 2-day sessions (2019 and 2020, NY). All conference servicing/resource figures are non-recurrent.

2019 assessed-budget request is $206,700 under Section 4 (Disarmament) for consultants and ad hoc expert groups; to be charged against the contingency fund and requires an additional appropriation. 2019 conference-servicing costs totaling $221,000 under Section 2 (DGACM)—$107,800 for meetings and $113,200 for documentation—will be absorbed within existing resources (no new appropriation). Additional projected requirements to be considered in future programme budgets: $763,800 in 2020 and $325,100 in 2021 (across Sections 2, 4, and 29E). Regional consultations requested under OP4 entail $442,600 in 2018–2019 (travel/DSA/terminal for participants and cost-sharing of conference services) to be funded from voluntary contributions; they cannot be undertaken from existing assessed resources. Section 29E (Administration, Geneva) has $7,000 in 2020 for sound technician/recording services related to Geneva sessions.

title: Report of the International Law Commission on the work of its 70th session : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.6/73/L.22 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Programme 6 (Legal affairs) – International Law Commission. Additional non-recurrent resources required to extend the 2019 session to 11 weeks at UNOG (seven additional days). Breakdown: $114,900 for members’ travel and $13,200 for staff travel. Charge against the contingency fund; requires an additional appropriation for the biennium 2018–2019. Existing baseline for ILC in section 8 is $3,008,100 (context only).

Secretary-General states that, if the GA decides to hold an 11-week session of the International Law Commission in 2019 (instead of the 10 weeks budgeted), additional non-recurrent resources of $128,100 are required under Section 8, Legal affairs, for the biennium 2018–2019. This would be a charge against the contingency fund and would require an additional appropriation. Breakdown: $114,900 for travel expenses for members (seven additional days) and $13,200 for travel expenses for staff (seven additional days). The existing approved budget for the ILC under Section 8 for 2018–2019 totals $3,008,100, based on an 11-week session in 2018 and a 10-week session in 2019 with assumed average attendance of 82% of membership. The SG indicates the requirement cannot be absorbed within Section 8.

title: Situation of human rights in the Autonomous Republic of Crimea and the city of Sevastopol, Ukraine : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/73/L.48 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: One-time additional resources for 2019 consist of: Other staff costs (GTA) at P-3 level for six months ($74,500) to prepare the report and develop options/recommendations, and travel for the head of the Human Rights Monitoring Mission in Ukraine to New York to present the report ($4,700). An additional documentation workload of one pre-session document (8,500 words) in six official languages in 2019 under Section 2 ($26,000) would be absorbed within existing resources. No absorption possible under Section 24; $79,200 would require an additional appropriation and be a charge against the contingency fund.

One-time additional requirements of $79,200 in 2019 under section 24 for GTA (P-3, six months) and travel ($4,700) for the head of the human rights monitoring mission in Ukraine to present the report. Documentation costs of $26,000 (one pre-session document of 8,500 words in six languages) will be absorbed within section 2 (DGACM) and are not requested as new appropriations. The $79,200 would require an additional appropriation for 2018–2019 and represent a charge against the contingency fund.

Fifth Committee decided to inform the General Assembly that, should it adopt draft resolution A/C.3/73/L.48, no additional resources would be required. The Committee considered the SG statement A/C.5/73/14 and ACABQ report A/73/650.

title: A global call for concrete action for the total elimination of racism, racial discrimination, xenophobia and related intolerance and the comprehensive implementation of and follow-up to the Durban Declaration and Programme of Action : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.3/73/L.52/Rev.1 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: Secretary-General requests an additional appropriation of $563,000 for 2019 (net of $53,600 existing provisions under GA res. 69/16). Mix of recurrent ($366,800) and non-recurrent ($196,200) costs. Charge against the contingency fund; requires GA approval. Establishes 1 P-4 Human Rights Officer post effective 1 Jan 2019 under section 24. Activities include a one-day 2019 consultation meeting and annual three-day permanent forum meetings alternating Geneva/New York, plus website redesign and maintenance. Document contains a typographical reference to section 21; table and context confirm section 24, Human rights.

Secretary-General requests an additional appropriation of $447,500 for 2019, chargeable to the contingency fund, to implement draft resolution A/C.3/73/L.52/Rev.1. Breakdown of additional requirements: $423,700 under Section 24 (Human rights), $21,000 under Section 28 (Public information) for one-time website redesign, and $2,800 under Section 29F (Administration, Geneva). Section 2 (General Assembly and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) 2019 needs of $73,900 are to be absorbed within the approved appropriation; existing provisions total $95,200 ($52,600 in Section 2; $35,900 in Section 24; $6,700 in Section 28). Activities include a 2019 one-day consultation in Geneva and annual three-day meetings of the permanent forum alternating between Geneva and New York. Conference-servicing recurrent requirements: $85,600 in odd-numbered years and $79,700 in even-numbered years. Geneva sound/recording services: $2,100 in odd years and $700 one-time in 2019 for the consultation. Staff travel: $11,300 every even-numbered year starting 2020. Website maintenance: $6,700 annually (provision already exists). GA is requested to approve establishment of one P-4 Human Rights Officer post effective 1 January 2019 to serve as secretary of the permanent forum. Staff assessment (Section 36) $11,000 is fully offset by income and not counted as new money.

title: Report of the United Nations Environment Assembly of the United Nations Environment Programme : programme budget implications of draft resolution A/C.2/73/L.49 : statement / submitted by the Secretary-General in accordance with rule 153 of the rules of procedure of the General Assembly

stages.notes: This SG statement (A/C.2/73/L.68) presents PBI for draft resolution A/C.2/73/L.49. Total biennium (2018–2019) requirements are $2,006,100: $1,595,700 under Section 2 (GA and ECOSOC affairs and conference management) and $410,400 under Section 14 (Environment). Existing provisions in the 2018–2019 budget total $1,137,100 ($1,050,100 under Section 2 and $87,000 under Section 14). Under Section 2, additional conference-servicing needs of $545,600 would be absorbed within the approved appropriation. Under Section 14, additional resources of $323,400 are required via an additional appropriation for 2018–2019 and would be a charge against the contingency fund (resolutions 41/213 and 42/211). Costs under Section 14 include recurrent overtime (security, medical, substantive servicing), ICT and equipment rental, and hospitality tied to UNEA, open-ended CPR meetings, and annual CPR Subcommittee meetings. Future governance cycles (even/odd years from 2020 onward) would have requirements included in proposed budgets for those years.
